title: Financial Accounting
description: This course emphasizes the provision of relevant and reliable information used in making financial and business decisions. Areas of emphasis include the basic concepts and principles of financial accounting; the accounting cycle from the analysis of business transactions and their systematic recording through the preparation of the basic financial statements - balance sheet, income statement and statement of cash flows; the basic theory and practice for recognition, measurement and classification of assets, liabilities, equity,revenues and expenses; and the interpretation of accounting data, income and cash flow analysis. Alternative forms of business organization, internal control and interrelationships of accounting data and the ethical implications of accounting are also covered. Co-requisites: MAT 097 or Mathematics Placement Exam results.

title: Taxes II
description: A study of tax compliance for corporations, partnerships and fiduciaries, as well as for not-for-profit organizations, including tax accounting for formation, reorganization, distribution and liquidation of organizations. The study of transfer taxes and the preparation of the related federal tax forms are included. Students are required to identify and resolve tax issues through the utilization of various sources, including applicable tax law and regulations, administrative and judicial opinions, interpretative texts and published procedures. In addition to tax research, the practical compliance aspects of tax return preparation are covered. While the focus is on federal taxes, state tax implications are also studied, including multi-state tax situations. Prerequisites: ACC 106 , ACC 321 .

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content, having an appropriate, complementary role within the student's academic program. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Intermediate Accounting I
description: A rigorous study of current accounting theory and practice, this course builds on the fundamental concepts covered in the introductory financial and managerial accounting courses, and develops a more professional level of accounting knowledge and analysis. Coverage includes the conceptual framework of financial accounting, the development of accounting standards, the accounting process, financial statements and the time value of money. Included in this study is an analysis and review of cash and receivables, inventories and cost of goods sold, fixed assets and depreciation, intangibles and amortization, and revenue recognition. The review of each group of accounts includes conceptual considerations, technical accounting procedures, and the necessary and appropriate disclosures within the body of the financial statements and the related accounting schedules and notes. Prerequisite: ACC 106 .

title: Intermediate Accounting II
description: This course is a continuation of Intermediate  Accounting I. Includes an analysis and review of  investments, current and long-term liabilities,  contingencies and equity. The review of each  category includes conceptual considerations,  technical accounting procedures and the necessary  and appropriate disclosures within the body of  the financial statements and the related  accounting schedules and notes. Topics include  dilutive securities and basic and fully diluted  earnings per share, accounting for leases, income  taxes, executive compensation, retirement and  postretirement benefit plans and accounting  changes. The course concludes with a  comprehensive review of financial statement  analysis and interpretation and full disclosure  in financial reporting. Prerequisite: ACC 205 .

title: Senior Thesis/Project
description: In conjunction with a faculty advisor, the student will design and implement a major research project. Prerequisite: ACC 206 and senior standing with permission of advisor.

title: Cost Accounting
description: Cost accounting furnishes management with the necessary accounting tools for planning and controlling activities and for making many business decisions. Specifically, the collection, presentation and analysis of cost data helps management as it deals with the areas of cost-volume-profit analysis, job-order and process cost systems, standard, differential, and activity based cost systems, variance analysis, and static and flexible budgeting. By focusing on basic concepts, analyses, uses and procedures instead of procedures alone, the course shows cost accounting as a tool for business strategy and implementation. The role of the accountant as both decision-maker and data provider is considered. Prerequisite: ACC 106 .

title: Animal Studies: Canines
description: An exploration of canines within the framework of evolution, normal and abnormal behavior,  domestication, and comparative training  techniques.  Emphasis will be placed on the  animal-human bond and cooperative service.  Additional topics include:  ethical treatment in  service and current issues and topics.  Prerequisites: ANS-101 and BIO-101 or BIO-110 .

title: Introduction to Animal Studies
description: Through discussion, survey and observation,  students will, within the culture of animal-human interactions, develop their ability to think   critically and ethically; review related   international, national and state laws and the  associated regulatory agencies; develop impartial   observation skills; and define employment-related  skill sets. ETH

title: Animal Studies: Felines & Exotics
description: An exploration of felines and exotic animals within the framework of evolution, normal and abnormal behavior, domestication, laboratory use, and comparative training techniques.  Emphasis will be placed on the animal-human bond and cooperative service.  Additional topics include:  ethical treatment and current issues and topics.  Prerequisites: ANS-101 and BIO-101 or BIO-110 .

title: Animal Studies: Equines
description: An exploration of the equine within the framework  of evolution, normal and abnormal behavior,  domestication, and comparative training  techniques.  Emphasis will be placed on the  animal-human bond and cooperative service.  Additional topics include:  ethical treatment in  service and current issues and topics.  Prerequisites: ANS-101 and BIO-101 or BIO-110 .

title: Animal Studies: Wildlife
description: An exploration of wildlife within the framework of normal and abnormal behaviors relative to evolving predator/prey roles, civilization's effects on traditional food sources, increasing human contact (encroachment, zoos and tourism), and the training of wildlife for entertainment.  Emphasis will be placed on the animal-human interaction outcomes.  Additional topics include:  ethical conservancy and current issues and topics.  Prerequisite: ANS-101 and BIO-101 or BIO-110 .

title: Animal Studies: Farm Animals
description: An exploration of farm animals within the  framework of normal and abnormal behaviors  relative to family structure (herd, flock, drove, etc.), production (meat, milk, wool, eggs, etc.), housing and transport.  Emphasis will be placed on  stewardship and ethical treatment.  Current issues and topics will be discussed. Prerequisite: ANS-101 and BIO-101 or BIO-110 .

title: Topics in Animal Studies
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty that are not covered in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Managerial Accounting
description: Managers in all organizations are confronted daily with the need to make decisions and solve problems. They need information for making informed judgments, solving problems and managerial control. Information is a valuable resource to an organization, and the management accounting system is the primary source for much of the information managers need and receive. Areas of emphasis include cost concepts, cost management and behavior, standard costing and variance analysis; cost-volume-profit analysis; budgetary controls; and responsibility accounting. Understanding the use of financial and managerial accounting for information for pricing, product costing, and operational, investment, and capital budgeting decisions, as well as the ethical implications of managerial accounting decisions are also covered. Prerequisite: ACC 105 . Co-requisite: MAT 115 .

title: Animal Studies Advanced Seminar
description: Through research, experiential learning,  observations, internship and interviews,students  will identify specific areas of interest that  would prepare them for employment or graduate  school. Prerequisite: PSY 110 and three ANS courses at the 200 level.

title: Taxes I
description: This course introduces the fundamentals of taxation, the types of taxes, the structure of the tax system, and how taxes are applied to various entities. It is an in-depth study of the income taxation of individuals and provisions applicable to all tax return filers. Topics include gross income, exclusions, deductions, losses, adjustments, credits, property transactions, capital gains and losses, as well as business expenses, depreciation, cost recovery, amortization, and depletion, and the determination of the regular tax and the alternative minimum tax. Tax research and the practical compliance aspects of individual tax return prepartion are covered. While the focus is on federal taxes, state tax implications are also studied, including multi-state tax situations. Prerequisite: ACC 105 .

title: Senior Thesis
description: The culminating course for the animal studies  major, the senior thesis will be a project or  area of study, defined through coursework in the 200-level courses and the advanced seminar that  culminates in the preparation of a thesis or oral  presentation. Prerequisite: ANS 380 .

title: Accounting Information Systems
description: The course helps students understand concepts of accounting information systems so they can effectively use software and system processes to affect and evaluate systems of internal control, know how accounting information systems gather and transform data into useful information and understand the interrelationships among systems. Using flowcharting techniques, students appreciate how transactions affect an organization and recognize when management or ethical issues need to be addressed. Audit procedures and risks in a computer environment are also covered. Prerequisite: ACC 106 .

title: American Sign Language I
description: American Sign Language I (ASL) is an introductory course that provides students an opportunity to learn basic ASL vocabulary and grammatical structures through visual memory and discrimination skills as well as gestural expressive skills. Students will also be introduced to the American Deaf Culture and Community.

title: American Sign Language II
description: American Sign Language II (ASL) is a continuation course that provides students an opportunity to further develop intermediate ASL vocabulary and grammatical structures through increased communication skills. Students will experience  skills through visual memory and discrimination as well as expressive and receptive fingerspelling.

title: Auditing and Assurance Services
description: This course is an analysis and appraisal of audit theory and practice, focusing on issues relevant to the public accounting profession and to internal auditors and managers in private and governmental organizations. It introduces students to the role of auditors and the audit standards, and emphasizes the importance of the concept of risk. Topics covered include auditing principles and procedures involving staff organization, professional ethics, legal responsibility of the accountant, the various forms of audit reports, internal control, fraud detection and prevention procedures, audit programs, working papers, original record examination and the nature of evidential matter, and an introduction to audit related statistical applications. The content, meaning and impact of the Sarbanes/Oxley Act are covered. Students gain an understanding of audit risk, and learn the fundamentals of auditing from the planning to obtaining and documenting sufficient evidence for expression of an audit opinion. They gain an understanding of the attest function and other assurance services and their applicability to various entities, operations audits, compliance audits and the requirements for an integrated audit. Prerequisites: ACC 206 , ACC 220 .

title: Leadership in Sport
description: Whether pursuing a career in coaching or as a  manager in sport, this course will introduce  students to the basic principles and foundations of leadership.  Case studies will be utilized to illustrate fundamental aspects of leadership, such  as creating a vision and overcoming obstacles.   Students will learn leadership theory, apply leadership concepts, and participate in practical strategies for becoming better leaders/coaches.

title: Med/Scientific Terminology-Greek/Latin
description: Introduction to the elements of Greek and Latin  terminology used in the medical and scientific  fields. Students will become familiar with the meaning of new terms and learn how to  analyze an unfamiliar word in order to discover  its meaning without using a medical dictionary. FWC

title: Coaching Internship
description: Each student, in consultation with the physical  education program director, will arrange a  coaching internship. Options include assisting as coach at a local public school or YMCA, coaching  at a summer sports camp, organizing and running a competitive community program, or teaching at a private sports school. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Classical Mythology
description: Study of primarily Greek and Roman myths and their  lasting influence upon modern culture. An  introduction to the major figures and cycles of stories as seen in ancient  literature and art, and their role in human  society.  FWC, LIT

title: Women in Antiquity
description: The role and status of women in ancient cultures (primarily those of the Graeco-Roman world, including Egypt, northern Africa, and the Middle East) from historical and archaeological sources as well as from myth, literature and art. Topics may include: comparisons with contemporary society, the social construction of gender, women and religion, and perceptions of female and male bodies.  Prerequisite: Foundations course in English. FWC, WS, WI

title: Greek Archaeology and Art
description: Introduction to methods of archaeology through  study of the artifacts and sites of the Greek  world, from prehistory to the Roman period. FWC, ART

title: Roman Archaeology and Art
description: Study of the origins and development of the  material culture of the Roman Empire through  analysis of selected excavations and artifacts. FWC, ART

title: Coaching and Officiating
description: As part of the athletic coaching minor, this lab focuses on the principles of coaching sport skill development, training and officiating/judging a sport.  Students will select the sport of their choice and attempt to receive coaching and/or judging certifications applicable to their sport. Students will develop a seasonal coaching plan, observe competitions and study sport-specific strategies.

title: Sexual Politics in Classical Mythology
description: Critical reading of episodes from Greek and Roman mythology that include elements of forced marriage and sexual violence. Readings of secondary literature and discussion will ask students to evaluate these myths in light of their legacy of misogyny and current developments in sexual political thought and activism. Prerequisite: Foundations course in English and any 200 level course. FWC, WS, WI

title: Topics in Classical Civilization
description: Study of a particular aspect of the civilizations  of Greece and Rome using the approaches of  various disciplines.

title: Greek Archaeology and Art
description: Introduction to methods of archaeology through study of the artifacts and sites of the Greek world, from prehistory to the Roman period. Prerequisite: Any 200 level course in AWS, FA, or HIS. FWC, ART

title: Topics in Athletic Coaching
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum and to address student and faculty interests.

title: Topics in Athletic Coaching
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum and to address student and faculty interests.

title: Roman Archaeology and Art
description: Study of the origins and development of the material culture of the Roman Empire through analysis of selected excavations and artifacts. Prerequisite: Any 200 level course in AWS, HIS, or FA. FWC, ART

title: The Olympics
description: An investigation of aspects of the Olympic games, both ancient and modern. Topics will include mythology, art and archaeology of Olympia, sports in the ancient world, ancient Greek religion, the Olympic games in ancient and modern international affairs, the history of the Olympic games, and the games in modern culture.   Prerequisite: Foundations course in English. FWC

title: Sexual Politics in Classical Mythology
description: Critical reading of episodes from Greek and Roman mythology that include elements of forced marriage and sexual violence. Readings of secondary literature and discussion will ask students to evaluate these myths in light of their legacy of misogyny and current developments in sexual political thought and activism.            Permission of program director required.

title: General Biology I Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-101 General Biology I.  Lab fee required.

title: General Biology I
description: Introduction to cell anatomy and organismal  biology. The kingdoms of life are surveyed,  emphasizing distinguishing features and  lifestyles. In-depth study of major organ systems  of multicellular animals. Emphasis on principles  underlying living organisms, problems they face  and solutions that have evolved. Designed as the  first of two semesters of a general survey. Three  hours lecture; three hours lab. NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Practical Plant Biology
description: Students will gain a basic understanding of the fundamental principles of botany and plant science. Topics include the study of plant form, function, and reproduction, and an overview of plant diversity including bryophytes, ferns, and seed plants. Emphasis is placed on human-plant relationships. Three hours lecture; three hours lab.  NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Contemporary Biology
description: A survey of relevant biological principles and  processes designed as an introduction to the life  sciences for the non-science major. Topics  include human biology (cellular and physiological  processes), evolution of biodiversity and  ecology. Three hours lecture; three hours lab.  NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Clinical Aspects of Microbiology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-111 Clinical Aspects  of Microbiology. Lab fee required.

title: Clinical Aspects of Microbiology
description: A general overview of microbiology with emphasis  on pathological mechanisms of infectious disease  and precautions necessary for the prevention and  treatment of infectious disease in clinical  settings. Students are exposed to the fundamental   principles of microbial structure, growth and  metabolism. Special attention is placed on  localization and identification of pathological  microorganisms. Topics of disinfection,  sterilization and immunity with respect to  clinical settings are emphasized. Three hours  lecture; two hours lab. Prerequisite: BIO 102 or BIO 110 . NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Topics in Classical Civilization
description: Study of a particular aspect of the civilizations  of Greece and Rome using the approaches of  various disciplines.

title: Contemporary Biology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-110 Contemporary Biology. Lab fee required.

title: Comparative Anatomy of Vertebrates
description: Major anatomical changes undergone by vertebrates  during their evolutionary history, from  invertebrate ancestors to present-day forms.  Emphasis on morphological adaptations and  evolutionary significance of anatomical features,  as well as on major paleontological events.  Laboratory study of representative types. Three  hours lecture; three hours lab. Prerequisite: BIO 102 . NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: General Biology II
description: Introduction to biochemistry, molecular cell  biology and cell energetics. Genetics covered in  relation to molecular mechanisms, population  biology and evolutionary theory. Survey of  botany, including anatomy and physiology of  plants. Introduction to ecological principles,  including ecosystem organization, interaction of  biotic and abiotic factors and the biosphere.  Designed as the second of two semesters of a general survey. Three hours lecture; three hours lab.  Prerequisite: BIO 101 . NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Practical Plant Biology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-108 Practical Plant Biology. Lab fee required.

title: General Biology II Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-102 General Biology  II. Lab fee required.

title: Comparative Anatomy of Vertebrates Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-205 Comparative Anatomy of Vertebrates. Lab fee required.

title: Vertebrate Physiology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-207 : Vertebrate Physiology. Lab fee required.

title: Invertebrate Zoology
description: Study of the major groups of invertebrates with  consideration of morphology, evolution,physiology  and ecology. Three hours lecture; three hours  lab. Prerequisite: BIO 102 . NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Vertebrate Physiology
description: Analysis of the physiological mechanisms of the  vertebrate organism from the cellular to the  organismic level. All major organ systems are  studied with emphasis on normal functions and  their controls. Laboratory study and  demonstrations of basic physiological parameters.  Three hours lecture; three hours lab.  Prerequisite: BIO 102 . NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Introductory Botany
description: Survey of the plant kingdom, emphasizing  classification, evolution, structure and  function. Emphasis on botanical, horticul- tural and economic aspects of plants used as  sources of food, fibers and pharmaceuticals.  Three hours lecture; three hours lab.  Prerequisite: BIO 102 . NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Introductory Botany Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-210 : Introductory Botany. Lab fee required.

title: Microbiology
description: Study of the morphology, physiology and genetics  of microorganisms with emphasis on bacteria and  bacteriological techniques. Three hours lecture; two hours lab. Prerequisites: BIO 102 , CHM 201 or CHM-104 .  NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Microbiology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-211 : Microbiology. Lab fee required.

title: Genetics
description: Mendelian genetics and principles of heredity,  introductory molecular genetics and evolutionary  genetics are covered. Three hours lecture; three  hours lab. Prerequisite: BIO 102 . NSL (4 sem.  hrs.)

title: Animal Behavior
description: An exploration of animal interactions within the  framework of evolution and natural selection.  Emphasis on social behavior and communication  between and among species. Other topics include  antipredator behavior, mating displays,  navigation, learning and memory. Laboratories  will include both observational and experimental  studies of behavior. Prerequisite: BIO  102 or BIO 110 . NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Genetics Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-208 : Genetics. Lab fee required.

title: Animal Behavior Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-220 : Animal Behavior. Lab fee required.

title: Marine Biology
description: A survey of the major marine habitats - the fauna, environmental characteristics and the processes involved in their formation. Emphasis placed on the evolutionary adaptations of organisms in these environments and the impact of humans on the ocean. A weekend field trip is a required component of the course. Field trip fee required. Prerequisite: BIO-102 . NS, ES

title: Invertebrate Zoology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-206 : Invertebrate Zoology. Lab fee required.

title: Conservation Biology
description: The science of preserving biodiversity and  sustaining the Earth, this course draws on many  disciplines and findings of theoretical biology  in an attempt to develop strategies for  preserving people, populations, species,  biological communities and ecosystems. Uses case  studies to survey the possibilities and problems  of applying conservation principles in the real  world. Students identify and find solutions to  conservation problems in the local area.  Prerequisite: BIO 102 or BIO 110 . NSL, ESL  (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Nutrition
description: A detailed study of nutrition and health, with  emphasis on metabolic pathways and relationships  between nutritional intake and normal and  pathological changes in the human organism.  Recommended for students interested in the health  professions. Prerequisite: BIO-102 . NS

title: Topics in Biology
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty that are not covered in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Developmental Biology
description: Descriptive and experimental analysis of the  development of multicellular eukaryotic organisms. A variety  of vertebrate and invertebrate animal systems are  studied. Early embryonic development is  emphasized; well-studied systems are chosen from  vertebrate embryos for analysis of later  organogenesis and cell differentiation.  Laboratories include study of vertebrate and  invertebrate embryonic development using both  living and preserved specimens. Three hours  lecture; three hours lab. Pre-requisite: BIO 102 .  NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Immunology
description: A lecture/discussion course for junior/ senior-level students interested in immunology. Explores the vertebrate immune system at the organismic, cellular and molecular levels. Topics include evolution of host defense mechanisms; structural and functional associations; generation of immune responses and effector mechanisms; and the immune system in health and disease. Interactive sessions reinforce current theory. A field trip to an immunology research laboratory informs students about current trends in the field. Three hours lecture. Prerequisites: BIO-102 and permission of instructor.

title: Histology
description: Microscopic structure of principal vertebrate  tissues correlated with their function and  interrelations with other tissues. Emphasis on  cellular details, ultrastructure and the  relationship between tissue structure and  function. Three hours lecture; three hours lab.  Prerequisite: BIO 102 ; BIO 205 advisable. NSL (4  sem. hrs.)

title: Molecular Cell Biology I
description: This course is designed to acquaint biology students with the foundational topics and theories needed to develop an in-depth understanding of the discipline of Biology. Biology of cells of higher eukaryotic organisms: Structure, Function, and Biosynthesis of Cellular Membranes and Organelles; Transport, Receptors, and Cell Signaling; the Cytoskeleton, the Extracellular Matrix, and Cell Movements. Prerequisites: BIO-102 and two  upper-level courses in biology or chemistry or permission of instructor. NS

title: Ecology
description: Introduction to the principles of ecology. Study  of factors governing distribution and abundance  of plants and animals in natural and disturbed  ecosystems. Three hours lecture; three hours lab.  Prerequisites: BIO 102 and MAT 103 . NSL, ESL, WI  (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Molecular Cell Biology II
description: An exploration of the principles of molecular genetics with emphasis on the control of cellular activities and the molecular structure, function, and regulation of genes. Topics include gene technology, genetic analysis in cell biology, regulation of the cell cycle, cell-to-cell signaling, gene control in development, and cancer. Designed as writing intensive, the course includes substantial writing assignments, including a major scientific research paper (multiple drafts), portfolio and essay exams. Prerequisite: BIO-208 . WI

title: Ecology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO 314 : Ecology.

title: Conservation Biology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-230 : Conservation Biology. Lab fee required.

title: Histology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-304 : Histology. Lab fee required.

title: Developmental Biology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-302 : Developmental Biology. Lab fee required.

title: Adv Molecular Bio/Biochm Lab Techniques
description: A laboratory course for junior-/senior-level  students interested in research methods in  molecular biology and biochemistry. Experimental  topics include enzyme kinetics; protein solation,  purification and analysis by electrophoresis and  immunofluorescence; cell culture techniques; and  advanced molecular genetics. Three hours lab.  (two semester hours.) Prerequisite: BIO 310 , CHM  310 or concurrent registration in BIO 310 or CHM  310. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Topics in Biology
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty that are not covered in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Senior Research Seminar I
description: The second course in the three-semester research  sequence for Biology and Chemistry majors,  focusing on research and oral presentations.  Seminar based on original research and/or recent  scientific literature following the proposal  developed in BIO-398 . Seminar presentations and a  draft research paper are required. Specific  research topics selected based on interests and  background of faculty and students. Prerequisite: BIO-398 .

title: Design & Methods of Scientific Research
description: Students learn to explore primary scientific literature, develop a research project and- depending upon student and faculty interest- learn advanced laboratory skills. The course, which is usually followed by BIO-400 and 402, results in a research proposal. Prerequisite: at least four courses at or above the 200 level in biology. Two semester hours.

title: Evolution
description: A study of the history of life on Earth and examination of principles of organic evolution in plants and animals. Current topics in evolutionary theory. Prerequisites: BIO-102 , and a quantitative skills course. NS, WI

title: Entrepreneurship/Small Business Managemt
description: Consideration of the problems of start-up,  survival, profit and growth of new or ongoing  venture. Examines small-business operations  including business plans, finance, legal aspects,  government regulation, record-keeping, marketing  and human resources issues. Prerequisite: BUS-124 .

title: Business Communications
description: Students will study the appropriate use of the various communications modalities in a business environment and demonstratetheir effective utilization of those modalities.  Traditional methods of written and verbal communication are covered as are the various vehicles provided by today's technology.  Prerequisite: BUS-124 and a Foundations in English course. WI

title: Marketing Management
description: Analytical systems approach to marketing  decision-making. Examines consumer behavior,  marketing research, pricing, product and  promotion  strategy, distribution channels and  why marketing activities develop in an industrial  economy. Analyzes social and ethical issues.  Prerequisite: BUS-124 or COM-233 .

title: Business Law
description: Study of contract, agency, debtor credit, trusts  and estates, property, corporation and  partnership law; Uniform Commercial Code; areas  of governmental regulation, including antitrust,  securities and employment law; work papers,  privileged communications and confidentiality.  Prerequisite: BUS-124 . CC

title: Business Environment and Public Policy
description: Examines the impact of public policy on business  through exploration and analysis of political,  social, regulatory and technological variables  that influence managerial decision-making in  domestic and global environments. Prerequisites: BUS-124 , ECO-101 , ECO-102

title: Topics in Business
description: A course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty that are not covered in the regular curriculum.

title: Business Leadership and Service
description: Covers the latest research in business  sustainability, leadership and project management  principles as students deliver on a  service-learning project in the local community.  Critical skills in teamwork, project management,  problem-solving and self-directed learning will  be gained as teams apply business principles  while working through challenging and real projects.  Prerequisites: BUS-124 and junior-level major in Accounting, Business or Economics.

title: Personal Finance
description: This course enables students to acquire an introduction to the concepts of personal finance. Students apply financial management concepts in the areas of basic financial planning, creating a budget, preparing personal income tax returns, managing investments, buying a house, the use of credit, the need for insurance and saving for retirement. This course fulfils the Quantitative Skills requirement.  Prerequisite:  MAT-096.

title: Senior Research Seminar II
description: The third course in the three-semester sequence  for Biology and Chemistry majors with the focus  on the analysis of research results, research  paper and a poster presentation. Completion of  this course will fulfill one writing-intensive  requirement. Prerequisite: BIO-400 . Two semester  hours. WI

title: Organizational Behavior
description: Multidisciplinary approach to the business  organization as a complex system. Concepts and  theories related to structure and function of  organizations; comprehensive study of strategy  formulation and organizational implementation;  analytical focus on internal operation and  structure and external posture from which  alternatives are developed and policies proposed.  Prerequisite: 124.

title: Introduction to Management
description: Study of management from a general perspective. Major functional areas of internal and external activities of an organization, the decision-making process and the computer's place in contemporary management.  Emphasis on formulation and implementation of policy in keeping with an organization's goals.

title: Human Resource Management
description: System elements required to recruit, select, train  and maintain a workforce in business. Emphasis on  management development, wage and salary  administration, and personnel planning.  Prerequisite: 124.

title: Labor and Employee Relations
description: Government regulations and industry practices guiding employer/employee relations, including  fair treatment, employment-at-will, employee  health, safety, workplace participation and  collective bargaining. Prerequisite: 124. CC

title: International Business
description: Introduction to international business  environments and how they affect international enterprises. Topics include international risk, market entry strategies, ownership policies,  functional areas and organizational planning and  control. Prerequisites: 124, ECO 101 , 102.

title: Corporate Finance Fundamentals
description: An introduction to the theoretical concepts of corporate finance. Focuses on management of  working capital, cost of capital, capital  budgeting and capital structure planning. Also provides an overview of money and capital markets. Prerequisites: ACC 105 , MAT 103 .

title: Investments
description: An in-depth analysis of investment planning of  individuals and institutions; analysis of  securities, functions and operation of securities markets. Overview of regulations and the role of  the government in the securities markets.  Prerequisite: 240.

title: Case Studies: Entrepreneurship
description: This course details the resources and strategies of entrepreneurship through case studies and original student research. We will explore organizations, the resources, management and strategies that make these organizations successes.  Prerequisites: ACC-106 and BUS-220 .

title: Entrepreneurial Innovation Mgt
description: Students learn about technology and business so that they can build a business that brings technology into the marketplace.  The course starts with cultivating innovation in its many forms and leads to developing a strategy to transform that innovation into a profitable strategy. Prerequisites: ACC-106 and BUS-220 .

title: Senior Thesis/Project
description: In conjunction with a faculty adviser, the student  will design and implement a major research project.

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content,  having an appropriate, complementary role within  the student's academic program. Approval of the  academic dean is required.

title: Healthcare Economics
description: An understanding of healthcare economics furnishes management with the necessary tools for planning and controlling activities and for making many business decisions. Specifically, the collection, presentation and analysis of cost data helps management as it deals with strategic, tactical and operating issues and decision-making. By focusing on concepts, analyses, uses and procedures instead of procedures alone, the course shows the strategic role of these tools for business strategy, implementation and performance evaluation. Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Operations Management for Sustainability
description: The study of the supply chain and innovations that improve efficiencies while benefiting the environment. Focus is on work-flow designs, productivity, quality, forecasting, process innovations, procurement management and other value-added strategies that lead to the promotion of a sustainable business operation. Prerequisites: MAT 115 , BUS 240 .

title: Business Environment and Public Policy
description: Examines the impact of public policy on business  through exploration and analysis of political,  social, regulatory and technological variables  that influence managerial decision-making in  domestic and global environments. Prerequisites: BUS-124 , ECO-101 , ECO-102

title: Strategic Management
description: Utilizes the case study method and the systems approach to integrate various perspectives and expertise in managerial problem-solving and decision-making. Knowledge and skills acquired through studies in the major are employed as students collaborate to apply a holistic approach to organizational planning and decision-making. Limited to seniors.

title: Entrepreneurial Business Plans
description: A business plan communicates the entrepreneur's vision for the business. It will be used to gain financing, procure supplies, hire workers, acquire customers, and to sift out ideas that sound good but won't work. In this practical course, every student will draft a workable business plan. Prerequisites: ACC-106 and BUS-220 . WI

title: Labor and Employee Relations
description: Government regulations and industry practices guiding employer/employee relations, including  fair treatment, employment-at-will, employee  health, safety, workplace participation and  collective bargaining.

title: Strategic Management
description: Utilizes the case study method and the systems approach to integrate various perspectives and expertise in managerial problem-solving and decision-making. Knowledge and skills acquired through studies in the major are employed as students collaborate to apply a holistic approach to organizational planning and decision-making.

title: International Marketing
description: Analysis of problems of marketing across national  boundaries and within different national markets,  from the vantage point of the exporter and the  international enterprise. The impact of cultural  influences on diverse strategies required to meet  challenges of international product policy,  pricing, promotion and distribution.  Prerequisite: 223.

title: Topics in Business
description: A course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty that are not covered in depth in the regular curriculum.

title: Human Resource Management
description: System elements required to recruit, select, train  and maintain a workforce in business. Emphasis on  management development, wage and salary  administration, and personnel planning.  Prerequisite: 124.

title: Organizational Behavior
description: Multidisciplinary approach to the business  organization as a complex system. Concepts and  theories related to structure and function of  organizations; comprehensive study of strategy  formulation and organizational implementation;  analytical focus on internal operation and  structure and external posture from which  alternatives are developed and policies proposed.  Prerequisite: 124.

title: Process Reengineering & Change Mgt
description: Process reengineering is a management strategy focusing on the analysis and design of workflows and processes within an organization. Process reengineering is used to help organizations fundamentally rethink how they do their work in order to improve service while decreasing operational costs. The course focuses on oversight and successful management of reengineering projects using change theory. Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Healthcare Finance II
description: The second of two courses offered in finance, this course continues the learning from Healthcare Finance I and emphasizes application of knowledge gained in the first course. Particular emphasis is placed on acquiring the necessary skills needed for fiscal management and budget development and oversight. Prerequisite: NUR 545 .

title: Healthcare Finance I
description: In the first of two courses designed to provide the nurse leader with an understanding of financial management techniques, the student will explore the unique problems facing healthcare in the financial relationships among hospitals and other providers. Budget, auditing, cost analysis, capital investment, and equity and debt financing decisions are included. Prerequisite: Admission into the MSN program.

title: Analysis/Reporting Healthcare Data
description: This course introduces students to the concept of data-driven decisions and its role in strategic leadership. Real-world examples are used to help the student develop data driven, analytic thinking in their role as a manager/leader. Data mining of big data sets may be used to uncover information that can inform leadership and drive courses of action.  Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Dmaic Six Sigma Management
description: DMAIC Six Sigma is one of three disciplines in the Six Sigma Management philosophy that concentrates on developing a data-driven improvement cycle used to improve, optimize and create stability in business processes. DMAIC Six Sigma uses the Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve and Control approach, which leads to a continuous improvement culture in business and industry. Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: International Business
description: Introduction to international business  environments and how they affect international enterprises. Topics include international risk, market entry strategies, ownership policies,  functional areas and organizational planning and  control. Prerequisites: 124, ECO 101 , 102.

title: Lean Six Sigma Management
description: Lean Six Sigma is one of three disciplines in the Six Sigma Management philosophy that concentrates on the reduction of the eight types of waste commonly found in manufacturing and service-based industries. Waste elimination leads to fewer quality deficiencies, increased revenue, increased profit and increased customer satisfaction. Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Interdisciplinary Topics
description: As a topics course, issues with which graduates need to be conversant are addressed. The topics represent areas that require specialized personnel with whom the leader will interacting or supervising. Areas of focus vary from year to year but can include content related to quality, technology, or human resources. A seminar approach is used to cover multiple issues.  Permission of th director.

title: DFSS Six Sigma Management
description: DFSS Six Sigma is one of three disciplines in the Six Sigma Management philosophy that concentrates on determining the needs of customers and designing out defects in products and processes rather than correcting the process or product after initial application or production. DFSS Six Sigma incorporates systems architecture, quality function deployment, axiomatic design, TRIZ and design of experiments into a continuous improvement culture in business and industries. Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: General Chemistry I Lab
description: Laboratory component for CHM 101 : General  Chemistry I.

title: Hist Healthcare Reform/Universal Models
description: Introduces students to the legislative successes, failures and fragmented approach to healthcare legislation over the past century. Students are introduced to universal healthcare initiatives in selected countries. Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Ethics, Policy, Legal Issues-Healthcare
description: Provides an examination of the healthcare policy processes and issues at organizational, governmental, and global levels, as well as a critical analysis of ethical principles, ethical theories, and contemporary healthcare ethical issues relating to individuals and society. Emphasis is placed on developing an interdisciplinary framework to solving ethical dilemmas.  Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Advanced Financial Management
description: Managers must be able to understand financial information contained in financial statements and reports. Additionally, healthcare managers must be able to make sound, data-driven financial decisions that lead to sustainability of the institution. This course will focus on the interpretation and use of financial information. Pre-requisite: BUS 546 or permission of instructor.

title: General Chemistry II
description: The continuation of General Chemistry I with  emphasis on solutions, kinetics, equilibrium,  acid-base chemistry, thermodynamics and  electrochemistry. Laboratory work reinforces  lecture topics and includes qualitative analysis.  Three hours lecture; three hours lab.  Prerequisites: CHM 101 , MAT 103 . NSL (4 sem.  hrs.)

title: General Chemistry II Lab
description: Laboratory component for CHM 102 : General  Chemistry II.

title: Innovative Management Models
description: Traditional approaches to the delivery of healthcare services in the U.S. will no longer allow an organization to be financially sustainable.  Explores various innovative models that will allow management to optimize its outcomes in the evolving regulatory environment created by PPACA. Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Fundamentals of Organic Chemistry Lab
description: Laboratory component for CHM 104 : Fundamentals of  Organic Chemistry in Biological Systems.

title: Fundamentals of General Chemistry
description: Designed to meet a one-semester general chemistry  requirement. Provides an overview of atomic  structure and bonding, stoichiometry, energy and  acid-base chemistry, redox chemistry, equilibrium  kinetics and hydrocarbons. Laboratory work  reinforces lecture topics. Three hours lecture;  three hours laboratory. Prerequisite: MAT 098/100  or equivalent. NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Organic Chemistry I
description: The first course of a two-semester sequence that  introduces structure, bonding and reactivity of organic (carbon-based) compounds. Lecture  focuses on properties and reactivity of alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, alcohols, aldehydes and ketones,  with an emphasis on reaction mechanisms and stereochemistry. Laboratory introduces basic  purification and synthetic techniques and  analytical methods including polarimetry,  infrared (IR) and nuclear magnetic resonance  (NMR) spectroscopy. Three hours lecture; three  hours laboratory. Prerequisite: CHM 102 . NSL (4  sem. hrs.)

title: Organic Chemistry I Lab
description: Laboratory component of CHM-201 : Organic Chemistry  I. Lab fee required.

title: General Chemistry I
description: The first semester of a two-semester sequence  intended for students majoring in the sciences.  The course focuses on the study of atomic  structure, nomenclature, stoichiometry,  energy,bonding and coordination chemistry.  Laboratory work reinforces lecture topics. Three  hours lecture; three hours lab. Prerequisite:  Concurrent enrollment in MAT 103 or placement  above MAT 103 . High school chemistry or an  introductory chemistry course is strongly  recommended. NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Organic Chemistry II Lab
description: Laboratory component of CHM 202 : Organic Chemistry II. Lab fee required.

title: Analytical Chemistry
description: A study of the fundamental principles of  statistical analysis of data, theory and  quantitative methods of spectrophotometry and  analytic separations. Laboratory work integrates  classical and instrumental techniques in the  analysis of real-life samples. Three hours  lecture; three hours lab. Prerequisite: CHM 102 .  NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Analytical Chemistry Lab
description: Laboratory component of CHM 205 : Analytical  Chemistry. Lab fee required.

title: Physical Chemistry I Lab
description: Laboratory component of CHM 301 : Physical  Chemistry I. Lab fee required.

title: Topics in Chemistry
description: Topics in the field (e.g., organic chemistry,  analytical chemistry, organometallic chemistry,  spectroscopy, etc.) offered on an irregular basis  in response to student and faculty interest.  Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

title: Fundamentals of General Chemistry Lab
description: Laboratory component of CHM 103 : Fundamentals of  General Chemistry.

title: Fund. of Organic Chem. in Biological Sys
description: Designed to meet a one-semester requirement in  organic chemistry of biological systems. Provides an overview of  nomenclature, functional groups and reactions,  with an emphasis on structure and reactions in  biological systems, including proteins, lipids, carbohydrates, nucleic acids and  enzymes. Three hours lecture; three hours  laboratory. Prerequisite: CHM 103 . NSL (4 sem.  hrs.)

title: Physical Chemistry II
description: Introduction to thermodynamics and equilibrium.  Topics include the laws of thermodynamics, ideal  equilibrium, ideal and non-ideal solutions,  electrochemistry and surface chemistry.  Laboratory work reinforces lecture topics and  emphasizes physical-analytical methods.  Prerequisite: CHM 301 . NS  (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Inorganic Chemistry Lab
description: Laboratory component of CHM 303: Inorganic Chemistry. Lab fee required.

title: Physical Chemistry II Lab
description: Laboratory component of CHM 302 : Physical Chemistry II. Lab fee required.

title: Organic Chemistry II
description: A continuation of CHM 201 with an added emphasis  on molecules with biological importance. Includes  a discussion of carboxylic acids and their  derivatives, enolates, aromatics, amines, lipids,  carbohydrates and amino acids. Laboratory work  includes more advanced synthetic methods and an  independent research project. Three hours  lecture; three hours lab. Prerequisite: CHM 201 .  NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Biochemistry I
description: Introduces students to the major classes of  biomolecules: proteins, carbohydrates, lipids and  nucleic acids. Emphasizes the chemistry that  dictates structure and functions of biological  molecules. Also involves detailed study of  proteinaceous enzymes, models of enzyme activity  and enzymatic regulation; covers nucleic acid  enzymes and current topics in nucleic acid  biochemistry. Discussion includes the combination  of the major classes of biological molecules to  form biological membranes and the transport  through such complex structures. Three hours  lecture. Prerequisite: CHM 202 and BIO 102 or BIO  110.

title: Senior Research Seminar II
description: The third course in the three-semester sequence  for biology and chemistry majors with the focus  on the analysis of research results, the research  paper and a poster presentation. Completion of  this course fulfills one writing-intensive  requirement. (2 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite:  CHM  400. WI

title: Senior Research Seminar I
description: The second course in the three-semester research  sequence for biology and chemistry majors focuses  on researchand oral presentations. Seminar based  on original research and/or recent scientific  literature following the proposal developed in CHM 398 . Seminar presentations and a draft  research paper are required. Specific research  topics selected based on interests and background  of faculty and students. (three semester hours.)  Prerequisite: CHM 398 .

title: Design/Methods Scientifc Rsrch
description: Students learn to explore primary scientific  literature, develop a research project and -  depending on student and faculty interest - learn  advanced laboratory skills. Usually followed by CHM 400 and 402, the course results in a research  proposal. (two semester hours.) Prerequisite: At  least four courses above the 200 level in  chemistry. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Physical Chemistry I
description: Introduction to kinetics and quantum chemistry. Topics include quantum mechanics, molecular spectroscopy and reaction rates. Prerequisites: CHM 205 or concurrent enrollment, MAT 140 and PHY 102 . NS

title: Ethics in Criminal Justice
description: Study of ethical thought and issues facing the criminal justice professional. Topics include moral development, standards of conduct, and the status of morality in criminal justice.  Prerequisite: Foundations in English course.  ETH

title: Corrections
description: This course analyzes a variety of correctional practices and institutions, including sentencing, probation, jails and prisons, capital punishment, and parole/reentry programs.  Prerequisite: CJ-101 .

title: Law Enforcement
description: This course analyzes contemporary policing and law enforcement practices. Topics include law enforcement culture, police conduct and misconduct, contemporary policing strategies, and the future of policing.  Prerequisite: CJ-101 .

title: Introduction to Law & Courts
description: A study of American legal system, this course addresses the nature of law and its function in society; structure and processes of federal, state, and local court systems; role of people who work in the judicial system, including judges, prosecutors, and defense attorneys. Prerequisite:  English Foundations course.  CC, ETH, WI

title: Biochemistry II
description: A continuation of the Biochemistry I course beyond  an introduction to the major classes of  biomolecules. The course involves a detailed  study of biomolecule metabolism (biochemical  thermodynamics, glycolysis, tricarboxylic acid  cycle, electron transport, oxidative  phosphorylation, gluconeogenesis, fatty acid  catabolism, amino acid metabolism, nucleic acid  metabolism, glycogen metabolism) and DNA  processing into RNA and protein (replication,  transcription, translation, protein folding,  protein processing and protein degradation).  Three hours lecture. Prerequisite: CHM 318 .

title: Criminal Law and Procedure
description: Examines the substance of criminal law and processes of the criminal justice system. This course focuses on criminal culpability; elements of felonies and misdemeanor crimes; rights of the accused; pretrial and trial procedures; punishment and sentencing, including the death penalty. Prerequisites:  Foundations in English course and PS-120 or CJ-101 .  ETH

title: Topics in Advanced Chemistry
description: Topics in the field (e.g., organic chemistry, analytical chemistry, organometallic chemistry, spectroscopy, etc.) offered on an irregular basis in response to student and faculty interest. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

title: Introduction to Criminal Justice
description: This course introduces the main elements of criminal justice studies, including the nature of crime and its causes, law enforcement, courts and adjudication, corrections, and contemporary issues in the field. CC

title: Transforming Communities
description: Study of the practices, attitudes, and other factors capable of transforming communities marred by violence into flourishing communities. Topics include violence, nonviolence, community organizing.  Prerequisites:  Foundations in English.  ETH

title: Effective Speaking
description: Students develop and practice public speaking skills that will be useful in a broad range of careers. The focus will be on informative and persuasive speaking skills, with an emphasis on speech research, organization and delivery. In the supportive climate of the classroom, students will grow in their  understanding of speaking principles and their ability to use public speaking skills. ETH

title: Internship
description: Work experience with a significant academic component. Students will complete a portfolio that exhibits both a visual and written display of learning, including evidence of theoretical knowledge and experiential learning. The portfolio may be used as a record of the student's work for graduate school or employment. A presentation to the college community is also required. Prerequisite: second-semester junior or senior if used to fulfill capstone requirement.

title: Gender, Crime and Justice
description: This course analyzes how gender is expressed in laws, policing practices, and who perpetrates or is victimized by crime. An intersectional approach to violence against women and gender non-conforming groups also will be examined. Prerequisite: CJ 101 or SOC 120 . CC, WS

title: Deviance & Criminal Justice
description: Explores sociological theories and critical  perspectives on deviance and criminal behavior  are explored. Considers informal and formal methods of  social control including stigma, incarceration,  and institutionalization.   Critically analyzes  the impact of social institutions and  inequalities on individual,  governmental and corporate crime and deviance is critically analyzed. Prerequisite: CJ-101 . CC

title: Public Relations
description: Study of persuasion and public messaging and the history and development of the field. Topics include general strategies and tactics, media forms and styles, and legal, ethical, and global concerns. Prerequisite:  Foundations in English course.

title: Social Media and Society
description: Examination of social messaging and networking systems from historical, economic, and ethical perspectives. Considers private and public clients, information and communications technologies, and the effects of social media on societies and cultures. Prerequisite:  Foundations in English.

title: Topics in Communications
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics  of interest to students and faculty that are not  treated in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite:  permission of instructor.

title: Strategic Communications
description: Advanced study and practice in the field of persuasive messaging, with focus on public campaigns. Emphases include planning and research methods, key audiences and publics, and communications management.Prerequisite: 200 level COM course.

title: Media Law and Ethics
description: Legal and ethics-based study of court rulings and  other issues that concern media practitioners, especially with respect to  censorship, obscenity, libel, copyright, privacy  and First Amendment rights and responsibilities.  HWC, ETH, WI

title: Gender, Media, and Society
description: Class, ethnicity, gender and other relevant social and cultural aspects of media audiences and media culture are examined, as well as current trends in feminist theory and criticism that come to bear on the production of popular media and communications in society. Prerequisites: ENG-101, ENG-104 , ENG-108 , OR ENG-180 and ne course in COM or ENG at the 200 level) CC, WS, WI

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content, having an appropriate, complementary role within the student's academic program. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Intro to Media Studies
description: Development and contributions of various media to  contemporary society. Historical analysis of  publishing, newspapers, film,radio, television,  advertising, public relations, cable and new  technologies. Includes communication theories and  research methods. CC

title: Interpersonal Communication
description: Survey of psychological, environmental, cultural  and socioeconomic variables that influence  communication. Defines competent communicators in a variety of  situations; explores appropriate tools to alter  communication behavior. CD

title: Topics in Communications
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics  of interest to students and faculty that are not  treated in the regular curriculum.

title: Media Law and Ethics
description: Legal and ethics-based study of court rulings and  other issues that concern media practitioners, especially with respect to censorship, obscenity,  libel, copyright, privacy and First Amendment  rights and responsibilities. ETH, HWC, WI  Prerequisite: permission of the instructor.

title: Media Theory and Methods
description: Examines the influence of traditional forms of  media as well as new media technologies and the cultural conditions they establish. The course  explores the history and theories of print media, communications, and digital technology, and their  impact upon and implications for contemporary society and intercultural dialogue. CC, WI

title: Integrated Marketing and Advertising
description: Examination of integrated communications  practices, including blending of both traditional  and electronic public relations and advertising  practices and social media. Course will also  explore integrated communication case studies and  theory, exploration of creative practices in  advertising, including brand concept, audience  analysis and design theory. A service-learning  component is required. Prerequisite: COM 130 or  permission of instructor. CC

title: Gender, Media, and Society
description: Class, ethnicity, gender and other relevant social and cultural aspects of media audiences and media culture are examined, as well as current trends in feminist theory and criticism that come to bear on the production of popular media and communications in society.

title: Interdisciplinary Collaboration I
description: Students explore theoretical and practical approaches to determining meaning in a variety of art forms. Practice-led and academic research focuses on collaboration across disciplines. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.)  Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Somatic Practice I
description: Students explore the combined role of the body-mind in art making practices. Embodied research helps students examine their art work in the context of human ecology. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.)Prerequisite: Admission to program.

title: Higher Education Seminar
description: This seminar is an examination of the path from being a graduate student to becoming a tenured faculty member. Students analyze academic job postings, craft application materials, and draft course proposals, syllabi and assessment rubrics. Students examine concerns such as Title XI, accreditation, and promotion and tenure, and engage in mock faculty interviews.  (1 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to program

title: Media Theory and Methods
description: Examines the influence of traditional forms of  media as well as new media technologies and the  cultural conditions they establish. Explores the  history and theories of print media,  communications, and digital technology, and their  impact upon and implications for contemporary  society and intercultural dialogue. CC, WI

title: Praxis: Visual Arts
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in visual-based art forms drawn from the last 100 years. Rotating topics place the visual artists studied in the sociopolitical context of their times. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.) Prerequisites: permission of director.

title: Praxis: Creative Writing II
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in movement-based art forms drawn from the last 100 years. Rotating topics place the movement-based artists studied in the sociopolitical context of their times. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Studio Practice III
description: Students deepen home-based studio practice during non-resident periods in order to produce a semester portfolio. Includes individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Online Critical Response Process included. Mentoring by Home Studio Mentors and Wilson faculty required. (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Studio Practice II
description: Students establish a strong home-based studio practice during non-resident periods in order to produce a semester portfolio. Includes individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Online Critical Response Process included. Mentoring by Home Studio Mentors and Wilson faculty required. (3 sem. hrs.) Permission of the Director.

title: Contemporary Arts Practice I
description: Students examine the practice of art beyond media boundaries and the tensions inherent in making and presenting work in the 21st century. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Studio Practice IV
description: Intensive creative practice with close mentoring. Students explore content, form, and materials in individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Students demonstrate a cohesive conceptual and technical approach to art-making. Critical Response Process is included. Prequisite: Permission of the Director.

title: Contemporary Arts Practice II
description: Students further examine the practice of art beyond media boundaries and the tensions inherent in making and presenting work in the 21st century. Each student is responsible for substantial advance preparation. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Site-Sensitive Art-Making I
description: Exposes students to environmental mediation in art-making by exploring site-sensitive instillation, performance, writing, etc. The application of technology to exhibition/performance is encouraged. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to program

title: Interdisciplinary Collaboration II
description: Students deepen exploration of theoretical and practical approaches to determining meaning in a variety of art forms. Practice-led and academic research focus on collaboration across disciplines. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Studio Practice I
description: Intensive creative practice with close mentoring. Students explore content, form, and materials in individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Students demonstrate a cohesive conceptual and technical approach to art-making. Critical Response Process is included. (6 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Somatic Practice II
description: Students expand their understanding of the body-mind in art making practices. Embodied research helps students examine their art work in the context of human ecology. Movement-based creative inquiry is emphasized. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. ((2 sem. hrs.)

title: Praxis: Creative Writing
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in writing-based art forms drawn from the last 100 years. Rotating topics place the writers studied in the sociopolitical context of their times. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.)Prerequisite:  Permission of the Director.

title: Thesis Performance/Exhibition I
description: Students pursue a sophisticated thesis question via rigorous practice-led and academic research, and begin documenting their process in a context statement. Frequent interaction with both the onsite and Wilson faculty mentors is required. Prerequisite: Finalized thesis proposal, and permission of the program director. (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Thesis Performance/Exhibition II
description: The student will complete and present the thesis exhibition/performance with appropriate documentation. All materials must demonstrate mastery of conception, intention and production. An oral defense of the thesis exhibition/performance is required. Prerequisite: FA 598 . (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Programming II
description: This course teaches students about the fundamentals of data access, data management, and expands upon the topics learned in Programming I. Students will work with HTML, XML and JSON data formats in Python. The course also introduces students to the fundamentals of Structured Query Language (SQL) and database design as part of a multi-step data gathering, analysis and processing effort. As part of the course, students will build web crawlers and multi-step data gathering and visualization processes. Prerequisite: CS-120 .

title: Data Management and Modeling
description: This course explores the fundamentals of data structures and why they are so crucial for optimizing performance. Students will examine linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, and graphs, and understand their properties, use cases, and implementation. Prerequisite: CS-122 and MAT-115 .

title: Topics in Computer Science
description: Courses offered periodically to broaden the curriculum with subjects of special interest. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

title: Site-Sensitive Art Making II
description: Students further their work in environmental mediation in art-making by exploring site-sensitive instillation, performance, writing, etc. The application of technology to site-specific work is encouraged. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Permissoin of Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Web Development and Design
description: This course introduces students to HTML, CSS, and Javascript-the core technologies which power modern websites. Students learn to critically evaluate website quality and create and maintain quality Web pages. Students will learn how to create interactive, aesthetically pleasing websites for a variety of purposes. This course culminates with a project in which students will build a website for a real or imaginary product of their choosing. ART Prerequisites: CS-120

title: Fundamentals of Applied AI
description: The course examines basic AI architecture and surveys how AI is applied in a vast range of areas based on its designs, including science, media, communications, and public policy. Students will practice the application of AI in a variety of fields and explore the ethics and legality of AI usage and generation.  Prerequisite: MAT-103 or MAT-115 or PSY-115 .

title: Application Development I: Backend
description: This course explores the fundamentals of building dynamic, database driven web applications using backend scripts including Java and JavaScript. Students will also construct RESTful APIs and develop middleware for user authentication and session management. Projects include real-world application development. Prerequisites: CS-122 .

title: Design Patterns and Algorithms
description: This course provides an introduction to mathematical modeling of computational problems and an introduction to algorithm design through a survey of the common algorithm design paradigms of greedy optimization, divide and conquer, dynamic programming, network flows, reductions, and randomized algorithms. The course emphasizes the relationship between algorithms and programming and introduces basic performance measures and analysis techniques for these problems.  Prerequisites: CS-222 and MAT-205 .

title: Programming I
description: Explores the basics of programming computers using Python, including constructing a program from a series of simple instructions in Python. We will explore the use of Python built-in data structures such as lists, dictionaries, and tuples to perform data analysis. This course will introduce the core syntax, commands, and data structures of the Python programming language.

title: Computer System Organization
description: This course explores the inner workings of computer systems while teaching students the C programming language. Students will learn the fundamentals of operating systems, memory management, and file systems. Students will also explore how microprocessors execute instructions and how peripherals communicate with the main system. Prerequisite: CS 122 .

title: Modern Technologies
description: This class explores why new technologies are developed and how they impact businesses and society. Students will understand the driving forces behind technological advancements and their potential benefits. They will also build functional applications using tools like Docker, AI, Jira, GitHub, Jenkins, and Confluence. Prerequisite: CS-120

title: Software Development
description: This class explores why new technologies are developed and how they impact businesses and society. Students will understand the driving forces behind technological advancements and their potential benefits. They will also build functional applications using tools like Docker, AI, Jira, GitHub, Jenkins, and Confluence. Prerequisite: CS-122

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content, having an appropriate, complementary role within the student's academic program. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Topic in Computer Science
description: Courses offered periodically to broaden the curriculum with subjects of special interest. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

title: Digital Ethics
description: This course examines the natural and societal impacts of technology while exploring ethical issues in the design and implementation of computing systems and technologies. It covers ethical theories, privacy, security, social media, and misinformation. Topics covered include intellectual property rights, data privacy, cybersecurity, digital rights management, artificial intelligence, and the societal impacts of emerging technologies. ETH  Prerequisites: Foundations in English course.

title: Application Development II: Frontend
description: This course instructs the student on building dynamic web apps using tools such as React and Angular. Students will create front-end interfaces connected to backend services for optimal performance. Students will explore component-based architecture, managing states, and develop a full-stack application by course's end.  Prerequisites: CS-322 and CS-325 .

title: Networking Technologies & Telecom
description: This course instructs students how to effectively manage a computer network, leveraging best practices in configuration, security and monitoring. Topics include network concepts, infrastructure, security, operations, performance, and repair. Prerequisites: CYS-101

title: Cybercrime and Governance
description: This course instructs the student on how to develop and implement robust security policies, procedures, and emergency measures that comply with legal and governmental requirements and protect individual rights. Topics include risk assessment; security procedures and policies; and legal frameworks and compliance.  Prerequisite: CYS-101

title: Security Operations
description: This course provides hands-on experience in incident detection and response, investigating cybercrimes, gathering digital evidence, and communicating findings effectively. Students will learn to conduct incident investigations using modern forensic tools and execute response and recovery plans to minimize the impact of security events. Topics include forensic tools and analysis; incidence response simulation; and post-incident analysis.  Prerequisite: CYS-231

title: Network and System Security
description: This course covers how to identify and mitigate common network and system vulnerabilities, implement security controls, and utilize tools like SIEM and encryption to protect against cyber-attacks. Topics include SIEM systems utilization; encryption and firewall application; and system hardening practices. Prerequisite: CYS-211

title: Foundations of Dance II
description: Beginning students will explore and utilize practices to develop efficient body mechanics, balanced strength and flexibility, total body coordination, clarity of spatial access, rhythmic play and integration, qualitative and energetic dynamics, expressive intentionality, and pattern recognition and memorization, in the context of a basic dance class structure. Prerequisite: DNC151 OR permission of the instructor. ART

title: Foundations of Dance I
description: Beginning students will explore and utilize practices to develop efficient body mechanics, balanced strength and flexibility, total body coordination, clarity of spatial access, rhythmic play and integration, qualitative and energetic dynamics, expressive intentionality, and pattern recognition and memorization, in the context of a basic dance class structure. Prerequisite: NONE. ART

title: Capstone Project
description: The Capstone offers students the opportunity to demonstrate their learning through project-based work and application. Students will also explore professional outcomes and work on preparing materials in furtherance of target opportunities in the field. Prerequisites:  Senior status and permission

title: Topics in Dance
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum and to address student and faculty interests.

title: Introduction to Cybersecurity
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of cybersecurity principles, threats, and defensive techniques, equipping students with the knowledge and skills to protect digital assets and secure online privacy. Topics include cybersecurity tools and frameworks, security system management, data protection, and foundations of cryptography.

title: Contemporary Dance Practices II
description: Students will establish a sound physical and creative dance practice, rooted in contemporary approaches, including aspects of Somatics, Modern, Jazz, Ballet, and Improvisation, with an emphasis on competency in effective/efficient body mechanics, balanced strength and flexibility, clarity of spatial access, rhythmic play and integration, qualitative dynamics, expressive intentionality, and pattern recognition/memorization.  Prerequisite: DNC 231 . ART

title: Dance in America
