description: An introductory survey of dance in 20th and 21st Century America, including the development of varying disciplines, styles, theories, and applications and their cultural and historical implications. Students will explore and examine dance as a means of expression, connection, protest, and physical practice through viewing, writing, research, and embodiment. CC

title: Contemporary Dance Practices I
description: Students will establish a sound physical and creative dance practice, rooted in contemporary approaches, including aspects of Somatics, Modern, Jazz, Ballet, and Improvisation, with an emphasis on competency in effective/efficient body mechanics, balanced strength and flexibility, clarity of spatial access, rhythmic play and integration, qualitative dynamics, expressive intentionality, and pattern recognition/memorization.  Prerequisite: DNC 152 OR Permission of the instructor. ART

title: Ethical Hacking
description: This course provides an understanding of cybersecurity risks and defenses through the practice of white-hat hacking. Students will learn hands-on techniques for attacking and penetrating networks and systems and develop and execute ethical hacking strategies, utilizing industry standard tools and techniques to discover vulnerabilities within networks and systems. Prerequisite: CYS-231

title: Topics in Dance
description: Special Topics in Dance

title: Improvisation & Composition Strategies
description: Students will explore, devise, and hone skills in various approaches to dance/movement improvisation, both as a performative discipline and a tool for choreographic development. Students will then further their craft by exploring and utilizing choreographic devices, movement research, and collaborative projects to create their own work.  Prerequisite: DNC-151 . ART

title: The Future of Cybersecurity
description: This course explores emerging trends and technologies in cybersecurity, such as mobile and cloud security, with a focus on how to assess and mitigate risks in these evolving landscapes. Topics include cloud security; social engineering; mobile security assessment; and AI and blockchain. Prerequisite: CYS-231

title: Teaching Apprenticeship
description: The teaching apprenticeship allows the student to mentor under a member of the Wilson faculty in up to two undergraduate courses. As part of the experience, students may take part in the creation of materials for the classes, observe the class, hold office hours, tutor students in the subject, research and devise selected teaching materials, and teach one unit as the primary instructor, including the grading of paper/assignments. Prerequisites: Junior standing (undergraduate) and Permission.

title: Decolonizing the Moving Body
description: Students will examine the influence and impact of cultural and sociopolitical systems of power on the way individuals experience, inhabit, and communicate through their bodies. Emphasis on the training, performance, representation, and accessibility within Dance and other related movement forms, and the parallel organizational structures within those areas throughout history. Prerequisite:  Foundations in English. CD, WS, WI

title: Contemporary Dance Practices IV
description: Students will deepen their physical and creative dance practice through contemporary approaches, including integration of Somatics, Modern, Jazz, Ballet, and Improvisation, honing their ability to access appropriate body-level support, spatial clarity, rhythmic integration and variation, qualitative dynamics, expressive intentionality/specificity, and more complex movement patterns.  Prerequisite: DNC 361 . ART

title: Topics in Dance
description: Special Topics in Dance

title: Contemporary Dance Practices VI
description: Students will move toward mastery in their physical and creative dance practice through contemporary approaches, while exploring their own value system and artistic voice within the context of this course. Emphasis on a high-level integration of body-level support, spatial clarity, embodied rhythm and variation, qualitative dynamics, expressive specificity, and complex movement patterns.  Prerequisite: DNC 461

title: Studio Practice I
description: Intensive creative practice with close mentoring. Students explore content, form, and materials in individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Students demonstrate a cohesive conceptual and technical approach to art-making. Critical Response Process is included. (6 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Somatics & Movement Integration
description: Students will explore and utilize a variety of Somatic approaches to access embodied awareness and the mind-body connection. Incorporating experiential anatomy, physical practice, journaling, movement observation/analysis, and discussion, students will examine the reciprocal relationship between their physical and cognitive selves, and the impact that has on their relationships with the world. Prerequisite:  Foundations in English.

title: Interdisciplinary Collaboration I
description: Students explore theoretical and practical approaches to determining meaning in a variety of art forms. Practice-led and academic research focuses on collaboration across disciplines. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.)  Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Contemporary Arts Practice I
description: Students examine the practice of art beyond media boundaries and the tensions inherent in making and presenting work in the 21st century. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Authority, Influence and the Moving Body
description: Students will examine the influence and impact of cultural and sociopolitical systems of power on the way individuals experience, inhabit, and communicate through their bodies. Emphasis on the training, performance, representation, and accessibility within Dance and other related movement forms, and the parallel organizational structures within those areas throughout history. Prerequisite:  Foundations in English. CD, WS, WI

title: Contemporary Dance Practices III
description: Students will deepen their physical and creative dance practice through contemporary approaches, including integration of Somatics, Modern, Jazz, Ballet, and Improvisation, honing their ability to access appropriate body-level support, spatial clarity, rhythmic integration and variation, qualitative dynamics, expressive intentionality/specificity, and more complex movement patterns.  Prerequisite: DNC 232 . ART

title: Higher Education Seminar
description: This seminar is an examination of the path from being a graduate student to becoming a tenured faculty member. Students analyze academic job postings, craft application materials, and draft course proposals, syllabi and assessment rubrics. Students examine concerns such as Title XI, accreditation, and promotion and tenure, and engage in mock faculty interviews.  (1 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to program

title: Somatic Practice I
description: Students explore the combined role of the body-mind in art making practices. Embodied research helps students examine their art work in the context of human ecology. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.)Prerequisite: Admission to program.

title: Praxis: Visual Arts
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in visual-based art forms drawn from the last 100 years. Rotating topics place the visual artists studied in the sociopolitical context of their times. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.)  Prerequisites: permission of director.

title: Contemporary Dance Practices V
description: Students will move toward mastery in their physical and creative dance practice through contemporary approaches, while exploring their own value system and artistic voice within the context of this course. Emphasis on a high-level integration of body-level support, spatial clarity, embodied rhythm and variation, qualitative dynamics, expressive specificity, and complex movement patterns.  Prerequisite: DNC 362

title: Studio Practice II
description: Students establish a strong home-based studio practice during non-resident periods in order to produce a semester portfolio. Includes individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Online Critical Response Process included. Mentoring by Home Studio Mentors and Wilson faculty required. (3 sem. hrs.)  Permission of Director.

title: Praxis: Creative Writing
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in writing-based art forms drawn from the last 100 years. Rotating topics place the writers studied in the sociopolitical context of their times. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.)Prerequisite:  Permission of the Director.

title: Studio Practice IV
description: Intensive creative practice with close mentoring. Students explore content, form, and materials in individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Students demonstrate a cohesive conceptual and technical approach to art-making. Critical Response Process is included. Prequisite: Permission of the Director.

title: Interdisciplinary Collaboration II
description: Students deepen exploration of theoretical and practical approaches to determining meaning in a variety of art forms. Practice-led and academic research focus on collaboration across disciplines. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Somatic Practice II
description: Students expand their understanding of the body-mind in art making practices. Embodied research helps students examine their art work in the context of human ecology. Movement-based creative inquiry is emphasized. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. ((2 sem. hrs.)

title: Site-Sensitive Art Making II
description: Students further their work in environmental mediation in art-making by exploring site-sensitive instillation, performance, writing, etc. The application of technology to site-specific work is encouraged. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Permissoin of Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Site-Sensitive Art-Making I
description: Exposes students to environmental mediation in art-making by exploring site-sensitive instillation, performance, writing, etc. The application of technology to exhibition/performance is encouraged. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to program

title: Independent Study
description: Independent Study

title: Studio Practice III
description: Students deepen home-based studio practice during non-resident periods in order to produce a semester portfolio. Includes individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Online Critical Response Process included. Mentoring by Home Studio Mentors and Wilson faculty required. (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Praxis: Movement Arts
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in movement-based art forms drawn from the last 100 years. Rotating topics place the movement-based artists studied in the sociopolitical context of their times. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Thesis Performance/Exhibition I
description: Students pursue a sophisticated thesis question via rigorous practice-led and academic research, and begin documenting their process in a context statement. Frequent interaction with both the onsite and Wilson faculty mentors is required. Prerequisite: Finalized thesis proposal, and permission of the program director. (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Thesis Performance/Exhibition II
description: The student will complete and present the thesis exhibition/performance with appropriate documentation, as well as an assessment portfolio. All materials must demonstrate mastery of conception, intention and production. An oral defense of the thesis exhibition/performance is required. (6 semester hours) Prerequisite: FA 598 .

title: Consumer Economics
description: Examines market economic principles, the role of  financial institutions and how this affects  individuals as consumers, producers and citizens.  Students investigate consumer practices and  responsibilities, which includes planning for  financial security. This course is for  nonbusiness, non-accounting and non-economics  majors. CC

title: Intro to Macroeconomics
description: Institutions and functioning of capitalism; national income and its determination; monetary and fiscal policy; international economics; and problems of growth and development. CC

title: Contemporary Arts Practice II
description: Students further examine the practice of art beyond media boundaries and the tensions inherent in making and presenting work in the 21st century. Each student is responsible for substantial advance preparation. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Public Finance
description: Includes taxation and government expenditures and their effects, fiscal policy and management of government debt. Prerequisites: ECO 101 and ECO  102.

title: Comparative Econmics/Political Systems
description: Study of major international economic and political systems and the role each plays in influencing global economics and politics. Prerequisites: ECO 101 , ECO 102 , PS 110, or PS 120 . NWC

title: Money and Banking
description: Covers money, commercial banking, the Federal Reserve system, unemployment, inflation, growth, monetary policy and international monetary problems. Prerequisite: ECO 101 .

title: Topics in Economics
description: Courses offered periodically in response to  student interests.

title: Senior Thesis
description: In conjunction with a faculty adviser, the student will design and implement a major research project. Prerequisite: Permission of the instructor.

title: Geography for Educators
description: Provides an introduction to geography for  pre-service education students in Pre-K-4 and 4-8  and secondary social studies. The focal points  addressed include: basic geography literacy and  tools, physical and human characteristics of  places and regions, and the interactions between  people and places.

title: Intro to Microeconomics
description: The mechanisms by which resources are allocated in a market system, and study of modifications to account for imperfections in the system. Topics include demand, production and cost, theories of business behavior and consumer behavior, market performance in the U.S. economy and comparative economic systems. CC

title: Child Development, Cognition & Learning
description: Focuses on the social, emotional, physical and  cognitive development of children from early  childhood to the high school years.  Requires observations in local schools and child  care facilities, including facilities with  special needs and English language learners (ELL)  populations. CC. Prerequisite: PSY 110 . Waived for TCP students.

title: Intermediate Macroeconomics
description: An introduction to national income accounting, determination of national income and employment, multiplier and accelerator, determinants of investment, causes of inflation and unemployment, and monetary and fiscal policy. Prerequisite: ECO 101 .

title: Educational Psychology
description: A pragmatically oriented study of learning  processes, factors and theoretical components  that stimulate and impede those processes.  Focuses on classroom management skills and  techniques of interpersonal relations, special  needs of learners with exceptionalities, and  racial and ethnic minorities. Observations in  local schools and other institutions.  Prerequisite: PSY 110 . Waived for TCP students.

title: Intermediate Microeconomics
description: Analysis of supply and demand, theory of firms, market situations, distribution of income, general equilibrium theory and welfare economics. Prerequisite: ECO 102 .

title: Education - Students W/Special Needs
description: Addresses the organizational skills and  alternative strategies needed to manage the  diversity within America's classrooms. Analyzes  teacher behaviors and their implications for  classroom management. Examines the  characteristics of unique individuals and their  relationship to learning situations. Identifies  principles of educational programming for  students with exceptional needs, including  collaborative models for use of support staff.  Requires classroom observations in local schools. CD.  Prerequisite: PSY 110 . Waived for TCP students.

title: Adaptive Physical Education
description: This course provides the students with the competencies necessary to screen and evaluate the needs of individuals with various physical and/or mental disabilities. The students will develop appropriate goals and learning objectives in the area of motor fitness as well as adapting activities based on the needs of the individual are emphasized. Students will complete a total of 4 observation hours. Prerequisites: EDU206

title: Environmental Education
description: Introduces students to fundamental approaches,  processes and goals of environmental education  (EE). Much of the course is fieldwork and  students learn EE curriculum recognized and  adopted on a national or statewide basis. Guest  speakers, experiential learning and field trips  to EE centers are integral parts of class  content. Uses a variety of EE instructional  methods for classroom and field use. The Fulton  Farm provides an excellent setting for many of  the studies. Students learn about standards the  Pennsylvania Department of Education requires in  EE curricula, how to evaluate lesson programs and  how to best deliver material in an engaging  fashion. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.  ES

title: Teaching Strategies for Health & PE
description: This course provides the students with the  application of best practices in instruction and  learning related to health and physical education. The students will develop appropriate  lesson planning strategies that relate to  differentiated instruction, assessment, and pedagogy. Students will complete a total of 4  observation hours. Prerequisites: Admission into  the Education Program or ATH-241

title: Topics in Economics
description: Courses offered periodically in response to student interests.

title: Health Methods, Prek-12
description: This course provides the students with the application of best practices in instruction and learning related to health education. The students will develop appropriate lesson planning strategies that relate to differentiated instruction, assessment, and pedagogy. Students will complete a total of 2 observation hours. Prerequisites: 206 and admission to the education  program.

title: Secondary Methods in Literacy
description: This course is designed to prepare PK-12 (HPE, Spanish, Business) and 7-12 secondary educators to enter into classrooms understanding literacy development and pedagogy. Current theory, research, and best practices will be integrated with reading, writing, and assessment practices. Pre-service educators will develop a literacy philosophy that encompasses major components of literacy instruction to ensure success for all students. Pre-requisite:  Official acceptance into the TIP/Education Program.

title: Teaching Social Studies in Prek-4
description: Each course prepares the certification candidate with the working knowledge required to become an  effective teacher in the designated discipline.  Examines the scope and sequence of the curricula.  Provides developmentally appropriate  instructional strategies, techniques for  individualizing instruction and assessing  achievement, classroom management skills and  using media. Develops competencies in lesson and  unit planning and instructional delivery. Informs  about professional development opportunities  relative to the various disciplines. Requires  peer teaching and observations of school classrooms. Prerequisites: 206 and admission to the education program.

title: Teaching Science in Pre-K - 4 Classroom
description: Each course prepares the certification candidate with the working knowledge required to become an  effective teacher in the designated discipline.  Examines the scope and sequence of the curricula.  Provides developmentally appropriate  instructional strategies, techniques for  individualizing instruction and assessing  achievement, classroom management skills and  using media. Develops competencies in lesson and  unit planning and instructional delivery. Informs  about professional development opportunities  relative to the various disciplines. Requires  peer teaching and observations of school classrooms. Prerequisites: 206 and admission to the education  program.

title: Educational Assessment
description: This course will provide pre-service educators with theory and practice of commonly used assessment tools including but not limited to formal, informal, authentic, and benchmark assessments. Content will focus on how to effectively interpret data and how to translate data into academic achievement. Prerequisite: EDU  206.

title: Teaching Math in Pre-K - 4 Classrooms
description: Each course prepares the certification candidate with the working knowledge required to become an  effective teacher in the designated discipline.  Examines the scope and sequence of the curricula.  Provides developmentally appropriate  instructional strategies, techniques for  individualizing instruction and assessing  achievement, classroom management skills and  using media. Develops competencies in lesson and  unit planning and instructional delivery. Informs  about professional development opportunities  relative to the various disciplines. Requires  peer teaching and observations of school classrooms. Prerequisites: 206 and admission to the education  program.

title: Middle Level Teaching Methods
description: This course, intended for middle level teacher certification candidates, will provide a foundation in proven, research-based teaching strategies. Students will use strategies to develop and teach lessons during the course. They will become knowledgeable with the Pennsylvania Standards Aligned System in order to plan effective lessons. Other topics include classroom management, differentiation, multiple intelligences, assessment, understanding poverty, and literacy skills across the curriculum. Prerequisites: 204, 206 and admission to the education program.

title: Pre-K-4 Language & Literacy Development
description: Provides students with theory and instructional  methods for creating effective and engaging  pre-K-4 classrooms. The importance of the five  building blocks, children's literature and  emergent literacy are stressed. Prerequisite: EDU  206.

title: Teaching English Learners
description: Addresses methods and materials to prepare English language learners to enter and succeed in Pre-K  through 12 mainstream classroom.  Prerequisite: EDU 206 .

title: Secondary Methods in Content
description: Each course prepares the certification candidate  with the working knowledge required to become an  effective teacher in the designated discipline.  Examines the scope and sequence of the curricula.  Provides developmentally appropriate  instructional strategies, techniques for  individualizing instruction and assessing  achievement, classroom management skills and  use of media. Develops competencies in lesson and  unit planning and instructional delivery. Informs  about professional development opportunities  relative to the various disciplines. Requires  peer teaching and observations of school  classrooms. Prerequisites: EDU 206 and admission  to the education program.

title: Methods in Literacy
description: This course is designed to prepare educators with foundational knowledge in literacy pedagogy and content area instruction with an emphasis on comprehension. Current theory, research, and best practices will be integrated with reading, writing, and assessment practices. Pre-service educators will develop a literacy philosophy that encompasses major components of literacy instruction to ensure success for all students. Pre-requisite: Official Acceptance into TIP/Education Program.  Prerequisites: EDU 204 , EDU 206 and admission to the education program.

title: Pre-Practicum
description: A full-time clinical (one day per week for 15  weeks) experience in a local school during the  student's junior year. Requires observing,  aiding, tutoring and teaching. Preparation for  the senior-year student teaching practicum.  Supervised by college faculty. Prerequisites: EDU  204, EDU 215 and admission to the education  program.

title: Tesol Instructional Methods and Assessme
description: Evidence-based instructional methods and assessments for students (PreK-12) with teaching English as a Second Language will be investigated and applied to student generated lesson plans, differentiated to meet the needs of students of other languages. Progress monitoring and data collection related to Individualized ESL will also be investigated.

title: Topics in Education
description: Special topics in Education.

Examines the scope and sequence of the curricula.  Provides developmentally appropriate  instructional strategies, techniques for  individualizing instruction and assessing  achievement, classroom management skills and  using media. Develops competencies in lesson and  unit planning and instructional delivery. Informs  about professional development opportunities  relative to the various disciplines. Requires  peer teaching and observations of school classrooms. Prerequisites: 206 and admission to the education  program.

title: Professional Practicum
description: This course will consist of placement in the appropriate classroom, full-time, for a period of three weeks, performing as a teacher's aide under the direct supervision of a certified teacher and a college supervisor. Restricted to candidates for the Associate of Arts in Elementary Education degree. This course will not substitute for, nor will it contribute to, the student teaching requirement for student or intern teachers. Prerequisite: Permission of the program director.

Student observes school calendar, not college calendar. Supplemented with the appropriate practicum seminar. Prerequisites: all professional coursework; adviser approval. (12 sem. hours)

title: Topics in Education
description: Courses designed to accommodate the needs of special interest groups, such as caretakers, nursery school teachers or early childhood educators.

title: Fundamentals of Online Teaching
description: This one-credit course is designed to impart the skills necessary to teach online as well as provide opportunities to reflect on teaching practices in general. Topics include (but are not limited to) course organization, accessibility, online pedagogy, teacher expectations, online collaboration, etc.

title: Health/PE Student Teaching Practicum
description: A 12-week clinical experience in local schools for majors who pursue instructional certification for a specific discipline at the secondary level. Student observes school calendar, not college calendar. Supplemented with the appropriate practicum seminar. Prerequisites: all professional coursework; adviser approval. (12 sem. hours)

title: Intern Teaching Practicum I
description: Combined with EDU/SPE5-509, this is a nine-month clinical experience for post-baccalaureate certification candidates in the Teacher Certification Pathways program who are employed in an accredited public or private school. Prerequisites: Intern certificate, adviser approval. (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Intern Teaching Practicum II
description: Combined with EDU-508 , this is nine-month clinical experience for post-baccalaureate certification candidates in the Teacher Certification Pathways Program who are employed in an accredited public or private school. Prerequisites EDU-508 . (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Secondary Methods in Content
description: Each course prepares the certification candidate with the working knowledge required to become an effective teacher in the designated discipline. Examines the scope and sequence of the curricula. Provides developmentally appropriate instructional strategies, techniques for individualizing instruction and assessing achievement, classroom management skills and using media. Develops competencies in lesson and unit planning and instructional delivery. Informs about professional development opportunities relative to the various disciplines. Requires peer teaching and observations of school classrooms. This graduate-level course requires permission of the appropriate Graduate Program Director.

title: Contemporary & Legal Issues in Education
description: This course will explore current and relevant educational issues that possess interconnections with politics, history, ideology, curriculum, and social practice. The primary focus of the course will be on examining the major opposing viewpoints on the issues in American schools of today. Through the integration of knowledge, the course prepares educators to critically examine various schools of philosophical thought and political issues related to education. Analyzing existing programs to improve school effectiveness, and review research-based best practices for effective educational outcomes will be addressed. While none of the topics will be covered in great depth, it is hoped that educators will be inspired to explore in depth topics of personal interest.

title: Early Intervention
description: This course provides an in-depth study of individuals with disabilities from birth to age 5.  Typical and atypical child development theories will be studied.  Methods of instruction/assessment and current research related to Early Intervention will also be examined. Seven (7) hours of field experience in an Early Intervention setting are required.  Course prerequisite/co-requisite: SPE 521 , SPE 526 or SPE 527 .

title: Educational Perspectives - Diverse Socty
description: This course prepares students to deal with the conceptual, theoretical, political and philosophical issues in multicultural education. It has been designed to clarify issues related to pluralistic education, adopt a philosophical position, design and implement effective teaching strategies that reflect ethnic and cultural diversity, and prepare sound guidelines for multicultural programs and practices. In this course, diversity refers to all ways in which people differ, including that of  socioeconomic status, ethnicity, sexual  orientation, religious practices and other differences.  Requires permission of the appropriate Graduate Program Director.

title: Differentiated Instruction
description: This course will explore the principles and elements of differentiated instruction through the study of current and promising practices, as well as relevant research. Graduate students will study instructional and management strategies that address the individual learning needs, strengths, styles and preferences of students within the classroom.

title: Advanced Professional Practicum
description: A period of guided teaching designed for experienced certified teachers who seek an additional instructional certification. Duration and specifications depend upon previous teaching experience and areas of certification. Supervised by a college supervisor and a master teacher in an accredited public or private school. Prerequisites: PA Instructional I or II certification, adviser approval. (6 sem. hours)

title: Environmental Education
description: The course introduces students to fundamental approaches, processes and goals of environmental education (EE). Much of the course will be fieldwork in which students will learn EE curriculum recognized and adopted on a national or statewide basis. Guest speakers, experiential learning and field trips to EE centers are all integral parts of class content. The course uses a variety of EE instructional methods for classroom and field use. The Fulton Farm provides an excellent setting for many of the studies that will be undertaken by the class. Students will learn about the standards that the Pennsylvania Department of Education requires in EE curriculums, how to evaluate lesson programs and how to best deliver material in an engaging fashion. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor. ES

title: Reading Instruction - Elementary (K-6)
description: This course studies the best practices in elementary reading instruction to accommodate the needs of varied learners. S tudy will involve best practices related to literacy development and exploration of the implications of knowledge about the reading/writing process for effective instruction. Topics include: research knowledge about literacy processes, early literacy experiences, the five building blocks in reading instruction, instructional materials, classroom organization for effective literacy instruction, and strategies for instruction/assessment in reading.

title: Teaching English Learners
description: Addresses methods and materials to prepare English language learners to enter and succeed in Pre-K  through 12 mainstream classroom.  Prerequisite: EDU 206 . Waived for degree-seeking graduate education students.

title: Teaching Math in Pre-K - 4 Classrooms
description: Each course prepares the certification candidate with the working knowledge required to become an effective teacher in the designated discipline. Examines the scope and sequence of the curricula. Provides developmentally appropriate instructional strategies, techniques for individualizing instruction and assessing achievement, classroom management skills and using media. Develops competencies in lesson and unit planning and instructional delivery. Informs about professional development opportunities relative to the various disciplines. Requires peer teaching and observations of school classrooms. This graduate-level course requires permission of the appropriate Graduate Program Director.

title: Educational Assessment
description: This course provides pre-service educators with  theory and practice of commonly used assessment  tools including, but not limited to, formal,  informal, authentic and benchmark assessments.  Content focuses on how to effectively interpret  data and how to translate data into academic  achievement. Prerequisite: EDU 206 . Waived for degree-seeking graduate students.

title: Teaching Strategies for Health & PE
description: This course provides the students with the  application of best practices in instruction and  learning related to health and physical education. The students will develop appropriate  lesson planning strategies that relate to  differentiated instruction, assessment, and pedagogy. Students will complete a total of 4  observation hours. Prerequisites: Admission into  the Education Program or ATH-241

title: Reading - Content Area
description: This course provides in-service teachers with an understanding of the essentials of reading processes necessary for students to become proficient readers.  The course will teach the most promising elements of effective literacy programs based in research and/or professional opinion.  Application of the elements to content area texts will help students derive greater comprehension of content area material.

title: Formal/Informal Classroom Assessment
description: This course examines philosophical conceptions of curriculum, educational assessment and principles of planning instruction. Students will construct assessment tools for the evaluation of student progress, teaching effectiveness and curriculum alignment. Additionally, students will investigate state testing, interpretation and the meaningful reporting of results. Prerequisite: Foundation in Statistics, MAT 115 , PSY 115 or permission from director of the M.Ed. program.

title: Best Practices/Effective Teaching
description: The course examines the qualities of effective teachers, research-based instructional strategies and research-based programs for both elementary and secondary educators. Independent projects accommodate specific disciplines and academic needs. Secondary teachers focus on the curricular area in which they are certified, and elementary teachers focus on a curricular area of interest.

title: M.Ed. Internship
description: The candidate must have successfully completed three M.Ed. courses with a grade of "B" or better and must be formally admitted to the M.Ed. program. Since each internship will be designed to meet the needs of the individual candidate, the course description intentionally lacks specificity. Refer to the M.Ed. internship application. Upon recommendation and approval of director, may substitute for a master's-level course.

title: Assessment & Data Analysis in Education
description: Assessment and Data Analysis will focus on the important role assessment and data play in a standards- aligned school. This course will guide you in identifying and communicating your current assessment and data practices and help the leader consider ways to collect and organize data that is more learner-centered.

title: Curriculum & Instruction- School Leaders
description: This course provides exploration and exposure to curriculum and instructional leadership. Students will develop a deeper understanding of the role building leaders play in the development, implementation and evaluation of the curriculum and instruction and what tools and processes are needed to ensure proper development, implementation, and evaluation of curriculum and instruction as it relates to learner success. The primary focus of the course will be to explore the role of a building principal as an agent of change; promoting strategic curriculum and instructional leadership at the building level to improve teaching and learning.

title: Supervision & Professional Growth - Edu
description: This course is practical instruction on how to supervise, coach and evaluate support and professional staff in a school setting. We will focus on methodology, technique, coaching and supportive measures. The course is designed to show the supervision process from informal walkthrough to termination. The topics and assignments will require you to collaborate with leaders within your current system, work with colleagues and grow in your knowledge of Supervision and Evaluation of Staff.

title: Inclusive Education - Special Needs
description: Examines the educational strategies and practices  helpful in meeting the needs of students with  special needs in the regular education classroom.  The course will help teachers read and understand  IEPs (Pa. Chapter 14), Section 504 plans (Pa.  Chapter 15), as well as gifted (Pa. Chapter 16)  requirements and implement them in inclusive  settings.  Co-teaching strategies with special  education teachers in regular education  classrooms will be covered. The course will help  teachers build relationships with and among  students, teach social skills, and provide  positive behavior supports.

title: Financial Resource Mgt in Edu
description: Financial Resource Management is a Masters level course that meets the Pennsylvania Inspired Leadership Standards (PIL) requirements and the School Principal requirements. This course will provide students with an overview of planning and fiscal management models and tools appropriate for public school financial resource management. Specifically, it is designed to help future educational leaders understand the legal and philosophical issues associated with school financial resource management. In addition, this course will assist students in leading educational organizations towards a model of reflective budgeting practices that promote student growth and achievement, develop educator professional practice, and operate in a fiscally-responsible fashion.

title: Middle Level Teaching Methods
description: This graduate-level course, intended for middle level teacher certification candidates, will provide a foundation in proven, research-based teaching strategies. Students will use strategies to develop and teach lessons during the course. They will become knowledgeable with the Pennsylvania Standards Aligned System in order to plan effective lessons. Other topics include classroom management, differentiation, multiple intelligences, assessment, understanding poverty, and literacy skills across the curriculum. Requires permission from the appropriate Graduate Program Director.

title: Technology Integration - Classroom
description: This productive, hands-on course will enable students to develop strategies for the effective integration of computer and multimedia technologies in their classrooms. Basic understanding and practical uses of major software applications and emerging computer and multimedia technologies will be emphasized. Students will develop various projects that use computer and multimedia technologies for curriculum enhancement, communication and record-keeping.

title: LETRS Units 3 and 4
description: Language Essentials for Teachers of Reading and Spelling (LETRS) Units 3 and 4:  LETRS (Language Essentials for Teachers of Reading and Spelling) is an empowering professional development course of study for instructors of reading, spelling, and related language skills. LETRS creates expert teachers of the English language so teachers can, in turn, give their students a deep understanding of how English works.

title: LETRS Units 1 and 2
description: Language Essentials for Teachers of Reading and Spelling (LETRS) Units 1 and 2:  LETRS (Language Essentials for Teachers of Reading and Spelling) is an empowering professional development course of study for instructors of reading, spelling, and related language skills. LETRS creates expert teachers of the English language so teachers can, in turn, give their students a deep understanding of how English works.

title: Topics in Education
description: Courses that consider recent research and pedagogy in an area of secondary teacher certification.

title: Letrs Units 7 and 8
description: Language Essentials for Teachers of Reading and Spelling (LETRS) Units 7 and 8:  LETRS (Language Essentials for Teachers of Reading and Spelling) is an empowering professional development course of study for instructors of reading, spelling, and related language skills. LETRS creates expert teachers of the English language so teachers can, in turn, give their students a deep understanding of how English works.

title: Leadership & Strategic Design in Edu
description: Leadership and Strategic Design is the first of a series of leadership courses that could lead to a Pennsylvania principal certification. The course is designed to introduce the students to the Pennsylvania Leadership Standards. This program delves into fundamental leadership skills, emphasizing strategic planning and design as essential tools for effective school management. Participants will explore leadership theories, develop their decision-making and problem-solving abilities, and gain insights into fostering a positive school culture. The students will explore what it means to be a leader in leadership frameworks and create a culture for continuous improvement.

title: Viable Learning Outcomes
description: Organized in progressions of learning, the Learning Outcomes provide the focus for instruction and incorporate a variety of learning formats (e.g. seminars, labs, online, etc.) with explicit consideration of how an outcome is best learned.  The learners will research and discuss; content, complex reasoning, and habits of mind outcomes.

title: Motivating Learning Opportunities
description: Learners will investigate learner agency, growth versus fixed mindset, engagement vs. compliance, varied learning, formative feedback and concepts of challenge, pace interest, and style of learning. The learner will be required to engage in an embedded activity demonstrating an understanding of the creation of ideal learning experiences.

title: Varied Evidences of Learning
description: The learner will examine how a diversity of data gleaned from the learning experiences provides a sound basis for adjusting instructional opportunities. Also, how the data offers constructive feedback for learners about their progress. Mastery and performance-based learning and multiple ways to demonstrate learning will be investigated.

title: Quality Instruction
description: Learners will embrace a shared pedagogy which demonstrates a sound understanding of learning.  Working individually and collaboratively, they design, implement, reflect on, and adjust their instructional strategies.  They will learn more about their craft, network with others, and incorporate technology as an integral tool for the instructional process.

title: Transformation Technologies
description: The learner will consider how technology systems provide the management of student information, learning outcomes and opportunities, and the management of complex schedules. They will explore online learning and the use of instructional tools to leverage learning.  The learners will study the current and future of transformational technologies.

title: Leading District-Wide Contin Improvement
description: The Leading District-wide Continuous Improvement course is based upon the research and best practices associated with leadership to develop organizational capacity for continuous improvement of student achievement. 40 Pennsylvania Inspired Leadership Hours(PIL) will be awarded upon completion

title: LETRS Units 5 and 6
description: Language Essentials for Teachers of Reading and Spelling (LETRS) Units 5 and 6:  LETRS (Language Essentials for Teachers of Reading and Spelling) is an empowering professional development course of study for instructors of reading, spelling, and related language skills. LETRS creates expert teachers of the English language so teachers can, in turn, give their students a deep understanding of how English works.

title: Implementing ISTE Standards - Classroom
description: The ISTE Certification for Educators is a competency-based, device-neutral certification designed for ambitious educators who want to use technology to catalyze learning. Designed to build competency with the ISTE Standards for Educators, the certification combines face-to-face and online pedagogy-focused professional development with a competency-based evaluation.  Note: Participant will have 6-months to develop and share a portfolio to ISTE in order to earn ISTE Certification.  Permission Only

title: Skilled Leadership in Education
description: Learners will investigate why and how skilled Leadership at all levels is essential to creating a learner- centered approach to customizing learning. The course emphasizes the importance of empowering others and collaboration. The learners will understand the five domains of Leadership: authentic, visionary, cultural, quality, and service.

title: Shifting the Mindset
description: The learner will be guided through four units that include the Mass Customized Learning Vision, Strategic Design, Research on Motivation and Engagement, and Paradigm Shifts. The learners will understand and be able to articulate why a shifting mindset is critical to better-preparing learners for their future. Prerequisite for other MMCL courses or may be taken concurrently.

title: Leadership Under Fire: Gettysburg
description: Students examine strategies utilized in the Battle of Gettysburg as a means of examining leadership principals relevant in today's education, business, and healthcare arenas. A three-to-four-day battlefield visit supports the online coursework. Prerequisite:  Must hold a current PA Instructional/Supervisory/Specialist/Administrativ e Certification.

title: Leadership Lessons: Flight 93
description: Lessons from Flight 93 affords participants the opportunity to look at critical events associated with that fateful flight and examine them for the lessons on organizational leadership that they so richly illustrate. Attention is given to attributes of strong leaders, strategic leadership initiatives, and tactical actions to address strategies, meet goals and fulfill missions.

title: Principal Internship I
description: The internship experience examines the skills required of a school principal to become the instructional leader and prepares them to enact these skills within the contemporary political, social, economic, and cultural context in which an educational system operates. The course requires individuals to practice these leadership skills and reflect on their impact. This course will provide practical experiences principals are faced with on a day-to-day basis. Interns will apply theories and activities developed throughout the course work in principal certificate preparation program.

title: Accomplished Teach: National Bd Cert I
description: Examines the most up to date pedagogy in education. Key features in the course include areas such as advanced knowledge in the subject area, management and assessment of student learning, reflective practices, and collaboration and partnerships with key stakeholders. Each student will have the opportunity to begin the National Board Certification process through Components 2 and 3. This course is in partial fulfillment of the National Board Certification process.  Requires permission of the appropriate Graduate Program Director.

title: Customized Structures
description: The learner will be confronted with the barriers to a Mass Customized Learning system.  They will engage in a comprehensive study of how the current industrial age educational structures of grade levels, schedules, and other institutional weight-bearing walls must be removed.

title: Educational Research & Design
description: This course will examine exemplary literature and research projects that represent examples of the systematic collection, evaluation, presentation and interpretation of research data in education. Various research designs and methodologies will be explored, emphasizing action research done in educational contexts. Student research projects will reflect a specific educational research style, such as experimental, correlational, survey, grounded theory, ethnography, narrative or mixed methods. Students will be guided in the selection of an appropriate master's project topic, in review and critique of relevant literature, and development of a design proposal for action research to be conducted in her/his own classroom.

title: Master's Project
description: This course bridges the void between theory and practice. The students will transfer their findings from EDU 598 to create a plan of action for execution. Strategies for overcoming obstacles to implementation are vital elements discussed in this course. The action plan will include a timeline, data collected, and how the data is displayed and analyzed. As a culminating project, each student will present their plan and augmented learning as a result of carrying out the proposed plan. Prerequisite:  EDU/ SPE-598 .

title: Tech-Infused Classroom
description: Explores how the specific technology available to each teacher can be integrated into students' daily learning activities to create a more collaborative learning environment, to improve creativity and critical thinking skills, and to customize and differentiate learning and assessment to meet individual student needs.

title: Tech Integration
description: Focuses on foundational elements of technology integration, including understanding the SAMR Model of Technology Integration and its application to lesson development, using digital tools for enhanced communication with parents and students, building a personal learning network, curating resources for direct instruction, using collaboration tools, using an online "home base," and differentiating instruction using current tools.

title: Blended & Flipped Learning Environments
description: Explores blended learning models and the implementation of such models in the classroom. Focuses on technological and pedagogical implementation, including resource identification and logistical and structural development of blended classroom environments to support student access and academic integrity. Pre-requisite: EDU 903

title: Reflective Teach: National Bd Cert II
description: Examines the most up to date pedagogy in education. Key features in the course include areas such as dedication to student learning, advanced knowledge in the subject area, management and assessment of student learning, reflective practices, and collaboration and partnerships with key stakeholders. Each student will have the opportunity to complete the National Board Certification process through Components 1 and 4. This course is the completion of the National Board Certification process. Prerequisite: EDU-595 .

title: The Flipped Classroom
description: Explores the pedagogical structure and philosophy of the flipped classroom.  Investigates the tools, content managements and classroom management skills necessary to teach in a flipped environment.

title: Data Collection & Visualizatn
description: Explores the connection between education standards and data collection, analysis, and visualization. Develop strategies for guiding students in the use of technology to collect and visualize data in order to more effectively teach higher-order thinking skills and digital-oriented inquiry activities.

title: Creativity & Innovation
description: Explores the philosophy and current research analyzing human innovation and creative processes, especially in an educational setting. Focuses on the use of technology to develop innovative and creative practices and to promote innovation and creativity in concert with state and national standards in the classroom.

title: Instructional Technology Internship
description: The learner conducts learning technology-related  activities with a self identified organization to  apply concepts and skills previously explored  during coursework. This experiential course also  provides an opportunity to demonstrate and  further develop core learning competencies, i.e.  critical thinking, communication, teamwork and  collaboration, information literacy, ethical  decision making, and global engagement.

title: The Mindful Classroom
description: Mindfulness implies attention toward and care for the ideas, beliefs, and feelings of others. This course demonstrates the history of mindfulness in the classroom environment and explores techniques designed to aid defusing and navigating social, cultural, and emotional stressors that can occur in a classroom setting.

title: Tech for Brain-Based Learning
description: Focuses on research from the Learning Sciences to examine concepts of teaching and learning. Participants will be asked to consider the following: how does learning occur? And, how should educators design instruction to support optimal learning opportunities? Course activities and assignments will examine brain-based learning and brain-targeted teaching while exploring strategies for instruction, assessment, and learner engagement using a variety of technologies.

title: Understanding Today's Students
description: This course delves into the powerful societal and technological influences contributing to the development and perspectives of today's students. As the influences of postmodernism shift our world from rational to experiential problem solving, technology moves us from linear to complex thinking, and collaboration replaces individual responsiblity, students are learning in a world that has changed dramatically.

title: Principal Internship II
description: The internship experience examines the skills required of a school principal to become the instructional leader and prepares them to enact these skills within the contemporary political, social, economic, and cultural context in which an educational system operates. The course requires individuals to practice these leadership skills and reflect on their impact. This course will provide practical experiences principals are faced with on a day-to-day basis. Interns will apply theories and activities developed throughout the course work in principal certificate preparation program.

title: Thinking Critically About Ed Tech
description: Thinking Critically About EdTech will challenge  participants to analyze the relationships between  the real or perceived effects of educational  technologies and research-based best practices or  the classroom. What is the intersection between  what educational technologies can do and what  research shows can help students in achieving our  classroom objectives?

title: Education in a 1:1 Environment
description: Focuses on transforming instruction for a 1:1 class environment. Strategies will be presented for developing daily instructional plans that embed local-level technology into the curriculum content and ISTE standards.

title: Authentic Connections-Project-Based Lrng
description: Develop real-world connections to your content  through project-based learning. The course  reaches beyond the typical checklist approach to  PBL and prepares teachers to implement relevant  challenges that take students to deeper levels of learning.

title: Humanizing Online Learning
description: This course examines how to use your teacher presence in virtual instructional environments to humanize online learning.  By fostering academic and social connections, teachers can build empathy, personalize learning and increase student motivation.

title: Mindful Research in Action
description: Mindful practices are rapidly finding their way into today's K-12 classrooms globally, but research is still in its infancy. This course offers educators a mechanism for creating a "mindful classroom action research project" that will expand practice while adding to the current scholarship on the topic.  Prerequisite: EDU-908GR

title: Digital Citizenship in the Classroom
description: Explore the nine elements of Digital Citizenship (effective and responsible use of the internet and digital devices) and incorporate the ISTE standards within K-12 classrooms. Using various digital creation sites, podcasts, and research, participants will create various usable tools to enhance their specific content area, enabling learners to feel comfortable in the integration of digital citizenship within their classroom.

title: Learning Management Systems
description: Investigate the function, benefits and challenges of an LMS in today's learning environment. Evaluate the best components to choose in building an online-supported course, and identify best practices in managing digital content, activities and collaboration using an LMS. Analyze the current shift in personal instructional practices and student engagement.

title: Ownership of Learning
description: Focuses on the concept of personalized learning, encouraging teachers to take on the role of facilitator while their learners develop the strategies, skills, and dispositions needed to become invested in their learning. This course is designed for educators across grade levels and disciplines. Participants should be open to shifts in pedagogy, leveraging technology, and cultivating opportunities for learners.

title: Effective Video Production
description: Examines the ways in which video content can effectively deliver instruction to the classroom. Through shooting original content, editing original and existing material, and production, teachers will investigate ways to deliver intended messages in multiple content areas. Connects with the ITSE standards for student learning and creativity.

title: Design Thinking in Education
description: This course will immerse educators in a design  thinking process. Participants will research and  develop a project that engages students in a  human-centered, problem solving process, specific  to their area of study.

title: Coding and Computational Thinking
description: Develop an understanding and application of computational thinking concepts for educators. Evaluates offline grade-level appropriate computational thinking activities for students, presents instructional strategies that foster student perseverance, and investigates the basics of coding author applications. Participants will develop an implementation plan for teaching computational thinking and coding.

title: Google Tools for Educators
description: Examines the Google Workspace of educational tools (formerly G Suite and Google Apps for Education) and explores how these technical tools can aid teachers in developing digital learning resources and methodologies. Students will learn to effectively implement G Workspace tools in classroom instruction and activities to facilitate collaborative, independent, and differentiated learning.

title: Gamification in Education
description: Gamification is the use of game design principles and elements in non-gaming environments. This course explores the elements and psychology of gamification as applied to the classroom environment, focusing on the application of these systems, gamification tech tools available for use, and developing gamification systems for classroom use.

title: Digital Portfolios
description: Explores the theory, implementation and use of digital portfolios in the classroom environment as ways to create, maintain, and share knowledge. Provides instructions on digital portfolio technology and tools as well as pedagogical applications for digital portfolios in the classroom.

title: Growth Mindset in the Classroom
description: This course will research growth mindset within the classroom. Participants will explore technology tools to support growth mindset in instruction. It provides practice implementing and utilizing a variety of tools, as well as,  how to monitor and modify instruction for student success with growth mindset at the forefront.

title: Tech to Motivate All Students
description: This course will explore major theories of motivation that can be implemented in the classroom to encourage and promote student learning. Learners will examine various approaches to encourage students' motivational mind-set through the support of technology integration. The techniques and strategies learned will help motivate even the most reluctant student.

title: Social Media for Educators
description: Explores the use of social media as an educational tool to enhance the learning environment and to create connected learning communities. Examines social media platforms such as Twitter, Diigo, LinkedIn, Google+, and more. Examine pros and cons for various social media platforms.

title: Emergent Educational Technologies
description: Survey emerging technologies that will have applications in the classroom environment. Students will study educational technology integration and the "ed tech" business model and how implementing new technologies will impact the classroom.

title: School Library-Blended & Online Learning
description: Libraries play a vital role in learner achievement. Whether schools are brick and mortar, blended, or entirely online, library services are critical. This course explores all aspects of contemporary school libraries, from collaborative digital instruction and curation to reading promotion and meaningful making to support inquiry.

title: Digital Storytelling
description: Researches and defines digital storytelling and its application to the classroom. Presents technology tools for compliance with and integration of PA Core Technology Standards into curriculum; provides instruction and practice in creation of digital storytelling projects.

title: Game-Based Pedagogy
description: Explores the use of videogames in the classroom environment. Examines pedagogical theory supporting the use of game-based learning, especially in developing critical thinking and creativity skills. Participants will analyze, use, and build games as part of the class.

title: Infrastructure & Tech Planning
description: Creating a sound infrastructure for your school or organization is vital to the successful integration of technology and daily workflow for employees. Course takers will explore best practices and current industry standards for developing and maintaining their infrastructure, financial and human resources, and software applications. Participants will develop leadership techniques for working with all levels of the educational community to manage and administer instructional technology programs -- all while adhering to policy and maintaining a secure environment for their staff and students.

title: Steam-Azing: Interdiscipl Lens for Instr
description: Explore the connection between science,  technology, engineering, art and math. STEAM  develops a set of thinking, reasoning, teamwork,  investigative, and creative skills that students  can use in all areas of their lives. Participants will learn about STEAM initiatives, investigate interdisciplinary connections, evaluate resources and create lessons.

title: Multimedia Classrooms
description: Learn best practices in efficiently creating and manipulating audio, video and image content for the classroom. Investigate and evaluate options that provide cutting-edge technical and educational solutions to educators, empowering them and their students to develop authentic, engaging, and relevant digital learning.

title: Instructional Media - Elementary Classrm
description: Instructional Media Integration in the Elementary Setting will prepare educators and coaches to integrate technology in a balanced, but purposeful, way to promote elementary student creativity over consumption. Course participants will learn from innovative elementary educators and explore best practices in integration with the earliest of learners.

title: Technology Coaching in the Classroom
description: Technology Coaching in the Classroom will enhance the learner's understanding of supporting educators effectively through developing and implementing a Leadership Plan. Learners will explore relevant frameworks, strategies, tools, and resources used to advance their leadership efforts.

title: Common Core Elementary Mathematics
description: Facilitates an understanding of how the mathematical practices and standards of the National Common Core and PA Core impact the design and delivery of math instruction at the elementary level. Investigates the use of formative and summative assignments to personalize instruction and deliver meaningful interventions.

title: Cultivating a Community of Readers
description: Cultivate a community of readers within your K-6 classroom. Connect with local and global reading initiatives while exploring the research, practices, and resources available to grow students into lifelong, passionate readers.

title: Digital Information & Media Fluency
description: This course provides strategies for managing digital information, which is a requisite skill for educators of all kinds. Recognizing information needs, locating appropriate resources, and managing and using digital information tools will be presented to help teachers, in turn, fully nurture these skills in students.

title: Conflicts in Social Studies Teaching
description: This course will guide educators in the appropriate and effective use of academic controversy, common perception, and "myth" in the social studies classroom. Participants will be guided in the development of higher order thinking and discovery-based units of instruction while learning how to both use and challenge commonly held perceptions in history, geography, and sociology.

title: Effective Formative Assessmnt & Technolg
description: This course will focus on effective formative assessment pedagogy, and the integration of appropriate instructional technology tools to facilitate successful formative assessment in the classroom. The knowledge gained from this course will allow each student to be an instructional leader in his or her school district.

title: American History Teaching Tools
description: Examines digital and non-digital methods of organizing and presenting American history, focusing on content organizational strategies, Common Core standards, and essential question development.

title: 21st Century Holocaust Ed.
description: This course demonstrates key topics and techniques needed for teaching the Holocaust in the 21st century. Course content will explore the challenges of instructing this subject during a time when first-hand accounts are limited. Students will focus on the effective use of primary sources available through free online content.

title: Tech in Secondary English
description: Examines the use and function of integrative technology in the Secondary English classroom. Explores current trends in educational technology and focuses on the implementation of technology in alignment with Common Core standards for Secondary English.

title: Tech in World Language Studies
description: This course helps teachers integrate technology into the World Language classroom. Technology strategies will be presented that spark relevant and engaging activity ideas, with emphasis on using technology to enhance activities that help students improve ACTFL proficiency levels. Technology-based assessment options will also be explored.

title: Engaging Writers in All Content Areas
description: Explores strategies for teaching writing across all content areas for grades 4-12, emphasizing key writing skills in the writing process model and technology tools that can be used to develop, practice, and sustain those skills.

title: Health Education for 21st Century
description: Examines the use and function of integrative technology in the middle and high school health and physical education classroom. Explores current trends in educational technology and focuses on the implementation of technology in alignment with the standards set forth by the Best Practices document published by SHAPE America.

title: Spec Topics - Educational Tech
description: Special topics courses offered on an irregular basis to enhance the curriculum in the Educational Technology area.

title: Doing History Digitally
description: Doing history is a social studies pedagogical method that asks students not only to think about history, but to replicate the conditions and means under which histories are made. This class examines interactive tech tools that will aid social science teachers in "doing history" and understanding the processes that make history happen.

title: Vocab Comprehension Through Technology
description: This course will research, define, and teach educators how to increase their students' success by building vocabulary and understanding of critical words and concepts across content areas.  Participants will explore, practice, monitor and modify technology and tools to support students in building their vocabulary and comprehension skills in a variety of content areas.

title: Tech Integration in Early Childhood
description: Participants in this hands-on course will learn about developmentally appropriate ways to integrate tech with children in PreK-2 classrooms. Exploration of interactive multimedia, age-appropriate apps, tools for communicating with families, and tech integration in creative ways for young learners are key course components.

title: Innovating With 3D Design & Printing
description: 3D design and printing has a place across all content areas and grade levels.  Using various apps, tools, and design platforms, learners will develop skills needed to fully and effectively integrate this methodology into their classroom setting for their own use and student-directed activities.  Course prerequisite: Conceptual understanding of 3 dimensional design using the x, y, and z axis terminology.

title: Ipads in Education
description: Explores the broad applications and utility of the iPad as a classroom teaching device. Practical instruction provides strategies and skills for effective classroom integration.

title: Tech in ELL
description: Addresses the use of technology as a tool to enhance language acquisition associated with English Language Learners/ing (ELL). Presents background knowledge of the learner, technology tools to assist in achieving academic and social goals, and how to design assessment and adapt activities to match student age and cognitive levels.

title: Tech in Secondary Math
description: This course will analyze the benefits of available technology for secondary math instruction and assessment. Formative and summative assessment tools will be compared. Online remediation and practice in the mathematics classroom will be investigated and critiqued.

title: Advanced Google Integration
description: Google Workspace is so much more than just sharing and real-time editing. Learners in this course will discover new and innovative ways to take some Google Workspace tools to the next level through the analysis and application of the 4C's of learning.

title: Ipads for Special Education
description: Examines the design of instructional strategies and creation of learning networks to enhance the development of digital skills for learners with special needs. Explores resources for differentiating instruction and monitoring learner progress, with a view toward optimizing tablet applications that support learners with special needs.

title: Chromebooks in Education
description: Explores the benefits and limitations of the Chromebook and facilitates the development of classroom management techniques designed for the 1:1 classroom. Investigates topics applicable to a Chromebook-based classroom environment, including project-based learning, cloud computing, the flipped classroom, Web 2.0 solutions, G Suite (formerly GAFE), content management systems and screencasts.

title: Virtual & Augmented Reality Tech Classrm
description: Explores to use of virtual and augmented reality in the classroom to enhance curriculum. Practical instruction focuses on the adaptation of virtual reality technologies as well as teacher and student creation of virtual reality experiences.

title: Adaptive Technology for Students
description: This course will research and define Adaptive  Technology for Struggling Students and its  application to the classroom. It directs  participants in correlating adaptive technology  tools/apps/extensions for compliance with student  plans and integration of standards into  curriculum. Provides instruction and practice in  creation of projects using adaptive technology  tools/apps/extensions.

title: Technology & Students With Autism
description: This course will research appropriate technology for students with Autism in the classroom. It directs participants in exploring technology tools that are the most suitable to support students with Autism. It provides instruction and practice utilizing a variety of tools, and how to monitor and modify instruction for student success.

title: Strengthening SEL Using Tech
description: A consistent focus on Social and Emotional Learning (SEL) enhances teaching and learning while supporting the overall well-being of students and educators in the classroom. This course focuses on using technology to maximize the benefits associated with SEL to improve students' overall connection with school and social emotional growth.

title: Future-Ready Classroom
description: Explores methods educators can implement to infuse their classrooms and curriculum with "future ready" skills as a focus. Practical instruction equips teachers with the technology tools necessary to help students with the development of skills they need to succeed in college and career fields.

title: Enhancing Literature With Technology
description: Enhance the literature that is used in the classroom through the use of makerspace and design challenges, coding and robotics, digital resources, QR codes and Augmented Reality.  A common theme throughout the course is effective integration of the 4C's: Creativity, Collaboration, Communication and Critical Thinking.

title: iPad Infused Classroom
description: Building off the practical skills developed in iPads in Education, this course focuses on the collaborative application of iPad tools and apps to enhance delivery of instruction and curriculum, including but not limited to screencasting, greenscreens, timeline and mind-mapping, and iTunesU.  Pre-requisite: EDU 960 or instructor approval.

title: Advanced Integration of Chromebooks
description: This course builds upon the knowledge and experiences of everyday Chromebook use. Learners will explore advanced features of Chromebooks and will use Google Workspace for Education to create instructional content including tech tools, strategies, and best practices for using Chromebooks in the classroom including hybrid and remote learning.  Prerequisites:  EDU-963 or instructor approval.

title: Managing a Technology Equipped Classroom
description: Classroom management is something that all teachers feel they can improve on, especially when it comes to student use of technology. Technology can help teachers enhance their classroom management, but can also cause unwanted distractions. Topics covered in this course will include strategies, tools, and expectations of managing a technology driven classroom.

title: Immersive Experiences in Education
description: Explore and experiment with the many possibilities for virtual reality (VR) integration in educational settings with an easy-to-use personal VR device. Hands-on experiences include VR apps, games, virtual communication and an evaluation of the advantages of and barriers to VR educational use in the present and near future.

title: Google Earth in the Classroom
description: Presents innovative strategies for the alignment  of Google Earth activities with grade-appropriate  ELA, STEM and ISTE standards. Engages in research  and discussion about how Google Earth can serve  as a cross-curricular teaching tool. Investigates  both the fundamentals of Google Earth, including  its creation, navigation, street view and sky  views, as well as in depth exploration of custom  features.

title: Navigating Future Learning through AI
description: Explores how the specific technology available to each teacher can be integrated into students' daily learning activities to create a more collaborative learning environment, to improve creativity and critical thinking skills, and to customize and differentiate learning and assessment to meet individual student needs.

title: Microsoft 365 Tools
description: Explores the Microsoft 365 tools available to educators for collaborative learning and personalized instruction in both classroom and online settings. Explore information sharing and collaboration strategies using OneDrive, Microsoft 365 Online, OneNote, Sway, Stream, Flip Grid, and Teams.

title: Learning Management Systems
description: Investigate the function, benefits and challenges of an LMS in today's learning environment.  Evaluate the best components to choose in  building an online-supported course, and identify best practices in managing digital content, activities and collaboration using an LMS. Analyze the current shift in personal instructional practices and student engagement.

title: Equine Fac Therapeutics I
