description: Introductory course addressing therapeutic program  development, program personnel and team-building,  qualifications and training; overview of  disabilities commonly encountered in the  therapeutic program; learning styles;  record-keeping and use of the horse in  equine-assisted activities. Prerequisite: EQS  116.

title: Teaching II
description: Under the supervision of the professor, students will teach riders with disabilities, perform the duties of alternative team members and provide critical evaluations of their peers. Prerequisite: EFT 331 .

title: Equine Biomechanics/Kinesiology
description: A study of equine movement. A thorough understanding of how a horse moves is used to improve the posture, balance and mobility of both horse and rider. To gain an understanding of the horse's movement in real time, students will complete a research project using video analysis, still photography and other media applications. Prerequisite: EQS 116 .

title: Ground Training the Horse
description: Student trainers will assess their assigned horses based upon use intended and behavior; develop an individualized training plan; and use classical and modern ground training methods to improve the performance of their assigned horses in the areas of: leading, behaviors, group dynamics, balance and movement, obstacles, "bomb proofing" and acceptance of equipment. Prerequisite: EQS 110 .

title: EAP: Combined Language Skills
description: A course designed for international students who wish to work on multiple aspects of language and culture while living in the United States. It will cover listening, speaking, reading, writing, academic skills and American culture. Offered fall semester. CC

title: EAP: Academic Research Writing
description: A research and composition course designed for non-native English speaking students. Emphasizes distinct areas of planning, conducting and writing for an independent research project including topic selection, thesis generation, research skills, primary and secondary source selection, validation of sources, draft and detailed outlining, literature reviews, interviewing techniques, questionnaire generation, introductory and concluding sections, effective use of work by other authors and researchers, organized presentation of findings, and well-supported argumentation and analysis. Offered spring senester. WI

title: Financial Literacy With Technology
description: The course equips educators with essential financial concepts and teaching strategies. The course covers the PA Model Framework for Financial Literacy, including Money Management, Banking, Investing & Retirement, Taxes, Credit, Paying for College, Insurance, and Careers. Emphasizes practical application of technology resources while preparing teachers to empower students to become financially independent.

title: Leveraging Ai for Effective Teaching
description: Students examine practical strategies to integrate AI tools that enhance student engagement, streamline management, and support personalized learning. This includes exploration of AI-driven resources for content creation, assessment, and support, with hands-on experience to transform their teaching practices, as well as an introduction to the ethical considerations students need to know before using AI.

title: College Writing
description: Emphasizes principles and practice of effective writing, reflection on composition as a process, thinking and organizational skills at the college level and preparation for academic research papers.

title: EAP: English Composition
description: Designed for nonnative English-speaking students, the course explores English for academic purposes with particular attention to the complexity of contrasting argumentation styles found in comparative rhetoric. Students focus on micro and macro elements of academic writing aimed at a native-speaking audience. These elements will include but - are not limited to - syntax, semantics, organization, rhetoric and argumentation. To ensure that students know how to use their academic writing for research purposes, the course introduces related skills such as drafting articles/ literature reviews, research skills, paper organization, outlining, note-taking, summarizing, paraphrasing and citation. Normally taken concurrently with ENG 103 . Students who complete ENG 104 must also complete ENG 106 in the following spring semester. Prerequisite: English Placement Examination.

title: Teaching I
description: Students will apply the skills and concepts from EFT courses in the following: intake and evaluation of riders; goal setting, task analysis, skill progression and lesson planning based on rider ability and age; assignment of  appropriate horse and equipment; training and oversight of the team; differentiation between the therapeutic riding instructor and medical or mental health professionals; role play; and mentored teaching of students having physical, emotional or mental disabilities. Prerequisites: EFT 202 , EQS 240 .

title: Writing About Literary Genres
description: Writing-intensive introduction to the histories, conventions, methods and pleasures of particular literary genres. Focus varies from year to year but could include poetry, drama, fiction, autobiography, popular literature and combinations thereof. The course demonstrates the interdependency of writing, learning and interpretation. Prerequisite: Foundations course in English. LIT, WI

title: Equine Facilitated Therapeutics II
description: Theories learned in EFT 201 are put into practical application through development of evaluation and  intake techniques; in-depth presentation of the  physical, emotional and mental disabilities  commonly seen in therapeutic programs; goal  setting and lesson planning; appropriate horse use; the role of the leader and sidewalkers during the mount, lesson and dismount, safety procedures and techniques, and mentored teaching.  Prerequisite: EFT 201 .

title: Comic-Book Memoirs of Illness
description: An introduction to visual storytelling through study of graphic memoirs of illness, disability, and caregiving. Texts feature illnesses ranging from Alzheimer's and cancer to anorexia and bipolar disorder, with a focus on marginalized voices often excluded from traditional medical discourse. Prerequisite: Foundations course in English.  LIT, ART, and WI.

title: Women Writers
description: Examines themes, techniques, goals and historical contexts of women's literary production. WS, LIT, WI

title: Advanced Exposition
description: Development of expository writing skills at an advanced level across academic curriculum and/or disciplines. Applications of advanced rhetorical techniques to several expository genres. WI

title: American Literature I
description: The intellectual and cultural milieu of the American "New World" as revealed in the prose and poetry-including that of Native Americans and African-Americans- produced between the early 1600s and the mid-1800s and culminating in distinctive American literature. HWC, LIT, WI

title: Major Writings European Tradition II
description: Students read authors whose works have strongly influenced modernity: e.g.,  Wollstonecraft, Austen, Flaubert, Dostoyevsky, Kafka, Woolf and Freud. Discussion topics include the romanticism-realism conflict, the critique of patriarchy and the emergence of the unconscious. HWC, LIT, WI

title: Creative Writing
description: Introduces students to techniques and skills in writing the four major genres of creative writing: poetry, fiction, drama and creative nonfiction. Class consists of craft exercises,critical renderings of the work of others, self-reflective analysis and completion of four distinct projects in portfolio format. ART,WI

title: Major Writings European Tradition I
description: Students read authors whose works have strongly influenced Western culture: e.g., Sappho, Homer, Sophocles, Plato, Aristotle, Dante, Boccaccio and Voltaire. Discussion topics include the history of ideas, the construction/critique of a canonical tradition and the self in society. FWC, LIT, WI

title: Young Adult Literature
description: Survey of literature written for and about young adults. Critical reading of young adult literature by genre (e.g., realistic fiction, modern fantasy, historical fiction, nonfiction and poetry) focusing on authors who have made significant contributions to the field, as well as on criteria for selecting works of proven literary merit for use in a secondary school classroom. LIT, WI

title: Writing About Literature and Environment
description: Writing-intensive approach to nature writing. Emphasizes composition, critical thinking, literary analysis, and reflection on the natural world. ES, LIT, WI

title: Media Writing
description: Development of journalistic skills required by newspaper writing, magazine writing, and writing for online source content, including principles of interviewing, reporting, and editing. WI

title: Film Analysis and History
description: Students analyze film using the elements of mise en scÃ¨ne. Technical discussions of film production and reception are supported by in-class screening of movies by such directors as Keaton, Welles, Hawks, Ford, Hitchcock, De Sica, Kazan, Lee and Scott. Discussion topics include film history, genres and criticism. ART, WI

title: African-American Literature
description: Introduces the diversity and intertextuality of the African-American literary tradition. Includes major writers, periods and  genres. Prerequisite:  Completion of English Foundations Course. CD, LIT, WI

title: British Literature 1200-1700
description: An intense examination of the literature and especially the changes in the forms of national literature of Britain from 1200 to 1700. Authors read may include the Gawain-poet, William Langland, Julian of Norwich, Christopher Marlowe, Mary Wroth, John Donne, and Ben Jonson. FWC, LIT, WI

title: American Literature II
description: The development of American literature from the later 19th century through 1945. Emphasizes the intellectual, social and aesthetic concerns that have shaped American fiction, poetry and drama. HWC, LIT, WI

title: The English Novel
description: The genre is examined through critical reading of novels from the 19th and 20th centuries, including works by Austen, Dickens, Stevenson, Ford, Conrad and Woolf. LIT, HWC, WI

title: Technical Writing
description: Examination of and practice in technical writing. Emphasis on developing effective style after analysis of purpose and audience. Analyses and assignments in formulating definitions, mechanical and process descriptions, reports, proposals and technical presentations. WI

title: Practicum: Social Media and Web Content
description: Practicum-based course where students work for campus communications offices, creating content for social media, websites, and other marketing materials. Students advance their knowledge of online content creation, monitoring and filtering online content, and brand creation. Pre-req: ENG 201 and junior standing

title: Practicum: News and Feature Writing
description: Practicum-based course where students work for the campus newspaper The Billboard and the Wilson Magazine. Students advance their knowledge of journalistic writing, copyediting, design and layout for print publications. Pre-req: ENG 201 and junior standing

title: American Literature Since 1945
description: New directions in poetry, drama, fiction and literary innovations in the context of  international conflict, feminism,  environmentalism, civil rights and gay rights. Prerequisite:  Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course.  CD, LIT, WI

title: Advanced Creative Writing: Poetry
description: Intensive study and practice in the creation of poetic writing, including detailed craft and skill-building instruction, written and oral peer-critiquing, self-reflective analysis, regular examination of contemporary theoretical trends in creative writing poetry and the completion of several significant projects. Prerequisite: ENG 220 . ART

title: Creative Non-Fiction/Literary Journalism
description: Study and practice in the development of creative non-fiction and literary journalism, including detailed craft and skill-building instruction, written and oral peer-critiquing, self-reflective analysis, regular examination of contemporary theoretical trends in the fields, and the completion of several significant projects. Prerequisite for 300-level course: ENG 220 or ENG 201 or HSC 222 . ART, WI

title: Multicultural Environmental Lit
description: This course examines inequality in access to natural resources and the wealth they produce, in exposure to toxins, and in participation in environmental decision-making as represented through literature by Native American, African-American, Latino, and Asian-American authors. Prerequisite:  Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course. ES, CD, WI

title: The English Language
description: Examination of the structural, historical, and ethical expansion of the English language. Course subjects include phonetics, morphemics, and syntax; the anthropological and transhistorical development of the language; and the relationship of the language to society, economics, and politics. ETH

title: Film Genres and Genders
description: Historical study of Hollywood film genres and their relation to dichotomous gender. Emphasis on the genres of screwball comedy, maternal melodrama and film noir. Representative directors may include Hawks, Sturgess, Rapper, Dmytryk,Ray, Hitchcock and Aldrich. Prerequisite: Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course. ART, WS, WI

title: African-American Literature
description: Introduces the diversity and intertextuality of the African-American literary tradition. Includes major writers, periods and  genres. Prerequisite:  Completion of English of Communications course at the 200 level. Prereqisite:  Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course. CD, LIT, WI

title: Topics in English
description: Topics course in English. LIT, WI

title: Shakespeare's Histories and Comedies
description: Critical reading of representative histories and comedies, including a strong theoretical approach to the texts. Prerequisite:  Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course. LIT, HWC, WI

title: Shakespeare's Romances and Tragedies
description: Critical reading of representative romances, tragedies, and genres, including a thorough introduction to Shakespeare and his sonnets. Prerequisite:  Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course. LIT,WI

title: Chaucer
description: Detailed analysis and study of The Canterbury Tales. Includes close, critical readings of the original Middle English text and examination of the social, political and cultural climate in which Chaucer composed. Prerequisite: Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course. FWC, LIT

title: Language and Culture
description: This course focuses on the interconnected  relationship of language to culture, including  exploring issues of cultural identity,  acculturation and resistance, ethnocentrism,  medial diffusion, and language study.    Prerequisite:  Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course.  CD

title: Literary and Cultural Interpretation
description: In-depth study of developments in the history of interpretation. Representative methods include hermeneutics, feminism, psychoanalysis and semiotics. FT, HWC, LIT

title: Teaching Apprenticeship
description: The teaching apprenticeship allows the student to mentor under a member of the Wilson faculty in up to two undergraduate courses. As part of the experience, students may take part in the creation of materials for the classes, observe the class, hold office hours, tutor students in the subject, research and devise selected teaching materials, and teach one unit as the primary instructor, including the grading of paper/assignments. Prerequisites: Junior standing (undergraduate) and Permission.

title: Advanced Creative Writing: Fiction
description: Intensive study and practice in the creation of prose fiction, including detailed craft and skill-building instruction, written and oral peer-critiquing, self-reflective analysis, regular examination of contemporary theoretical trends in creative writing fiction, and the completion of several significant projects. Prerequisite: ENG 220 . ART, WI

title: Topics in English
description: Special topics in English. Prerequisite:  Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course. LIT, WI

title: Senior Advanced Study and Research
description: Second semester of a two-semester independent, advanced study and research for the senior student in a specific phase of the major area. Distinguished achievement in these course provides a basis for the awarding of Honors in the Major. Approval of the Committee on Academic Policy and Procedure is required.

title: Sr Advanced Study & Research
description: Independent advanced study and research for the senior student in a specific phase of the major area. Distinguished achievement in these courses provides a basis for the awarding of Honors in the Major. Approval of the Committee on Academic Policy and Procedures required.

title: Independent Study in English
description: Independent Study in English. Permission.

title: American Literature Since 1945
description: New directions in poetry, drama, fiction and literary innovations in the context of  international conflict, feminism,  environmentalism, civil rights and gay rights.

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content, having an appropriate, complementary role within the student's academic program. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Applied Linguistics for TESOL
description: This course studies language as an integrative system of component parts. Students gain both a theoretical grounding and an understanding of scaffolding content for English language learners, including exploring learner strategies. Topics include syntactic structure, the English sound system, oral and written language variation, and pragmatics and semantics. Prerequisite:  Completion of 200 level ENG course or a 200 level LIT course.

title: Advanced Creative Writing: Poetry
description: Intensive study and practice in the creation of poetic writing, including detailed craft and skill-building instruction, written and oral peer-critiquing, self-reflective analysis, regular examination of contemporary theoretical trends in creative writing poetry and the completion of several significant projects.

title: Creative Non-Fiction/Literary Journalism
description: Study and practice in the development of creative non-fiction and literary journalism, including detailed craft and skill-building instruction, written and oral peer-critiquing, self-reflective analysis, regular examination of contemporary theoretical trends in the fields, and the completion of several significant projects.

title: Chaucer
description: Detailed analysis and study of The Canterbury Tales. Includes close, critical readings of the original Middle English text and examination of the social, political and cultural climate in which Chaucer composed.

title: Advanced Creative Writing: Fiction
description: Intensive study and practice in the creation of prose fiction, including detailed craft and skill-building instruction, written and oral peer-critiquing, self-reflective analysis, regular examination of contemporary theoretical trends in creative writing fiction, and the completion of several significant projects.

title: Assessment Portfolio in Literary Studies
description: A study in writing and reflection, the Assessment  Portfolio in Literary Studies course will impart  the advanced rhetorical and critical skills that  will enable a student to demonstrate mastery of  the department's assessment criteria. Taken  concurrently with a senior-level Shakespeare  class in the spring semester. Prerequisite:  senior standing in English. Co-requisite: ENG 345 or ENG 346 . 1 semester hour.

title: Shakespeare's Romances and Tragedies
description: Critical reading of representative romances, tragedies, and genres, including a thorough introduction to Shakespeare and his sonnets.

title: Project in Creative Writing
description: Students will put together a literary magazine, learning the fundamentals of selecting manuscripts, editing work, design, and layout well as compose two projects in their chosen genre which they will submit for publication. Students will also complete a self-reflexive study. Prerequisite: ENG 321 /323/325.

title: Shakespeare's Histories/Comedies
description: Critical reading of representative histories and comedies, including a strong theoretical approach to the texts.

title: Teaching Apprenticeship
description: The teaching apprenticeship allows the student to mentor under a member of the Wilson faculty in up to two undergraduate courses. As part of the experience, students may take part in the creation of materials for the classes, observe the class, hold office hours, tutor students in the subject, research and devise selected teaching materials, and teach one unit as the primary instructor, including the grading of paper/assignments. Prerequisites: Junior standing (undergraduate) and Permission.

title: Language and Culture
description: This course focuses on the interconnected  relationship of language to culture, including  exploring issues of cultural identity,  acculturation and resistance, ethnocentrism,  medial diffusion, and language study.

title: Topics in English
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty which are not treated in the regular curriculum.

title: Intro to Environmental Science I
description: Introduction to the environmental issues that confront our species. The course focuses on science and the environmental issues that confront our species. It will analyze the causes of environmental problems; basic information of science, matter and energy as they pertain to environmental issues; biodiversity and how biodiversity can be sustained. The course will end by analyzing natural resource challenges (including food production, water, and mineral resources). ES

title: Environmental Geology
description: An introduction to how geology affects the human-built environment and how human activities alter the geologic environment. Environmental geology is applied geology. Prospective content of the course includes the following topics: fundamental concepts, earth materials and processes, river flooding, landslides and related phenomena, earthquakes and seismicity, volcanism, rock cycle, coastal hazards, hydrology, waste disposal, mineral resources, energy and the environment, and landscape evolution. ES

title: Environmental Science II Lab
description: Lab component of Introduction to Environmental Science II.

title: Film Genres and Genders
description: Historical study of Hollywood film genres and their relation to dichotomous gender. Emphasis on the genres of screwball comedy, maternal melodrama and film noir. Advanced readings in gender-analysis and film-studies scholarship. Representative directors include Pabst, Hawks, Sturgess, Rapper, Dmytryk, Ray, Hitchcock and Aldrich.

title: Intro to Environmental Science II
description: This course builds on foundation information  from Introduction to Environmental Science I. It covers energy resources, environmental health, climate  disruption, air and water pollution, solid and hazardous waste, urbanization, environmental economics, environmental politics and environmental  ethics. A laboratory component provides experiential learning  exercises.  Prerequisite: ENV-115 . ESL

title: Multicultural Environmental Lit
description: This course examines inequality in access to natural resources and the wealth they produce, in exposure to toxins, and in participation in environmental decision-making as represented through literature by Native American, African-American, Latino, and Asian-American authors. Permission of Director.

title: Gardening for Fitness/Pleasure
description: Provides hands-on skills for the small-scale gardener/agriculturist, while exploring the physical, scientific, artistic, spiritual and aesthetic dimensions of gardening. Basic gardening skills to be covered included composting and use of household refuse; soil management; seed storage, seeding and seedling management; integrated pest management; post-harvest handling of perishable produce; organic gardening and other sustainable gardening practices. Weekly fieldwork will be supplemented with assigned readings and projects that will enhance the student's enjoyment of gardening and the outdoors. (Two semester hours). PE activity credit.

title: Science, Technology and Society
description: A study of the relationships between technology and humans, ranging from traditional societies to the modern industrial and post-industrial West. Issues include the  relationship of science and technology,  technology and culture, and the effect of tools and machines on human experience. ES, ETH

title: Topics Environmental Studies
description: Special Topics in Environmental Studies

title: Applied Linguistics for TESOL
description: This course studies language as an integrative system of component parts. Students gain both a theoretical grounding and an understanding of scaffolding content for English language learners, including exploring learner strategies. Topics include syntactic structure, the English sound system, oral and written language variation, and pragmatics and semantics.

title: Stewardship of Watershed Ecosystems
description: The purpose of this workshop is to educate professional naturalists, elementary, junior and senior high school teachers and graduate and undergraduate students from colleges in southcentral Pennsylvania in theory and techniques of responsible ecological management at the watershed level. Methods addressed include technology, scientific research, and hands-on activity. Experiential learning will be complemented with lectures provided by professionals and activists in the field of ecological management. Not open to freshman students. Prerequisites: Foundations in English. ESL

title: Literary and Cultural Interpretation
description: In-depth study of developments in the history of interpretation. Representative methods include hermeneutics, feminism, psychoanalysis and semiotics.

title: Topics in Environmental Studies
description: Seminar course offered on an occasional basis. Possible topics include: low-input sustainable agriculture, sustainable energy, stewardship of watershed ecosystems (lab), sustainable architecture and permaculture design (lab).

title: Environmental Education
description: An introduction to fundamental approaches, processes and goals of environmental education (EE). Much of the course involves fieldwork, including at the Fulton Farm; guest speakers, experiential learning and field trips in which students will learn EE curricula recognized and adopted on a national or statewide basis. Students will learn Pennsylvania Department of Education standards and how to evaluate and deliver lessons in an engaging fashion. Prerequisite: permission of instructor. ES

title: Agroecology
description: Basic principles of ecology as they apply to agricultural systems. Comparison of industrialized and sustainable agriculture. Survey of traditional agricultural practices in global and historical context. Field and laboratory work in association with the Center for Sustainable Living. Students taking the course at the 300 level will complete a laboratory component. Prerequisite: ENV 115 or BIO 102 . ES, ESL

title: Ecological Perspectives Sci/Humanties
description: A senior capstone seminar that integrates student coursework, internships and other educational experiences,. Examines current  theoretical topics in  environmental and ecological thought, and will vary based on faculty and student interest. As a writing-intensive course, detailed research and written and oral presentations will accompany rigorous examination of primary literature. Prerequisites: Senior standing in environmental studies or related program of study and permission of instructor. ES, ETH, WI

title: Stewardship of Watershed Ecosystems
description: The purpose of this workshop is to educate professional naturalists, elementary, junior and senior high school teachers and graduate and undergraduate students from colleges in south-central Pennsylvania in theory and techniques of responsible ecological management at the watershed level. The methods that will be addressed include technology, scientific research, and hands-on activity. Experiential learning will be complemented with lectures provided by professionals and activists in the field of ecological management. Not open to freshman students. Prerequisite: ENV-115 . ESL

title: Topics Environmental Studies
description: Seminar course offered on an occasional basis. Possible topics include: low-input sustainable agriculture, sustainable energy, stewardship of watershed ecosystems (lab), sustainable architecture and permaculture design (lab).

title: Agroecology
description: Basic principles of ecology as they apply to agricultural systems. Comparison of industrialized and sustainable agriculture. Survey of practical agricultural practices in global and historical context. Field and laboratory work in association with the Fulton Center for Sustainable Living. Prerequisite: ENV 115 or BIO 102 . ES

title: Environmental Education
description: Introduction to fundamental approaches, processes and goals of environmental education (EE). Much of the course involves fieldwork, including at the Fulton Farm; guest speakers, experiential learning and field trips in which students will learn EE curricula recognized and adopted on a national or statewide basis. Students will learn Pennsylvania Department of Education standards and how to evaluate and deliver lessons in an engaging fashion. Prerequisite: permission of instructor. ES

title: Environmental Law
description: An introduction to the American legal system and the sources of environmental law; the litigation process and other tools to resolve environmental disputes; and the impact of Administrative law on the environment. Concludes with an introduction to some of the major environmental statutes in the U.S. Prerequisite: Foundations in English Course. ES, ETH

title: Agroecology Lab
description: Laboratory component of ENV316

title: Topics in Environmental Studies
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty which are not treated in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Intro to Equine Management
description: The foundation course for equestrian studies  addresses: equine-related careers; evolution and  history of the domestic horse; breeds, color and  marking genetics; conformation, behavior and  training; age and size determination; basic  anatomical structures; basic kinesiology; an  introduction to blemishes and unsoundness; basic  health and foot care; and ethical stewardship.  Barn assignments and horse care outside of class  are mandatory.

title: Equine Anatomy/Physiology
description: A study of the musculoskeletal, respiratory, cardiovascular, digestive, urinary, nervous, endocrine and reproductive systems of the horse from anatomical and physiological perspectives. Three hours lecture; two hours laboratory. Prerequisite: BIO 101 or 110. (4 sem.  hrs.)

title: Equine Breeding Management
description: An introduction to all aspects of equine breeding  management including: heredity, selection, the  reproductive system of mares and stallions, live  cover and artificial insemination, physiology of  gestation and fetal development, parturition and  dystocia, care of the neonate, care and handling  of breeding stock and foals, and breeding farm  design and management. Prerequisite: EQT 116 or  permission of the director of equestrian studies.

title: Equine Health Management
description: Examines the care of the horse, symptoms, causes, care and prevention of diseases and injuries; assisting the farrier and veterinarian; basics of blood testing, immunizations, parasites and their control; and record-keeping. Lecture, discussion, practica, laboratory and experiential work required at the stable. three hours lecture; two hours laboratory.  Prerequisites: EQS 110 , EQS 116 or VMT 216 . (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Internship
description: Work experience with a significant academic component. Students will complete a portfolio that exhibits both a visual and written display of learning, including evidence of theoretical knowledge and experiential learning. The portfolio may be used as a record of the student's work for graduate school or employment. A presentation to the college community is also required. Prerequisite: second-semester junior or senior if used to fulfill capstone requirement.

title: Equine Health Management Lab
description: Laboratory component of EQS-225 : Equine Health Management.

title: Intro to Training the Horse
description: Presents the basic concepts of a variety of traditional and modern training methods. The course provides an understanding of the horse's natural behavior, instincts and ability to learn. Students are taught the use of aids, tack, equipment, schooling exercises and training techniques, all designed to develop a calm, obedient, forward-moving horse. Lecture, discussion and practica.

title: Applied Horse Training Techniques I
description: A practicum in which students work with young, green or problem horses to break, train or retrain for use as riding horses. Coursework includes lunging, long-lining, ground driving, use of cavalletti systems and gymnastic jumping, backing and work under saddle. Does not satisfy PE activity requirement. Extensive work outside of class time required. Prerequisite: EFT 213 and EQT 203 or permission of instructor.

title: Applied Horse Training Techniques II
description: An advanced practicum class that builds on the  experience gained in EQS 235 in which students  break, train and/or retrain young, green and/or  problem horses. Coursework includes more advanced  schooling exercises, use of cavaletti systems and  gymnastic jumping, cross-training and  conditioning. Does not satisfy PE activity  requirement. Prerequisites: EQS  235 and EQT 303  or permission of instructor.  (two semester  hours.)

title: Equine Anatomy & Phys Lab
description: Lab component of EQS 116 .

title: Equine Nutrition
description: In this course students will develop an understanding of equine nutrition and its application to a variety of horses. Topics include: gastrointestinal physiology, classes of nutrients, concentrates and supplements, forage analysis and pasture management, reading feed tags and ration formulation, diseases and disorders, and feeding management. Prerequisite: EQS-116 or VMT-216 and EQS 225 .

title: Topics Equestrian Studies
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to broaden  the curriculum with subjects of special interest.

title: Management of Equine Events
description: Designed for show managers, riders, trainers and instructors, this course examines the operation of various shows, trials, events and competitions, including basics of course design. Reviews national and international rules, governing bodies and organizations. Practicum experiences running college-sponsored events, lecture and discussion. Practica and experiential work requirement at the stable. Not open to first-year students. Weekend commitments to running of horse shows and clinics required.

title: Ground Training the Horse
description: Student trainers will assess their assigned horses based upon use intended and behavior; develop an individualized training plan; and use classical and modern ground training methods to improve the performance of their assigned horses in the areas of: leading, behaviors, group dynamics, balance and movement, obstacles, "bomb proofing" and acceptance of equipment. Prerequisite: EQS 110 .

title: Equine Performance Management
description: Addresses the management of the equine athlete, including nutrition, lameness and conditioning; and modern techniques of prevention and treatment of injuries. Lectures, discussion, laboratory, practica and experiential work requirement at the stable Prerequisite: EQS 225 .

title: Methods Teaching/Training I
description: Courses designed to provide knowledge of riding and schooling principles, methods of instruction, lesson planning, student assessment and lesson horse selection. Lecture, discussion, student teaching practica, laboratory. Prerequisites: EQS 240 .

title: Topics Equestrian Studies
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to broaden  the curriculum with subjects of special interest.

title: Methods Teaching/Training III
description: Capstone courses in teaching horsemanship. Emphasis is placed on building a repertoire of teaching techniques; and critical evaluation of riders, horses, lesson plans, methodology and style. Discussions include the ethics of the industry and philosophical differences among disciplines. Preparation of riders and horses for competitive riding, supervision of teaching staff and peer evaluations. Discussion, presentations, supervised teaching practica, laboratory and experiential work requirements. Prerequisite: EQS 327

title: Language and Culture
description: This course focuses on the interconnected  relationship of language to culture, including  exploring issues of cultural identity,  acculturation and resistance, ethnocentrism,  medial diffusion, and language study.    Prerequisite:  200-level course in ENG or COM.   CD

title: Intro to Teaching Horsemanship
description: A preparatory course for teaching the basics of safe horsemanship, emphasizing theory of riding principles, methods of instruction, safety, prevention of accidents, and supervision of groups and individual riders. Lecture, discussion, observation, practica. Prerequisites: EQS 110 .

title: Methods Teaching/Training IV
description: Capstone courses in teaching horsemanship. Emphasis is placed on building a repertoire of teaching techniques; and critical evaluation of riders, horses, lesson plans, methodology and style. Discussions include the ethics of the industry and philosophical differences among disciplines. Preparation of riders and horses for competitive riding, supervision of teaching staff and peer evaluations. Discussion, presentations, supervised teaching practica, laboratory and experiential work requirements. Prerequisite: EQS 328

title: TESOL Instructional Methods/Assessment
description: Evidence-based instructional methods and assessments for students (PreK-12) with teaching English as a Second Language will be investigated and applied to student generated lesson plans, differentiated to meet the needs of students of other languages. Progress monitoring and data collection related to Individualized ESL will also be investigated.

title: Applied Linguistics for TESOL
description: This course studies language as an integrative system of component parts. Students gain both a theoretical grounding and an understanding of scaffolding content for English language learners, including exploring learner strategies. Topics include syntactic structure, the English sound system, oral and written language variation, and pragmatics and semantics.

title: TESOL Practicum
description: This undergraduate course applies the knowledge, skills, and strategies to extensive field experiences in local education agencies under the supervision of Wilson College faculty and qualified educators/ser-vice providers serving individuals from PreK-12 Grades. A minimum of 60 hours of field experiences is required. Field experience from previous EDU or ESL courses will be considered.  Prerequisites:  Students must be officially accepted into the Education Program and complete courses ESL 360 , 361,312, 362.

title: Methods Teaching/Training II
description: Courses designed to provide knowledge of riding and schooling principles, methods of instruction, lesson planning, student assessment and lesson horse selection. Lecture, discussion, student teaching practica, laboratory. Prerequisites: 326.

title: Horsemanship Techs-Behavior Modification
description: Course designed to introduce students to training and schooling techniques.  Emphasis placed on the application of theory and the impact of training exercises on the horse's behavior and way of going.  Exercises cover lunging techniques, groundwork, and addressing behavior concerns.  This course will utilize western tack. For safety reasons this course may be taken more than once. Prerequisites: Completion of Intermediate Horsemanship (English or western) and placement.  (two semester hours.)

title: Educational Perspectives - Diverse Socty
description: This course prepares students to deal with the conceptual, theoretical, political and philosophical issues in multicultural education. It has been designed to clarify issues related to pluralistic education, adopt a philosophical position, design and implement effective teaching strategies that reflect ethnic and cultural diversity, and prepare sound guidelines for multicultural programs and practices. In this course, diversity refers to all ways in which people differ, including that of  socioeconomic status, ethnicity, sexual  orientation, religious practices and other differences.  Requires permission of the appropriate Graduate Program Director.

title: Equine Facility Management
description: A study of the management and maintenance of an equine facility. Includes management of equines, employees and the facility as well as related activities such as boarding, horse shows and clinics.  Lecture, discussion, practicum, computer labs and site visits to local stable. Includes an experiential work requirement at the stables outside of classroom time. Prerequisites: EQS 225 and EQS 315 .

title: Applied Linguistics for TESOL
description: This course studies language as an integrative system of component parts. Students gain both a theoretical grounding and an understanding of scaffolding content for English language learners, including exploring learner strategies. Topics include syntactic structure, the English sound system, oral and written language variation, and pragmatics and semantics.

title: Technology Integration - Classroom
description: This productive, hands-on course will enable students to develop strategies for the effective integration of computer and multimedia technologies in their classrooms. Basic understanding and practical uses of major software applications and emerging computer and multimedia technologies will be emphasized. Students will develop various projects that use computer and multimedia technologies for curriculum enhancement, communication and record-keeping.

title: Language and Culture
description: This course focuses on the interconnected  relationship of language to culture, including  exploring issues of cultural identity,  acculturation and resistance, ethnocentrism,  medial diffusion, and language study.

title: Care & Prevention - Athletic Injuries
description: Basic instruction in the concepts and techniques in the prevention, diagnosis, treatment and rehabilitation of athletic injuries. Students will receive certification in CPR, first aid and AED. Three hours lecture, three hours lab. NSL (4 sem.hrs.)

title: Func Anatomy & Phys Fnd of Training
description: This course introduces the fundamentals of  anatomy, biomechanics, and exercise physiology.   Emphasizes the anatomical and mechanical  fundamentals of human movement and the  physiological theories related to the development  of muscular strength and endurance,  cardiovascular endurance, flexibility, power and  speed; along with cellular energy systems used in  training and performance.  Prerequisites: BIO102  or BIO110.

title: Educational Research/Design
description: This course will examine exemplary literature and research projects that represent examples of the systematic collection, evaluation, presentation and interpretation of research data in education. Various research designs and methodologies will be explored, emphasizing action research done in educational contexts. Student research projects will reflect a specific educational research style, such as experimental, correlational, survey, grounded theory, ethnography, narrative or mixed methods. Students will be guided in the selection of an appropriate master's project topic and a review and critique of relevant literature. They will develop a comprehensive design proposal for a major research project.

title: Teaching English Learners
description: Addresses methods and materials to prepare English language learners to enter and succeed in Pre-K  through 12 mainstream classroom.  Prerequisite: EDU 206 . Waived for degree-seeking graduate education students.

title: Care/Prevention Lab
description: Laboratory component of Care & Prevention.

title: Exercise Testing/Prescription
description: Assessment and development of the basic components of physical fitness. Discussion of the principles of exercise prescription based on current practices in the field of exercise science, physiology, medicine and physical education. Three hours lecture, three hours lab. (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Master's Project
description: Represents the final stages in the completion of a Master's Project begun in EDU 598 , where action research is conducted and a master's paper is completed and presented.  Students will work closely with a research advisor to conduct the action research project and associated report. Prerequisite:  EDU/ SPE-598 .

title: Exercise Testing/Prescription Lab
description: Laboratory component for Exercise Testing and Prescription.

title: Exercise Management - Special Cases
description: Principles of exercise prescription for individuals with acute or chronic health problems.  Discussion of exercise testing and  prescription for individuals with cardiac  abnormalities or problems will be included. Two  hours lecture, three hours lab. Prerequisite: ESS  220. (4 sem. hrs.)

title: TESOL Instructional Methods/Assessment
description: Evidence-based instructional methods and assessments for students (PreK-12) with teaching English as a Second Language will be investigated and applied to student generated lesson plans, differentiated to meet the needs of students of other languages. Progress monitoring and data collection related to Individualized ESL will also be investigated.

title: Physiology Foundation - Training
description: This course focuses on underlying physiologic theories supporting proper diet and training techniques for the athlete.  The physiologic basis for the development of muscular strength and endurance, cardiovascular endurance, flexibility, power and speed, along with cellular energy systems used in training and performance, will be addressed. Prerequisite: BIO 110 or 102. (2 semester hours)

title: TESOL Practicum
description: This course applies the knowledge, skills, and strategies to extensive field experiences in local education agencies under the supervision of Wilson College faculty and qualified educators/service providers serving individuals from PreK- Grade 12. A minimum of 60 hours of field experiences is required.

title: Sport in Society
description: Students will explore the relationship between sport and society. Through interdisciplinary perspectives, students analyze current issues including society-imposed barriers, Federal legislation and media depiction of sport.  By engaging with readings, discussions, and writing reflection papers, students will develop a deeper understanding of sport in society. Prerequisite: SOC120 and ENG101, ENG108,ENG104 or ENG180 or permission of instructor. WS,CD, WI

title: Total Wellness
description: Instead of focusing on one area of health, this course will explore a variety of topics related to mental, physical and emotional well-being through a series of content focused on positive behavior change/motivation, mindfulness, self-care, etc. Other public health concerns that impact well-being (such as physical inactivity, alcohol and other drugs, grief, loneliness, mental health, etc.) may be covered. The course looks at evidence-based strategies to integrate health and wellness as a part of building resilience to manage the fluctuations of life, and to make thoughtful choices that promote overall health and well-being. (2 semester hours)

title: Physiology of Exercise
description: Provides an understanding of the physiology of  exercise with an emphasis on physiological  responses and adaptations to exercise, and the  physiology of athletic performance. Emphasis on  scientific theories and principles underlying the  development of strength, endurance, flexibility  and cardiovascular endurance. Practical  application of course content in terms of  conditioning and training. Lecture and  laboratory. Prerequisites: BIO-102 , HSC-216 . NSL

title: Sport Facility Management
description: This course closely examines standards and  guidelines underlying operation of a fitness or  sport facility. Topics may include risk management; policy development; planning,  organizing and evaluating facilities; and  marketing, budgeting and public relations in facilities.

title: Kinesiology & Applied Anatomy
description: Study of anatomy, mechanics and human motion  specific to motor performance.  Emphasis on  skeletal and muscular systems and the  biomechanical principles related to human motion  and sports skills.  Lecture and Laboratory.  Prerequisites: BIO102; HSC215; MAT110; or  permission. NSL

title: Physiology of Exercise Lab
description: Lab component of Physiology of Exercise lab.

title: Exercise Management Lab
description: Laboratory component for Exercise Management for Special Cases.

title: Internship
description: ESS majors can select an internship experience that meets their individual career and educational goals. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Admin Aspects of Exercise/Sport Science
description: This course closely examines standards and  guidelines underlying operation of a fitness or  sport facility. Topics may include risk management; policy development; planning,  organizing and evaluating facilities; and  marketing, budgeting and public relations in facilities.

title: Topics - Exercise & Sports Science
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum with subjects of special interest.

title: Topics - Exercise & Sport Science
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum with subjects of special interest.

title: Topics in Exercise Science
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty which are not treated in the regular curriculum.

title: Psychology of Sport
description: The course will provide an overview of the psychological principles as they relate to sport, coaching and exercise; and will give students an understanding of human behavior and performance in sports situations. Topics will include theoretical foundations of behavior, neurophysiological responses to stress and exercise, group and team dynamics, adherence and motivation, practical application of mental training strategies, etc.

title: Kinesiology & Applied Anatomy Lab
description: Lab component of Kinesiology & Applied Anatomy  lab.

title: Psychology of Sport
description: The course will provide an overview of the psychological principles as they relate to sport, coaching and exercise; and will give students an understanding of human behavior and performance in sports situations. Topics will include theoretical foundations of behavior, neurophysiological responses to stress and exercise, group and team dynamics, adherence and motivation, practical application of mental training strategies, etc. Prerequisite: PSY110 or permission of instructor.  CC

title: Drawing I
description: Introduction to ideas and techniques in drawing in a wide range of subject matter. Media may include pencil, ink, charcoal and pastel. ART

title: Art Appreciation
description: Study of the perception of visual forms as expressed in painting, sculpture and architecture. Key works from a number of historical periods examined in terms of differences in style, technique and media. Emphasis on the classical style of Greek and Roman culture and their configuration during the Renaissance and in neoclassicism. FWC, ART

title: Introduction to Design
description: Introduction of the basic elements and underlying principles vital to understanding the organization of two-dimensional space. Students will explore design concepts while seeking individual solutions to a wide range of open-ended design problems. 2D Design introduces the computer as a design tool, though no previous computer experience is required. The course consists of a series of lectures in which design principles, concepts and theories are presented. Lectures are followed by studio exercises in which students have an opportunity to apply these principles in solving design problems. ART

title: Topics in Fine Arts
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty which are not treated in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Graphic Design I
description: Examines the basic types of computer graphics: pixel, object and vector based. Participants will learn the basic computer graphic tools, develop original graphics and manipulate existing graphics using popular graphics packages. Graphic layout for artistic, publication and multimedia purposes will be discussed and implemented, including the use of color, foreground, background, text and images. ART

title: Printmaking
description: Introduction to printmaking techniques; aesthetic concepts will be emphasized. Media may include silkscreen and etching. Lectures and exams on the history of printmaking. ART

title: Ceramics
description: Introduction to three-dimensional thinking and studio techniques. Projects may include pottery and/or ceramic sculpture. Lectures and examinations on the history of ceramics. ART

title: Painting
description: Introduction to traditional and experimental techniques and processes using acrylic or water-soluble oil paint. ART

title: Teaching Strategies for Health & PE
description: This course provides the students with the  application of best practices in instruction and  learning related to health and physical education. The students will develop appropriate  lesson planning strategies that relate to  differentiated instruction, assessment, and pedagogy. Students will complete a total of 5  observation hours. Prerequisites: Admission into  the Education Program

title: Western Art History: 1000-1800
description: Uses slide lectures, writing and field trip experiences to examine the formal and contextual language of art from medieval times through the 18th century with a focus on the Western tradition. Students apply feminist and other contemporary critiques to the discipline with an emphasis on understanding artists' choices. Prerequisite: Foundations course in English. ART, HWC, WI

title: Women Artists & Women in Art
description: A survey of women as creators, performers and subjects in the visual arts from ancient to contemporary times, including lectures and discussion of the cultural and social context in which women artists had to function. Includes field trips to museums. Prerequisite: Foundations course in English. HWC, WS, ART,  WI

title: Typography and the Letterform
description: Students in this course will examine and utilize typographic form as well as examine how letters and their designed applications help readers navigate language and retain information. Investigation focuses on gaining a working knowledge of fundamental typographic use and systems, terminology, hierarchy, composition, and history of the letterform. Prerequisite: Foundations course in English. ART

title: World Art
description: Surveys of art from Asia, Oceania, Africa and the Americas examining the integration of visual art, material culture and everyday life in non-European cultures. Artifacts will be related to their original context and the context of the Euro-American concept of art history. Prerequisite: Foundations course in English. ART, NWC, WI

title: Western Art History:1800-1980
description: Uses slide lectures, writing and field trip experiences to examine the formal and contextual language of art from the late 18th century through the 1970s, with a focus on the Western tradition. Students apply feminist and other contemporary critiques to the discipline with an emphasis on understanding artists' choices. Prerequisite: Foundations course in English. ART, HWC, WI

title: Digital Photography
description: Introduction to basic skills of making photographs with digital cameras and editing software. Emphasis on the concepts of seeing photographically and unifying Form, Subject and Content in your images as you transition from taking pictures to making photographs. ART

title: Photography Workshop
description: This course emphasizes in-depth exploration of a student's conceptual and technical development integrating film and digital media. Experimentation, historical research, and demonstrations form the basis for the student to develop a personal photographic voice. Substantial self-direction is expected. Prerequisite: FA-208 . ART

title: Women Artists & Women in Art
description: A survey of women as creators, performers and subjects in the visual arts from ancient to contemporary times, including lectures and discussion of the cultural and social context in which women artists had to function. Includes field trips to museums. Prerequisite: Foundations course in English. HWC, ART, WS, WI

title: Graphic Design II
description: This course is a continuation of the Graphic Design I course. Participants will build on the basic principles and practices of computer graphic design, using the higher level functions of the graphics packages available to them for the development of various projects in the areas of illustration, graphic design, business use, multimedia, desktop publishing and fine art. Participants will also critique and explore existing design/art materials. Prerequisite: FA 210 . ART

title: Web Design I
description: User Experience (UX) refers to all elements of a customer's interaction with a particular product, and this course will focus on UX design for technology products. Silicon Valley companies spend billions of dollars ensuring that technology products are intuitive to use. Students will learn what drives product usability, the fundamentals of UX design, and how to build wireframes and prototypes. Prerequisite: FA 210 .

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content, having an appropriate, complementary role within the student's academic program. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Teaching Apprenticeship
description: The teaching apprenticeship allows the student to mentor under a member of the Wilson faculty in up to two undergraduate courses. As part of the experience, students may take part in the creation of materials for the classes, observe the class, hold office hours, tutor students in the subject, research and devise selected teaching materials, and teach one unit as the primary instructor, including the grading of paper/assignments. Prerequisites: Junior standing (undergraduate) and Permission.

title: Topics in Art History
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum with subjects of special interest.

title: Ceramics III
description: Students build increasingly complex forms by combining and refining learned techniques. Students learn advanced concepts of clay and glaze formulation and are required to create a new glaze and new glaze combinations. Requirements include a hands-on approach to studio management, including loading, unloading and firing the electric kiln. Prerequisite: FA 247.

title: Senior Seminar
description: Students design a research proposal then execute the process and methodology required to realize an original body of work as a capstone experience. A portfolio, presentation, and defense are required for completion. Prerequisite: Permission.

title: Topics in Art History
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum with subjects of special interest.

title: Web Design III
description: In this course, students will propose and build a new website for an existing company. This website should be optimized to boost conversion events for the company in question. Students will need to research customer behavior and industry trends in order to successfully complete this course. This course will test the skills a student has built up through their Web Design major-design, prototyping, optimization and programming. Prerequisite: FA-344 .

title: Senior Seminar II
description: This course is the second half of a two-semester sequence capstone experience. Students complete, curate, exhibit and defend a body of original creative work as a capstone experience. A portfolio of professional documentation will accompany the exhibition. Prerequisite: FA 420 .

title: Drawing/Painting Workshop
description: Emphasis on combined techniques in drawing and painting as well as conceptual and theoretical problem solving within the disciplines as the focus of the course. Prerequisite: undergraduate section: FA-114 or FA-206 . ART (4 semester hours)

title: Web Design II
description: This course builds upon Web Design I and teaches students how to build effective user experiences through a rigorous process of implementing best practices, testing designs and iterating. This course will also cover topics such as branding, color palettes, user journeys, and designing for multiple platforms. Prerequisite: FA-343 .

title: Higher Education Seminar
description: This seminar is an examination of the path from being a graduate student to becoming a tenured faculty member. Students analyze academic job postings, craft application materials, and draft course proposals, syllabi and assessment rubrics. Students examine concerns such as Title XI, accreditation, and promotion and tenure, and engage in mock faculty interviews.  (1 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to program

title: Interdisciplinary Collaboration I
description: Students explore theoretical and practical approaches to determining meaning in a variety of art forms. Practice-led and academic research focuses on collaboration across disciplines. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.)  Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Studio Practice I
description: Intensive creative practice with close mentoring. Students explore content, form, and materials in individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Students demonstrate a cohesive conceptual and technical approach to art-making. Critical Response Process is included. (6 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Site-Sensitive Art-Making I
description: Exposes students to environmental mediation in art-making by exploring site-sensitive instillation, performance, writing, etc. The application of technology to exhibition/performance is encouraged. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to program

title: Studio Practice III
description: Students deepen home-based studio practice during non-resident periods in order to produce a semester portfolio. Includes individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Online Critical Response Process included. Mentoring by Home Studio Mentors and Wilson faculty required. (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Praxis: Movement Arts
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in movement-based art forms drawn from the last 100 years. Rotating topics place the movement-based artists studied in the sociopolitical context of their times. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Praxis: Creative Writing
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in writing-based art forms drawn from the last 100 years. Rotating topics place the writers studied in the sociopolitical context of their times. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.)Prerequisite:  Permission of the Director.

title: Photography Workshop
description: This course emphasizes in-depth exploration of a student's conceptual and technical development integrating film and digital media. Experimentation, historical research, and demonstrations form the basis for the student to develop a personal photographic voice. Substantial self-direction is expected. Prerequisite: Permission of the instructor.

title: Studio Practice IV
description: Intensive creative practice with close mentoring. Students explore content, form, and materials in individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Students demonstrate a cohesive conceptual and technical approach to art-making. Critical Response Process is included. Prequisite: Permission of the Director.

title: Somatic Practice I
description: Students explore the combined role of the body-mind in art making practices. Embodied research helps students examine their art work in the context of human ecology. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.)Prerequisite: Admission to program.

title: Contemporary Arts Practice I
description: Students examine the practice of art beyond media boundaries and the tensions inherent in making and presenting work in the 21st century. Substantial advance preparation is expected. (2 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Studio Practice II
description: Students establish a strong home-based studio practice during non-resident periods in order to produce a semester portfolio. Includes individual and collaborative assignments, and self-designed projects. Online Critical Response Process included. Mentoring by Home Studio Mentors and Wilson faculty required. (3 sem. hrs.) Permission of the Director.

title: Praxis: Visual Arts
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in visual-based art forms drawn from the last 100 years. Rotating topics place the visual artists studied in the sociopolitical context of their times. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.) Prerequisites: permission of director.

title: Contemporary Arts Practice II
description: Students further examine the practice of art beyond media boundaries and the tensions inherent in making and presenting work in the 21st century. Each student is responsible for substantial advance preparation. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Interdisciplinary Collaboration II
description: Students deepen exploration of theoretical and practical approaches to determining meaning in a variety of art forms. Practice-led and academic research focus on collaboration across disciplines. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Site-Sensitive Art Making II
description: Students further their work in environmental mediation in art-making by exploring site-sensitive instillation, performance, writing, etc. The application of technology to site-specific work is encouraged. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Permissoin of Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Somatic Practice II
description: Students expand their understanding of the body-mind in art making practices. Embodied research helps students examine their art work in the context of human ecology. Movement-based creative inquiry is emphasized. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. ((2 sem. hrs.)

title: International Finance
description: Introduction to principles, practices and institutions involved in acquisition, transfer and administration of funds in the international financial environment. Financial markets for  international operations, uses of the foreign exchange market, business implications of exchange  rate changes, and the future of the international monetary system. Prerequisites: ACC 106 , ECO 204 .

title: Corporate Finance Fundamentals
description: An introduction to the theoretical concepts of corporate finance. Focuses on management of  working capital, cost of capital, capital  budgeting and capital structure planning. Also provides an overview of money and capital markets. Prerequisites: ACC 105 , MAT 103 .

title: Investments
description: An in-depth analysis of investment planning of  individuals and institutions; analysis of  securities, functions and operation of securities markets. Overview of regulations and the role of  the government in the securities markets.  Prerequisite: 240.

title: Financial Institutions & Technologies
description: This course details the role and functioning of financial markets, financial institutions, and financial products. We will explore the technologies that underpin these institutions through the perspective of how they affect cash holdings, investments (stocks, bonds), foreign exchange, and derivatives.  Prerequisites: FIN-240 .

title: Introduction to Global Studies
description: Interdisciplinary introduction to Global Studies and current theories related to the processes of globalization with an emphasis on social change and conflict.  Students will study major cultural, social, political, and economic concepts and theories.  NWC

title: Physical Geography
description: Students analyze the physical world and the spatial interrelationship between human beings and their physical environment. Special attention given to exogenic and endogenic forces of land formation, climate, soil, vegetation and water resources in a regional framework. Considers the nature of geography as a discipline, its methods of analysis and the kinds of problems which the geographer investigates.

title: Cultural Geography
description: Examines how innovation-diffusion processes of major cultures affect the landscape. Focuses on non-environmental reasons for the diverse ways human beings use the Earth. Students analyze cultural factors such as taste preferences, systems of social organization and traditional systems and techniques of economic production.

title: First Year Seminar
description: Required for all first-time students in the first semester of enrollment. Waived for continuing education and most transfer students. The First-Year Seminar (FYS) informs students about the College's Honor Principle, traditions, history and mission. FYS also promotes students' identification of their academic and personal strengths, interests and areas for potential growth. Assignments emphasize development of skills in writing, oral communication, library and database research methods, study skills, time management and critical thinking. The course consists of two parts: an academic seminar and workshops.

title: Thesis Performance/Exhibition I
description: Students pursue a sophisticated thesis question via rigorous practice-led and academic research, and begin documenting their process in a context statement. Frequent interaction with both the onsite and Wilson faculty mentors is required. Prerequisite: Finalized thesis proposal, and permission of the program director. (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Drawing/Painting Workshop
description: Emphasis on combined techniques in drawing and painting as well as conceptual and theoretical problem solving within the disciplines as the focus of the course. Prerequisite: permission of instructor(4 semester hours)

title: Personal Finance
description: This course enables students to acquire an introduction to the concepts of personal finance. Students apply financial management concepts in the areas of basic financial planning, creating a budget, preparing personal income tax returns, managing investments, buying a house, the use of credit, the need for insurance and saving for retirement. This course fulfils the Quantitative Skills requirement.

title: Cultural Geography
description: An introduction to human geography and a study of diverse human populations and the role of culture in shaping how people relate to the spaces and natural environments in which they live.  Foundations in English Course.  NWC

title: Thesis Performance/Exhibition II
description: The student will complete and present the thesis exhibition/performance with appropriate documentation. All materials must demonstrate mastery of conception, intention and production. An oral defense of the thesis exhibition/performance is required. Prerequisite: FA 598 . (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Global Health
description: This course includes an examination of global health theory and concepts, as well as an examination of critical issues such as  Ebola, development, sanitation and food security. It will also examine public health policy around the world. Prerequisite: Foundations in English  course. NWC

title: Special Topics in Global Studies
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis in response  to student interest in topics in global studies,  including the cultural, political, economic  and/or historical dimensions of global society.

title: Topics in Global Studies
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis in response  to student interest in topics in global studies,  including the cultural, political, economic  and/or historical dimensions of global society.

title: American History to 1877
description: An historical survey of the political, social, and economic development of America from the colonial period through Reconstruction. HWC, CD

title: Topics in History
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty which are not treated in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Ancient Medicine
description: Survey of the healing traditions of the ancient Mediterranean world. Critical reading of medical literature by Hippocrates and other significant authors from Egypt, Greece, and Rome. Topics may include: theories about anatomy and physiology, the relationship between medicine and religion, the social status of doctors, and beliefs about women's bodies. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing course. FWC, WI

title: The Olympics
description: An investigation of aspects of the Olympic games, both ancient and modern. Topics will include mythology, art and archaeology of Olympia, sports in the ancient world, ancient Greek religion, the Olympic games in ancient and modern international affairs, the history of the Olympic games, and the games in modern culture. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing course. FWC

title: Civil War & Society
description: A social history of the American Civil War. The focus is on the experiences of everyday Americans in this most terrible of conflicts. War as a "gendering activity" will also be explored. Particular attention will be given to the impact of the war on women, African- Americans and the common soldier. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing course. HWC, WI

title: Gender and Power in Medieval Europe
description: An investigation of the lives and experiences of women in Europe from c. 500 CE - 1500 CE, placing medieval Europe within a global perspective. The relationship between gender and power will be explored through topics such as political power, religious life, work, and family work in different historical contexts across medieval Europe. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course. FWC, WS, WI

title: The Crusades
description: A historical study of the Crusading movement in Western Europe from approximately 1095 - 1300 CE, focusing on the first four Crusades.  We will investigate Christian and Muslim concepts of "holy war", the impact of the Crusading movement on current events in the Middle East, and the use of the Crusades in popular culture, as demonstrated by their use in movies. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course, FWC, NWC

title: America in the 50'S & 60'S
description: Covers major events and movements of the period, including McCarthyism; the Cold War; space race; Korean and Vietnam wars; the presidency from Truman to Nixon; the growing impact of mass media on politics; the civil rights, feminist and environmental movements; and other economic, social, political and cultural trends. Prerequisite: Foundations in English course. CC, CD

title: Contemporary Issues in History
description: An introduction to the discipline of history and an interrogation of the ways that we interact with the past on a daily basis, including museums, podcasts, television, film, Broadway musicals, and more, focusing on the way that the past is remembered and who gets to tell that story. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course. CC, WI

title: World History I (Pre-1492)
description: A comparative survey of the world's major civilizations and their interconnections from the inception of human civilization through the beginning of the colonial age (1492). FWC, NWC

title: World History II (post-1492)
description: This course examines the history of the world from the age of colonialism (1492), through the renaissance, age of enlightenment, industrial revolution, and the modern era up until modern day. HWC, NWC

title: The Silk Roads
description: A study of the cross-cultural interactions of the trade routes between China and Central Asia, including commercial and cultural interactions between the Chinese, Indian, Iranian, Roman, and Islamic peoples, from c. 200 BCE through the 1500s CE. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course. FWC, NWC, WI

title: American History Since 1877
description: An historical survey of the political, social, and economic development of America from the end of Reconstruction to the present. CC, HWC

title: The American Revolution
description: The study of the Revolution as a political, social and military struggle. Examines the relationship of the colonies to the British Empire, the legacy of England's own revolutionary struggles, and the events and arguments that led from resistance to rebellion-in particular their impact on those traditionally excluded from political action and identity. Also looks at constitution-making, the development of a national government, the emergence of a national culture and ways the Revolution has been perceived by later generations. Foundations in Writing Course. HWC

title: Archaeology of Pennsylvania
description: An introduction to the discipline of archaeology and the study of human culture with an emphasis on the pre-historical archaeology of Pennsylvania. Students will also be introduced to various methods for doing local history from a material perspective.   Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course, FWC, NWC

title: Women in Antiquity
description: The role and status of women in ancient cultures (primarily those of the Graeco-Roman world, including Egypt, northern Africa, and the Middle East) from historical and archaeological sources as well as from myth, literature and art.
