Topics may include: comparisons with contemporary society, women and religion, and perceptions of female and male bodies.  Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing course. FWC, WS, WI

title: The Crusades
description: A historical study of the Crusading movement in Western Europe from approximately 1095 - 1300 CE, focusing on the first four Crusades.  We will investigate Christian and Muslim concepts of "holy war", the impact of the Crusading movement on current events in the Middle East, and the use of the Crusades in popular culture, as demonstrated by their use in movies. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course, Any HIS course at the 200 level. FWC, NWC

title: Civil War & Society
description: A social history of the American Civil War. The focus is on the experiences of everyday Americans in this most terrible of conflicts. War as a "gendering activity" will also be explored. Particular attention will be given to the impact of the war on women, African- Americans and the common soldier. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing course and any HIS course at the 200 level. HWC, WI

title: Women in Antiquity
description: The role and status of women in ancient cultures (primarily those of the Graeco-Roman world, including Egypt, northern Africa, and the Middle East) from historical and archaeological sources as well as from myth, literature and art. Topics may include: comparisons with contemporary society, women and religion, and perceptions of female and male bodies. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing course and any 200-level course. FWC, WS, WI

title: The Silk Roads
description: A study of the cross-cultural interactions of the trade routes between China and Central Asia, including commercial and cultural interactions between the Chinese, Indian, Iranian, Roman, and Islamic peoples, from c. 200 BCE through the 1500s CE. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course. Any HIS course at the 200 level. FWC, NWC, WI

title: Ancient World in Film/Popular Culture
description: An investigation of the history and mythology of the ancient world in popular culture, including film and literature.  Topics could include "History vs. Myth in the Greek World", "Women", "Slavery", "Great Men" and "Film as a Historical Thought Experiment." Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course and any HIS course at the 200 level. FWC, WI

title: The Olympics
description: An investigation of aspects of the Olympic games, both ancient and modern. Topics will include mythology, art and archaeology of Olympia, sports in the ancient world, ancient Greek religion, the Olympic games in ancient and modern international affairs, the history of the Olympic games, and the games in modern culture. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing course and any HIS course at the 200 level. FWC

title: Museums and Material Culture
description: An examination of museums as social and cultural institutions and how they represent the past, the role that they play in heritage management, and how they actively shape our understandings of the past. Students will also apply methods for the study of material culture through a research project using artifacts from the Wilson College Archives. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course, any course at the 2xx level. FWC, ETH, WI

title: Topics in History
description: Courses offered periodically to broaden the curriculum with subjects of special interest. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

title: Ancient Medicine
description: Survey of the healing traditions of the ancient Mediterranean world. Critical reading of medical literature by Hippocrates and other significant authors from Egypt, Greece, and Rome. Topics may include: theories about anatomy and physiology, the relationship between medicine and religion, the social status of doctors, and beliefs about women's bodies. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing course and any 200 level course. FWC, WI

title: The American Revolution
description: The study of the Revolution as a political, social and military struggle. Examines the relationship of the colonies to the British Empire, the legacy of England's own revolutionary struggles, and the events and arguments that led from resistance to rebellion-in particular their impact on those traditionally excluded from political action and identity. Also looks at constitution-making, the development of a national government, the emergence of a national culture and ways the Revolution has been perceived by later generations. Prerequisite: Foundations in Writing Course. Any HIS course at the 200 level. HWC

title: Internship
description: Work experience with a significant academic component. Students will complete a portfolio that exhibits both a visual and written display of learning, including evidence of theoretical knowledge and experiential learning. The portfolio may be used as a record of the student's work for graduate school or employment. A presentation to the college community is also required. Prerequisite: second-semester junior or senior if used to fulfill capstone requirement.

title: Topics in History
description: Courses offered periodically to broaden the curriculum with subjects of special interest. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

title: Microbiology for Nurses
description: Emphasizes the interaction of non-pathogenic and pathogenic microorganisms with humans and their implications with health. This will enable students to understand disease-causing presentatives of different groups of microorganisms from modes of transmission to methods of control. They also learn the accepted policies pertaining to the containment of infectious microorganisms within the hospital environment. The lecture material is complimented by laboratory exercises in which students acquire hands-on experience by applying the microbiological concepts. NSL

title: Gender and Power in Medieval Europe
description: An investigation of the lives and experiences of women in Europe from c. 500 CE - 1500 CE, placing medieval Europe within a global perspective. The relationship between gender and power will be explored through topics such as political power, religious life, work, and family work in different historical contexts across medieval Europe. Foundations in Writing course and any HIS course at the 200 level. FWC, WS, WI

title: Ancient World in Film/Popular Culture
description: An investigation of the history and mythology of the ancient world in popular culture, including film and literature.  Topics could include "History vs. Myth in the Greek World", "Women", "Slavery", "Great Men" and "Film as a Historical Thought Experiment."

title: Microbiology for Nurses Lab
description: Lab component for HSC-111 : Microbiology for Nurses

title: Human Nutrition
description: This course is designed for health and nursing professionals to understand the current and trending discipline of nutrition, relationships of diet to health and disease, and the role of health and nursing professionals in the discipline.  This course examines the nutritional needs throughout the life span with emphasis on health impact with regard to the physiological, social, economic, and life style factors that influence nutritional status, food choices, and specific life stage concerns with emphasis from a clinical perspective.  Prerequisites: HSC-216 . NS, WI

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology I
description: This laboratory course is the first of a two-semester sequence designed to provide a systemic background in human anatomy and physiology for students entering health, medicine or nursing professions. Using an integrated approach involving lecture and laboratory, this first semester of two courses studies cellular structure and function, and the following organ systems of the body: integumentary, skeletal, muscular and nervous. Prerequisite: BIO 102 . Waived for Prelicensure BSN students.

title: Intro to Medical Terminology
description: This course is intended for members of the  community who need a medical terminology course  for personal need, employment, or as a  pre-requisite for further study. College credit  will be earned for this self-paced class. The  course fee includes all materials needed for this  course, including the textbook. This is an online  course and internet access is required.

title: Human Anatomy/Physiology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-215: Human Anatomy and Physiology I. Lab fee required. Restricted to students in the LPN to BSN Program.

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology II
description: This laboratory course is the second of a two-semester sequence designed to provide a systemic background in human anatomy and physiology for students entering health, medicine or nursing professions. Using an integrated approach involving lecture and laboratory, this second semester of two courses studies the following organ systems of the body: endocrine, cardiovascular, lymphatic, immune, respiratory, digestive urinary, and reproductive. Additionally, the processes of metabolism, electrolytes and fluids, and acid-base balance will be discussed. Prerequisite: HSC 215 . NSL

title: Human Anatomy/Physiology Lab
description: Laboratory component of BIO-215: Human Anatomy and Physiology I. Lab fee required.

title: Assessment Portfolio - History
description: This course prepares the student for their final portfolio in history. The student will intensively study the form, construction, and meaning of critical thought and product in History, including their own work. Prerequisite: Junior or Senior standing in History. Co-requisite: HIS-330 or HIS-332

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology II
description: This laboratory course is the second of a two-semester sequence designed to provide a systemic background in human anatomy and physiology for students entering health, medicine or nursing professions. Using an integrated approach involving lecture and laboratory, this second semester of two courses studies the following organ systems of the body: endocrine, cardiovascular, lymphatic, immune, respiratory, digestive urinary, and reproductive. Additionally, the processes of metabolism, electrolytes and fluids, and acid-base balance will be discussed. Prerequisite: HSC 215 .

title: Museums and Material Culture
description: An examination of museums as social and cultural institutions and how they represent the past, the role that they play in heritage management, and how they actively shape our understandings of the past. Students will also apply methods for the study of material culture through a research project using artifacts from the Wilson College Archives.

title: Narrative Medicine
description: Introduction to theories of Narrative Medicine through close readings and analysis of illness and caregiving narratives. Works may include essays, memoirs, theoretical texts, and fiction. Students will also learn to use reflective writing and narration in their own lives as a means of processing difficult emotional experiences. Prerequisite: Foundation course in English. LIT, WI

title: Human Anatomy/Physiology Lab II
description: Laboratory component of HSC 216 : Human Anatomy and Physiology II. Lab fee required.

title: Genetics for Health Sciences
description: A sound knowledge of genetics and genomics is essential for health care providers in evaluating needs of patients and delivering of care to patients and families. The course will prepare nursing and health science students by reinforcing the basic principles of genetics while exploring new advances and discussing how these advances will affect health care, including genetic variation and inheritance; ethical, legal, and social issues in genetic health care; genetic therapeutics; genetic basis of selected alterations to health across the life span; and cultural considerations in genetic health. Prerequisite: HSC-216 . (3 sem. hrs.) NS

title: Health Promotion/Program Development
description: Provides a foundation for examining factors that influence the health of communities and populations locally, nationally and globally. Health promotion and health maintenance are emphasized, including Healthy People 2020 initiatives. Students explore models of health promotion and challenges in creating a healthy society. Prerequisite: PSY 110 .

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology I
description: This laboratory course is the first of a two-semester sequence designed to provide a systemic background in human anatomy and physiology for students entering health, medicine or nursing professions. Using an integrated approach involving lecture and laboratory, this first semester of two courses studies cellular structure and function, and the following organ systems of the body: integumentary, skeletal, muscular and nervous. Prerequisite: Restricted to students in the LPN to BSN Program.

title: Healthcare/Medcl Humnty Service Project
description: Students will apply theories and knowledge from program coursework to an educational service experience as well as participate in a seminar devoted to discussion of advanced readings in Healthcare and Medical Humanities. Students will create an e-portfolio in which they will chronicle their experiences and reflect on the program goals. Prerequisite: Permission

title: Hlth Care Systms, Policies & Regulations
description: HSC-430 Health Care Systems, Policies (3 Credits): Examines the health policy world from the perspective of building healthier communities. Students gain an appreciation for the political, ethical, financial, and regulatory processes involved in the provision of healthcare. Emphasis is placed on understanding health disparities within the United States and globally. Prerequisite: PSY-110 and SOC-120

title: Public Health & Epidemiology
description: Presents the principles and practice of epidemiology and its application in controlling health problems. By applying the concepts learned to population health problems and issues, students relate the practice of epidemiology to public health and develop an appreciation of public health programs and policies. Prerequisite: MAT-115 , PSY-110 or PSY-110 .

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content,  having an appropriate, complementary role within  the student's academic program. Approval of the  academic dean is required.

title: Poetics of Listening:Healthcare Writing
description: Train to become a listener poet, especially for healthcare settings. Learn to create a psychologically safe space, use silence to advance a conversation, reflect back, and synthesize what you learn into a creative practice. Listener Poet certification available through The Good Listening Project. Prerequisites: Permission of the Graduate Program Director.

title: Human Anatomy/Physiology Lab II
description: Laboratory component of HSC 216 : Human Anatomy and Physiology II. Lab fee required. Restricted to students in the LPN to BSN program.

title: Empathy
description: An historical, cultural, and social exploration of the concept and manifestation of empathy in both the past and the present, emphasizing an applied component to foster leaders who are caring, civic minded, and socially aware and connected. Permission of program director required.

title: Holistic Health
description: This course will provide an overview of holistic healthcare values/tenets that encompass health, wellness, well-being, healing, healthy coping, and transitional processes. It will enhance the concept of health and wellness beyond traditional Western Medicine techniques. The goal is to offer knowledge of a new facet to healthcare as it relates to a more integrative approach across the curriculum (nursing, health science, exercise science, vet therapies, equine therapies, STEM sciences, etc.)Prerequisites:  Foundations in English Course. NWC

title: Topics in Language & Literature
description: In-depth examination of a limited field of study in the language and literature area, unified by a singular focus, which may include author, genre, theme, period, or objective. Emphasizes critical and theoretical analysis of text and the relationship of literature and language to social and cultural history. Permission of program director required.

title: Humanities Internship
description: A practical course in experiential learning that combines research components, authorship and manuscript generation, and the presentation of appropriate materials. Permission of program director required. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content,  having an appropriate, complementary role within  the student's academic program. Approval of the  academic dean is required.

title: Topics in Arts & Culture
description: In-depth examination of a limited field of study in the arts and culture area, unified by a singular focus, which may include genre, period, style, artist, or auteur. Emphasizes critical and theoretical analysis of subject and the relationship of art and culture to social and cultural history.

title: Biological Basis-Perception & Movement
description: Perception and Movement are fundamentally driven by biological processes. This course provides students with an understanding of the various systems and organs that play a role in the human ability to perceive the world and move through it. It builds upon Neuroscience Fundamentals to allow students to understand the impact of core neuroscience concepts. Prerequisite: INS-301

title: Methods & Materls Humanities Resrch
description: Introduces students to the study, practice, and materials of humanities scholarly inquiry, especially in preparation for further graduate-level work. Students will learn how to craft and answer inquiries typically found within the humanities field. Through analytic reading, guided discussion, and intensive study of best research practices, students will explore the disciplinary knowledge, modes of inquiry, defining principles, and core ideas of the humanities. Permission of program director required.

title: Master's Thesis I
description: Preparation of thesis for M.A. degree carried out under supervision of thesis director.  A student preparing a thesis must complete the thesis sequence in consecutive semesters (excluding the January term) and must be in the final two semesters of degree study. Prerequisite: HUM 510 ; completion of thesis proposal; permission of program director.

title: Neuroscience Foundations
description: This course introduces students to the field of neuroscience, explores the cellular and molecular basis of neural systems, and discusses the neural basis of cognition. Students are expected to leverage their understanding of biology and chemistry to build a working knowledge of neuroscience fundamentals. Prerequisites: PSY 218 , BIO 102 , and CHM 102 . NS

title: Clinical Neuropathology
description: This course captures foundational concepts in modern psychiatric care and neuroscience and makes them clear and accessible. It provides students with a broad knowledge base covering many of the latest developments in the field of neuroscience, including our most modern understanding of developmental disorders, various pathologies of neurological systems, the role of microbiology in neurological care and more. Upon completion, students will be well prepared to pursue graduate study or work in the sciences, armed with a strong understanding of the current state of both Neuroscience and Mental Health and the connections between both.  Prerequisite: INS-303 .

title: Elementary Latin I
description: Introduction to Latin grammar with emphasis on reading. Includes lectures on Roman civilization and study of English derivatives. (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Elementary Korean I
description: Basic foundation in Korean language and vocabulary, including speaking, listening, reading, and writing.

title: Elementary Latin II
description: Introduction to Latin grammar with emphasis on reading. Includes lectures on Roman civilization and study of English derivatives.  Prerequisite: LAT 101 or placement. (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Ethics and Leadership
description: This course examines ethical theories and decision-making methods relevant to leadership, the virtues of leadership, and the role of leadership in cultivating ethical organizations and communities. Prerequisite: Foundations in English.  ETH

title: Master's Project
description: Preparation and completion of a significant project carried out under the supervision of a project director in the final semester of study. Student will complete 10,000 word academic article; abstract; and oral defense. Prerequisite: HUM 510 ; permission of program director.

title: Master's Thesis II
description: Completion of thesis for M.A. degree carried out under supervision of thesis director. An oral defense of thesis is required. A student preparing a thesis must complete the thesis sequence in consecutive semesters (excluding the January term) and must be in the final two semesters of degree study. Prerequisite: HUM 598 ; permission of program director.

title: Leadership Models and Practices
description: This course reviews contemporary and empirically proven approaches and methods to inspire, motivate and lead. Its focus is on high-level, integrative approaches to fortify and empower effective change agents and leaders to design and implement the highest standards in any professional setting. Prerequisites:  English Foundations and Junior Standing.

title: Teaching Apprenticeship
description: The teaching apprenticeship allows the student to mentor under a member of the Wilson faculty in up to two undergraduate courses. As part of the experience, students may take part in the creation of materials for the classes, observe the class, hold office hours, tutor students in the subject, research and devise selected teaching materials, and teach one unit as the primary instructor, including the grading of paper/assignments. Prerequisites: Junior standing (undergraduate) and Permission.

title: Ethics and Leadership
description: This course examines ethical theories and decision-making methods relevant to leadership, the virtues of leadership, and the role of leadership in cultivating ethical organizations and communities. Prerequisite: Permission of the Program Director.

title: Cognitive Neuroscience
description: Cognitive Neuroscience is the study of the biological process which underlie behavior, learning, thought and experience. This course builds on students' understanding of neuroscience and psychology to explore information processing, behavior, language, and more. Special attention is paid to the neurological factors which drive behavior and give rise to a range of disorders. Prerequisite: INS-302 .

title: Interpersonal Dynamics
description: The course provides students with a greater understanding of interpersonal communications in a data-gathering setting. Students learn to assess verbal and nonverbal behavior and become more effective at obtaining information through individual interviews and interactions. Instruction covers non-confrontational interview techniques; techniques for conflict resolution and negotiation become integral parts of these activities. Permission of director.

title: Applied Leadership Project
description: Students propose, design, organize, and carry out a service-oriented community/campus enterprise. This project could comprise a fundraiser, a conference, a series of workshops, or other service project designed to demonstrate the application of student learning and benefit the broader community. All projects must be approved by the Program Director.

title: Teaching Apprenticeship
description: The teaching apprenticeship allows the student to mentor under a member of the Wilson faculty in up to two undergraduate courses. As part of the experience, students may take part in the creation of materials for the classes, observe the class, hold office hours, tutor students in the subject, research and devise selected teaching materials, and teach one unit as the primary instructor, including the grading of paper/assignments. Prerequisites: Junior standing (undergraduate) and Permission.

title: Elementary Korean II
description: Basic foundation in Korean language and vocabulary, including speaking, listening, reading, and writing.  Prerequisite: KOR-101

title: Topics in Leadership
description: In-depth examination of a limited field of study in leadership. Permission of program director required.

title: Basic Mathematics
description: Developmental course to prepare students for MAT 098. Topics include: operations of decimal numbers; fractions and signed numbers; conversion between fractions, decimal numbers and percentages; conversion of units; powers roots, signed numbers and beginning algebra. Credit does not apply toward graduation, but the course grade is calculated into the cumulative GPA.

title: Basic and Intermediate Algebra
description: Developmental course that bridges concepts to provide the foundation for all majors. Topics include numbers in different forms, operations, conversions, units, power rules, signed numbers, equations, functions, graphs, inequalities, and logarithms, basic statistics interpretations and applications. Credit does not apply toward graduation, but the course grade is calculated into the GPA.

title: Intermediate Algebra
description: Topics include real number system and its laws, linear equations and inequalities, direct and inverse variations, properties of exponents, scientific notations, solving quadratic equations by using factoring and quadratic formulas, graphing of linear equations, system of linear equations and linear inequalities, linear programming and logarithms. Credit does not apply toward graduation, but the course grade is calculated into the cumulative GPA. Prerequisite: MAT 096/099 or math placement exam results.

title: Pre-Calculus Mathematics
description: Introduction to analytic geometry, vector algebra, trigonometric functions and properties, combinatorics, sequences, and limits and their relations to functions. Prerequisite: MAT 103 or math placement results. NS

title: College Algebra
description: Introduction to coordinate systems, geometry of the plane, linear, quadratic, rational, exponential, logarithmic functions and properties, combinatorics, inverse functions, and complex numbers and operations. Prerequisite: MAT 097/098 or  math placement results. NS

title: Leadership Internship
description: A practical learning course where students apply the principles and theories of leadership in a professional setting. Leadership internships require opportunities for the student to lead others in a corporative, structured, or hierarchical environment. Permission of program director required. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Leadership Models and Practices
description: This course reviews contemporary and empirically proven approaches and methods to inspire, motivate and lead. Its focus is on high-level, integrative approaches to fortify and empower effective change agents and leaders to design and implement the highest standards in any professional setting.  Permission of the Director.

title: Calc & Analytic Geometry I
description: Review of functions and introduction to the concepts of limits, differentiation and integration. Use of the derivative and analytic geometry for sophisticated graphing of functions and relations. Application of the derivative and definite integral to the study of problem-solving techniques in the physical sciences. Prerequisite: MAT 110 or math placement exam results. NS (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Introductory Statistics
description: A course in basic statistical concepts and techniques for the non-mathematics major. Topics include descriptive statistics, measures of central tendency and dispersion, probability theory, standardized probability distributions, correlation and regression, inferential statistics, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing and analysis of variance. Prerequisite: 097/098 or math placement exam results. NS

title: Calculus & Analytic Geometry II
description: Continuation of the study of the derivative and definite integral for problem-solving, involving more challenging functions. Topics include techniques of integration, improper integrals, numerical approximation techniques, conics, sequences and series, and polar coordinates. Prerequisite: MAT 130 . NS (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Discrete Mathematics
description: An introduction to basic techniques and modes of reasoning for discrete problem-solving. Topics include set theory, mathematical logic, mathematical induction, number theory, recurrence relations, counting, graphs and lattices. Prerequisite: MAT 103 or math placement exam results. FT

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content, having an appropriate, complementary role within the student's academic program. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Geometry
description: A study of the theoretical and historical development of geometry, including the tools of geometry. Topics include axiomatic systems, Euclidean geometry, non-Euclidean geometries and applications of these geometries to other fields of study. Prerequisites: MAT 205 . NS, FT

title: Mathematics for Liberal Studies
description: An introduction to mathematical structures and applications designed to help students understand the historical and contemporary role of mathematics in everyday life. The course includes topics from management science, probability and statistics, social choice, geometry and measurement, and computer sciences, along with a substantial review of algebraic concepts. This course fulfills the foundations requirement for graduation. Not intended for students who will continue to take mathematics.

title: Intro Theory/Functions Complex Variable
description: Discussion of the algebraic properties of the complex number field; study of the elementary properties of analytic functions; introduction to complex integration. Prerequisite: MAT 242 . NS, FT

title: Ordinary Differential Equations
description: Introduction to ordinary differential equations, existence and uniqueness theorems; methods of solving first-order equations and second-order linear equations. Prerequisite: MAT 140 . NS, FT

title: Intro to Abstract Algebra
description: Topics in concepts and methods of abstract algebra include groups, rings, integral domains and fields. An emphasis placed on the relationships among numbers and number systems, the relevance of algebraic properties in the number systems and operations. Prerequisite: MAT 205 . NS, FT

title: Mathematical Statistics I
description: Study of probability theory and introduction to mathematical statistics. Topics include random variables, central-limit theorem, hypothesis-testing, regression, analysis of variance and analysis of covariance. Prerequisites: MAT 115 , MAT 140 . NS, FT

title: Advanced Statistical Methods
description: The study, application and interpretation of information from the natural, physical, life, and social sciences, using advanced and multivariate statistical techniques. Methods include factorial and multivariate analysis of variance; covariance; multiple regression; path analysis, factor and discriminant analysis; and logistic regression. Students will apply these advanced methods in order to interpret the results of research studies. Access to SPSS is required. Prerequisite: MAT 115 or PSY-115 .

title: Calculus & Analytic Geometry III
description: Continuation of MAT 140 and introduction to multivariable calculus. Prerequisite: MAT 140 . NS, FT

title: Intro to Linear Algebra
description: Covers vector spaces, matrices, linear transformations, characteristic values and quadratic forms. Prerequisite: MAT 205 . NS, FT

title: Senior Research Seminar
description: Students explore primary scientific literature, develop a research project and conduct mathematical research or exploration. A substantial research paper and seminar presentation is required. Prerequisite: open to seniors, juniors by permission of instructor. WI

title: Public Memory/Public Art
description: Students analyze the intersection of public memory, public art, and visual rhetoric, and consider the ethics of memorials and museums. Substantial advance preparation is expected. Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Topics in Advanced Mathematics
description: Topics in advanced mathematics that are not covered in the regular curriculum, including, but not limited to, mathematical modeling, numerical analysis and partial differential equations.

title: Studio Practice I
description: Students explore content, context, form, structure, materials, and subject matter through creative practice and process in individual and collaborative assignments. Work-in-progress showings and Critical Response Process are included.

title: Introduction to Real Analysis
description: Metric spaces; limits and continuity; differentiation and integration; sequences and series of functions. Prerequisites: MAT 205 , MAT 242 . NS, FT

title: Site-Sensitive Performance
description: Students investigate environmental mediation in artmaking through the exploration of performance, place, and environment. Utilizing improvisation, structured collaboration, and ensemble building, students will devise and create original, dynamic, location-responsive work. Prerequisite: Admission to program

title: Higher Education Seminar I
description: Students prepare for the path from graduate student to tenured faculty by crafting application materials specific to academic job postings, and drafting course proposals, syllabi, and assessment rubrics. Students examine concerns such as Title IX, accreditation, and promotion and tenure, and engage as observer-participants in mock faculty interviews. Prerequisite: Admission to program

title: Mathematical Statistics II
description: Topics include introduction to limiting distributions, hypothesis-testing, analysis of variance and non-parametric models. Prerequisite: MAT 325 . NS, FT

title: Studio Practice II
description: Students engage in active artmaking, as well as self analysis and reflection, in their home-based studio practice, supported by studio mentors and Wilson faculty, in order to produce a semester portfolio pursuant to their own practice-led research and creative agendas.  Work-in-progress showings and Critical Response Process are included.  (3 sem. hrs.)  Permission of Director.

title: Studio Practice III
description: Students hone their active artmaking, self analysis and reflection, while deepening their home-based studio practice, supported by studio mentors and Wilson faculty, in order to produce a semester portfolio pursuant to their own refined practice-led research and creative agendas.  Work-in-progress showings and Critical Response Process are included.   (3 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: MFA-511

title: Contemporary Practices- Creative Writing
description: Students examine contemporary practices in writing-based art forms. May include both practice-led and academic scholarship. (3 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: Permission of the Director.

title: Research & Writing in the Arts
description: Students are introduced to the conventions of research and academic writing within higher education, with an emphasis on the visual, performing, and creative arts. Students will learn to critically engage with literature, synthesize diverse perspectives, and write for both scholarly and practitioner audiences, with a focus on developing practice-led research. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Aesthetics in Practice
description: Students examine and analyze the factors that have shaped their aesthetic values and hone their ability to articulate those values, interests, and goals verbally and in writing. Prerequisite: MFA-526 . (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Studio Practice IV
description: Students deeply explore content, context, form, structure, materials, and subject matter through creative practice and process, demonstrating a cohesive conceptual and technical approach to artmaking, in the creation of realized work. Work-in-progress showings and Critical Response Process are included. Prequisite: Permission of the Director.

title: Interdisciplinary Practice II
description: Guided by and in collaboration with faculty and guest artists, students explore a variety of art forms, media, and approaches to practice, process, creation, and meaning-making, culminating in the creation of new interdisciplinary/transdisciplinary works. Prerequisite: Permission of the Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Somatic Practice I
description: Students explore the role of body-mind connection and integration in artmaking and the impact of embodied experiences on artistic voice, perspective, and process. Through embodied exploration and research, students examine their art and practice in the context of human ecology.  (2 sem. hrs.)Prerequisite: Admission to program.

title: Interdisciplinary Collaboration I
description: Guided by and in collaboration with faculty and guest artists, students explore a variety of art forms, media, and approaches to practice, process, creation, and meaning-making, culminating in the creation of new interdisciplinary/transdisciplinary works.  Prerequisite: Admission to the program.

title: Somatic Practice II
description: Students expand and deepen their body-mind connection and integration, specifically as applied to their artmaking practices, exploring intentional somatic approaches to meaning-making. Continued embodied exploration and research is utilized to examine art and practice in the context of human ecology. Prerequisites: MFA-506

title: Thesis II
description: Students complete and present the thesis artwork with appropriate documentation, as well as an assessment portfolio. Students must demonstrate mastery of conception, intention, process, and production. An oral defense of the thesis is required. Prerequisites: MFA-598

title: Artmaking As Fieldwork
description: Students examine histories, theories, and practices in movement-based artforms through the lens of cultural anthropology. They conduct anthropological fieldwork and develop creative ethnographies. Emphasis is on dance theatre and devised performance. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Higher Education Seminar II
description: Students develop skills, materials, and practices necessary to navigate online and on-campus faculty interviews, including the articulation of their teaching philosophy and artistic practices through participation in mock faculty interviews, and the creation and delivery of specialized presentations and master classes. Prerequisite: MFA-507

title: Curation & Dramaturgy
description: Students engage in environmental mediation while investigating the impact that curatorial and dramaturgical practices have across various artforms. Prerequisite: Permission of Director. (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Educational Theory & Pedagogy in Arts
description: Students are introduced to foundational educational philosophies, theories, and pedagogical approaches in higher education and studio-based contexts. Through the lens of the visual, performing, and creative arts, students will critically examine teaching practices and develop their own teaching philosophy and artistic identity. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Music Through the Ages
description: Students will study the historical and cultural developments of Western classical music from Ancient to Modern times. Emphasis is placed on the development of musical form and style in its historical and cultural milieu. Prerequisite: Foundations in English course.     FWC, HWC, WI

title: Foundations of Music
description: Through a hands-on exploration of sound and its organization, students will engage with and experience the building blocks of music, its composition, and its performance. Essential rhythmic, melodic, and harmonic structures are studied, in addition to musical form and style through the voice and found and created instruments. ART

title: Health Assessment Clinical Experience
description: Clinical experience for NUR-101 : Health Assessment

title: Health Assessment
description: This course provides the framework for preparingstudents to perform comprehensive healthassessments on patients across the lifespan.Emphasis is placed on taking a thorough nursinghistory, performing physiological, psychological,sociological, cultural, and spiritualassessments, as well as identification ofstressors and health risks. Laboratoryexperiences provide an opportunity to practiceassessment skills on patients across the lifespanin a variety of settings. Course prerequisite:Admission into the generic BSN program;co-requisite: HSC 215 . (4 sem. hrs. - 3 hours didactic lecture) and 3 hours of  clinical per week)

title: Fundamentals of Nursing Practice
description: This course reviews comprehensive health  assessments including physiological,  psychological, sociological, cultural, and  spiritual assessments, as well as identification  of stressors and health risks. Roles of the nurse  in micro- and macrosystems, as well as  profession-related and patient care concepts to  provide safe, quality care are also covered.  Nursing process provides a decision-making  framework to improve clinical judgment skills.  Students review the knowledge, skills, and  attitudes needed to promote health and preventing  disease as patients strive to reach their optimal  state of wellness along with wellness as a means  of minimizing burnout and compassion fatigue.

title: Health Promotion and Wellness
description: The purpose of this course is to provide students the knowledge, skills, and attitudes needed to support patients in promoting their own health and preventing disease as they strive to reach their optimal state of wellness. Emphasis is placed on motivational theory to facilitate behavior change in relation to minimizing stress, maintaining a healthy weight and level of activity, and establishing healthy sleep patterns. Wellness for the student and practicing nurse are emphasized as a means of minimizing burnout and compassion fatigue. Prerequisite: NUR  101 (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Independent Study
description: Independent Study

title: Thesis I
description: Students pursue a sophisticated thesis question via rigorous practice-led and scholarly research and begin documenting their process in a context statement. Frequent interaction with Wilson faculty and studio mentors is required. Prerequisites: MFA-526 and MFA-533 .   (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Chronic Illness Clinical Experience
description: Clinical Experience of NUR-201 :  Chronic Illness in Adult Populations

title: Pharmacology for Healthcare Providers
description: This course introduces basic concepts of pharmacotherapeutics. The focus is on mechanics of drug administration, along with concepts related to safety and governmental regulations.  Specific emphasis will placed on measures to  ensure prevention of medication errors and the evolving science of pharmacology. Prerequisite: NUR-101 , Co-requisite: NUR-102 .

title: Chronic Illness - Adult Populations
description: This course focuses on the care of adult patients with health alterations that require medical and/or surgical intervention. Emphasis is placed on the care of patients with alterations in selected body functions. Concepts of patient centered care, cultural sensitivity, informatics, safe practice, and professionalism are integrated throughout the course. Clinical experiences provide the student an opportunity to apply theoretical concepts and implement safe patient care to adults in a variety of settings. Prerequisite: HSC-216 and NUR-131 ; co-requisite: NUR-231 (5 sem. hrs.)

title: Mental Health Clinical Experience
description: Clinical Experience for NUR 212 : Mental Health Nursing.

title: Population-Focused Care
description: This course is intended to introduce students to nursing care of individuals, families, aggregates, communities, and populations. Principles and practices of community health are discussed. Emphasis is placed on assessing factors that influence the health of populations and the use of evidence-based practices in the delivery of spiritually and culturally appropriate health promotion and disease prevention interventions. The role of the nurse as advocate for social justice is explored. Course prerequisite: NUR 361 . (2 sem. hrs.)

title: Foundational Concepts - Nursing Practice
description: This course provides an introduction to nursingand roles of the nurse in micro- and macrosystems,as well as profession-related and patient careconcepts. Emphasis is placed on the knowledge andskills needed to provide safe, quality care. The theoretical foundation for basic assessmentand nursing skills is presented, and the studentis given an opportunity to demonstrate theseskills in a clinical laboratory  setting. Anintroduction to the nursing process provides adecision-making framework to assist students indeveloping  effective clinical judgment skills.This course also provides an introduction to the principles of pharmacology, including:pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, medicationinteractions and potential adverse medicationreactions. Emphasis is placed on drugclassifications and nursing care related tothe safe administration of medication to patientsacross the life span. Course prerequisite: NUR102; co-requisite: HSC 216 . (5 sem. hrs. - 3 hours of didactic (lecture) and 8 hours of clinical per week)

title: Intersection of Healthcare and Society
description: Examines the influence of healthcare professionals on Western society from the mid-1700's to modern times. Students will be asked to think critically about the changing role of women, contributions of healthcare providers to societal change, and the role of providers as change agents and their influence on western society. Pre-requisite: ENG 108 or its equivalent. HWC, WS, WI

title: Found Conc of Nursing Practice Lab
description: Clinical for Foundations Nursing Practice

title: Health Promotion for Health Providers
description: This course will focus on health promotion and illness prevention across the life span, including theoretical approaches to adherence. Students will identify interventions to support health promotion for individuals, families and/or selected age groups. Exploration of concepts such as alternative health strategies, health, exercise, screening, and wellness will be included.

title: Pharmacology for Healthcare Providers II
description: This is the second of two courses designed to provide nursing students with the necessary knowledge of pharmacotherapeutic agents used in the treatment of illness, health maintenance and promotion. Included are drug classifications, pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics. The course will also provide a brief overview of pharmacogenomics its impact healthcare practice. Emphasis is placed on the understanding of nursing responsibilities in safe administration and monitoring of pharmacotherapeutic agents. Prerequisite: NUR-102 and NUR-131 . Co-requisite: NUR-201 .

title: Healthcare Informatics
description: Introduces perspectives and concepts of the flow  of healthcare information, technologies and their  applications to patient care, and the electronic  health record in its many forms. Also included  are institutional policies important to  communication and documentation of health care  interventions with an emphasis on privacy, confidentiality, and  security. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Role Transitions
description: Focuses on the leadership roles of professional nurses, including the impact of health policy, healthcare financing, and legislative and regulatory authority on nursing practice and the healthcare delivery system. Collaborative practice will be examined with emphasis on professional values, accountability, role transition, and collegiality. Prerequisite: Admission to the RN-to-BSN program. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Childbearing/Families Clinical Experienc
description: Clinical experience for NUR-304 : Nursing Care of Childbearing and Child Rearing Families

title: Mental Health Nursing
description: This course focuses on the care of patientsacross the lifespan experiencing cognitive,mental and behavioral disorders. Emphasis isplaced on management of patients facing emotionaland psychological stressors as well as promotingand maintaining the mental health of individualsand families. Concepts of crisis intervention,therapeutic communication, anger management, andcoping skills are integrated throughout thecourse. The community as a site for care andsupport services is addressed. Clinicalexperiences provide the student an opportunity toapply theoretical concepts and implement safepatient care to patients in selected mentalhealth settings. Prerequisite: NUR 201 . (5 sem. hrs. - 3 hours of didactic (lecture) and 8 hours of clinical per week)

title: Nursing Care - Chronically Ill
description: This course provides an introduction to the  principles of pharmacology, including:  pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, medication  interactions and potential adverse medication  reactions. Emphasis is placed on drug  classifications and nursing care related to the  safe administration of medication to patients  across the life span. Altered processes of human  physiology, biological process, effects on  homeostasis, clinical manifestations and  treatments are also covered along with concepts  of patient centered care, cultural sensitivity,  informatics, safe practice, and professionalism.  Prerequisite: NUR-105

title: Childbearing/Child Rearing Families
description: This course provides an integrative,  family-centered approach to the care of mothers,  newborns and children. Emphasis is placed on  normal and high- risk pregnancies, normal growth and development, family dynamics, common pediatric  disorders and the promotion of healthy behaviors  in patients. Clinical experiences provide the  student an opportunity to apply theoretical  concepts and implement safe patient care to  mothers, newborns and children in selected  settings. Course prerequisite: LPN to RN Program participants only.(5 sem. hrs.)

title: Nursing Care - Medical-Surgical Patients
description: This course focuses on the advanced concepts of nursing care as they relate to patients across the life span with complex, multi-system alterations including patients experiencing cognitive, mental, and behavioral disorders. Emphasis is placed on management of patients facing emotional and psychological stressors as well as promoting and maintaining the mental health of individuals and families. Emphasis is also placed on implementing time management and organizational skills while managing the care of patients with multiple needs and collaborating with the interdisciplinary team. Complex clinical skills, as well as priority setting, clinical judgment and tenets of legal and ethical practice are integrated throughout the course. Concepts of crisis intervention, therapeutic communication, anger management, and coping skills are also integrated throughout the course.  Prerequisite: NUR-205 .

title: Health Assessment
description: Provides students with the knowledge and skills necessary for the assessment phase of the nursing process including interviewing,history-taking, and physical assessment, which include assessment of individual health as a multi-dimensional, balanced expression ofbio-psycho-social-cultural well-being throughout the lifespan, across cultures, and within the environment. Prerequisites: NUR-281 with grade of C or better. (3 semester hours)

title: Adult Medical Surgical Concepts
description: This course focuses on the care of adult patients with medical and/or surgical health alterations. Emphasis is placed on the care of patients with alterations in selected body functions.  Concepts of health promotion, health education, evidence based practice, and interdisciplinary collaboration will be integrated throughout thecourse. Clinical experiences provide the student an opportunity to apply theoretical concepts and implement safe care to patients and selected groups in a variety of settings. Prerequisites: NUR-201 . (5 sem. hrs.- 3 hours of didactic (lecture) and 8 hours of clinical per week)

title: Pathophysiology for Nurses
description: This course focuses on the altered processes ofhuman physiology. An emphasis is placed onexploring changes of biological process of thebody and the effects on homeostasis. Alterationsof health problems are studied along with theassociated clinical manifestations andtreatments. Prerequisites: HSC-215 , HSC-216 , and AWS-105 . (3 semester hours)

title: Complex Care Across the Life Span
description: This course focuses on advanced concepts of nursing care as they relate to patients across the life span with complex, multi-system alterations in health. Emphasis is placed on implementing time management and organizationalskills while managing the care of patients with multiple needs and collaborating with the interdisciplinary team. Complex clinical skills,as well as priority setting, clinical judgment and tenets of legal and ethical practice are integrated throughout the course. Clinical experiences provide the student an opportunity to apply theoretical concepts and implement safe care to patients and selected groups in a variety of settings. Course prerequisite: NUR 361 (5 sem. hrs. - 3 hours of didactic (lecture) and 8 hours of clinical per week)

title: Complex Care Across the Life Span
description: This course focuses on advanced concepts of nursing care as they relate to patients across the life span with complex, multi-system alterations in health. Emphasis is placed on implementing time management and organizationalskills while managing the care of patients with multiple needs and collaborating with the interdisciplinary team. Complex clinical skills,as well as priority setting, clinical judgment and tenets of legal and ethical practice are integrated throughout the course. Clinical experiences provide the student an opportunity to apply theoretical concepts and implement safe care to patients and selected groups in a variety of settings. Course prerequisite: NUR-304A andNUR-305.  Limited to LPN to RN Programparticipants. (5 sem. hrs. - 3 hours of didactic (lecture) and 8 hours of clinical per week)

title: Childbearing/Families Clinical Experienc
description: Clinical experience for NUR-304A : Nursing Care of Childbearing and Child Rearing Families

title: Childbearing/Child Rearing Families
description: This course provides an integrative,  family-centered approach to the care of mothers,  newborns and children. Emphasis is placed on  normal and high- risk pregnancies, normal growth and development, family dynamics, common pediatric  disorders and the promotion of healthy behaviors  in patients. Clinical experiences provide the  student an opportunity to apply theoretical  concepts and implement safe patient care to  mothers, newborns and children in selected  settings. Course prerequisite: NUR  361,co-requisite: PSY 202 and HSC 209 . (5 sem. hrs.)

title: Complex Care Clinical Experience
description: Clinical experience for NUR-382 :  Complex Care Across the Life Span

title: Research in Healthcare
description: This course is designed to introduce students to  methods used in healthcare research. Students  examine and apply data to their professions with  an emphasis placed on the understanding of the  basic principles, techniques, and applications  involved with analysis, synthesis, and  utilization of data. Pre-requisite: MAT or PSY  115

title: Nursing Leadership
description: The purpose of this course is to provide the student the opportunity to function as a contributing member of the inter-professional team and collectively apply the knowledge and practice  to the skills acquired in previous courses. In addition, to the 80 hour clinical component of the course, the course will also  review the politics of healthcare within the context of the healthcare system.  Emphasis is placed on demonstration of professional behaviors; communication that supports information exchange; collaboration and conflict mediation; ethical comportment and the ability to effectively use clinical and  leadership skills. Current issues are critically analyzed in relation to their influence on the nursing profession and nursing practice. In addition, students will have the opportunity to prepare for employment as a professional nurse, appreciate the importance of lifelong learning, and prepare for the NCLEX licensing exam. Course  Prerequisite: NUR 382

title: Nursing Leadership
description: The purpose of this course is to provide the student the opportunity to function as a contributing member of the inter-professional team and collectively apply the knowledge and practice to the skills acquired in previous courses. In addition, to the 80 hour clinical component of the course, the course will also  review the politics of healthcare within the context of the healthcare system.  Emphasis is placed on demonstration of professional behaviors; communication that supports information exchange; collaboration and conflict mediation; ethical comportment and the ability to effectively use clinical and  leadership skills. Current issues are critically analyzed in relation to their influence on the nursing profession and nursing practice. In addition, students will have the opportunity to prepare for employment as a professional nurse, appreciate the importance of lifelong learning, and prepare for the NCLEX licensing exam. Course  Prerequisite: NUR 304A ; Course co-requisite: NUR 409AC .

title: Adult Med/Surg Clinical Experience
description: Clinical component of NUR-361 : Adult Med/Surg

title: RN-to-MSN Bridge Course
description: Focuses on the leadership roles of professional nurses, including the impact of health policy, healthcare financing, and legislative and regulatory authority on nursing practice and the healthcare delivery system. Examines collaborative practice with emphasis on professional values, accountability, role transition and collegiality; as well as the role of theory and research in nursing practice. Prerequisite: Admission to the RN-to-BSN or RN-MSN program. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Advanced Practice - Roles/Concepts
description: Focuses on socialization of nurses into advanced  practice through an investigation of nursing  history and advanced practice roles. Emphasizes  role development and regulatory and economic  policies that affect practice in today's  healthcare system, along with development of  skills in leadership to plan, manage and  implement change. Prerequisite: Admission to the  MSN program. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Ethics and Healthcare Regulations
description: Provides an examination of the healthcare policy processes and issues at organizational, governmental and global levels, as well as critical analysis of ethical principles, ethical theories and contemporary healthcare ethical issues relating to individuals and society. Emphasis is on developing an interdisciplinary framework to solving ethical dilemmas. Prerequisite: Admission to the MSN program. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Advanced Pathophysiology
description: Focuses on pathophysiological processes across the lifespan and development of clinical reasoning skills in understanding system alterations produced by disease. Emphasis is on the etiology, pathogenesis, developmental and environmental influences, and clinical manifestations of major health problems. Explores regulatory and compensatory mechanisms that aim at maintaining and restoring homeostasis. Prerequisite: Admission to the MSN program.

title: Informatics for Advanced Practice
description: Students apply concepts learned to the ethical use of data, information and knowledge in clinical practice and research, as well as explore the ways in which information and technology influence practice and decision- making in various aspects of nursing practice. Students also analyze the critical elements in the life cycle of information, patient care technology systems and the legal, ethical, cultural, economic and social factors affecting healthcare information technology. Prerequisite: Admission to the MSN program.

title: Advanced Pharmacology
description: Provides the opportunity to acquire advanced knowledge and systematic skills in the therapeutic use of pharmacologic agents and explores the pharmacologic treatment of major health problems encountered in the primary setting. Principles of altered pharmacodynamics will be discussed relative to age, race and ethnic groups. Prerequisite: Admission to the MSN program.

title: Complex Care Clinical Experience
description: Clinical experience for NUR-382 :  Complex Care Across the Life Span

title: Outcomes and Program Evaluation
description: Provides an introduction to theories and strategies of measurement and evaluation in nursing education. Experiential exercises increase proficiency in the development, use, and critique of measurement and evaluation methods to classroom and clinical learning situations. Includes steps in problem analysis and needs assessment, logistics of program evaluation and quality improvement. Prerequisite: Completion of core courses of MSN Program.

title: Fnd of Nursing Education and Curriculum
description: Provides in-depth examination of major philosophies of education and their relation to teaching practice, methods, curriculum and educational administration. The course provides opportunity for examination of factors that influence curriculum development and implementation. Prerequisite: Completion of core courses of MSN program.

title: Teaching Practices
description: Focuses on the role of the nurse as educator within clinical, classroom, laboratory, simulation and patient environments. Provides the opportunity to implement evidence-based adult teaching practices, philosophical perspectives and professional standards in areas of academia, staff development and/or patient education. Prerequisite: Completion of core courses of  MSN program. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Nursing Leadership Clinical Experience
description: Clinical experience for NUR-409A :  Nursing Leadership

title: Theory for Advanced Practice
description: Addresses nursing theory and its evolution in the  use of models/theories in advanced practice.  Emphasis is on the analytic processes basic to  determining the adequacy of selected  models/theories, as well as development of an  understanding of leadership and change theory and  their applications to practice. Prerequisite:  Admission to the MSN program. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Healthcare Systems
description: Examines the settings and organizations where healthcare is delivered, focusing on the impact of the setting on patient, delivery models and personnel providing care. Emphasizes the role and structure of care in various settings. Prerequisite: Admission into the MSN program.

title: Healthcare Finance I
description: In the first of two courses designed to provide the nurse leader with an understanding of financial management techniques, the student will explore the unique problems facing healthcare in the financial relationships among hospitals and other providers. Budget, auditing, cost analysis, capital investment, and equity and debt financing decisions are included. Prerequisite: Admission into the MSN program.

title: Healthcare Finance II
description: The second of two courses offered in finance, this course continues the learning from Healthcare Finance I and emphasizes application of knowledge gained in the first course. Particular emphasis is placed on acquiring the necessary skills needed for fiscal management and budget development and oversight. Prerequisite: NUR 545 .

title: Health Assessment-Adv Practice Nurse
description: Explores the process through which the advanced practitioner uses comprehensive physical, psychosocial and cultural assessment across the lifespan to gather specific data relevant to common health problems. Within a holistic framework, students learn how to systematically collect, analyze, synthesize and document their findings. Prerequisite: Admission to the MSN program.

title: Quality & Safety in Healthcare
description: This course introduces the fundamentals of patient safety, evaluation of quality and quality measures, and principals of quality improvement. The course will challenge students to think in an interdisciplinary manner when problem solving for quality improvement and will provide exposure to national and global implications of quality and safety initiatives in the healthcare arena. Prerequisite: Completion of core courses of MSN Program.

title: Generating & Using Evidence in Healthcar
description: The purpose of this course is to understand how to  solve clinical questions and appraise research.  The use of quality improvement techniques,  including root cause analysis, will be explored  through application exercises. Students will  generate a clinical question, determine the  correct methodology for finding an evidence-based  solution, and begin to investigate a solution.

title: Research and Statistics for Nursing
description: Provides students with the knowledge and skills to use, interpret and evaluate the statistical analysis techniques that are frequently encountered in the clinical literature of nursing, medicine, psychology and epidemiology. Includes an understanding of descriptive statistics, probability, linear regression and epidemiological concepts. Prerequisite: MAT 115 or PSY 115 , Admission to the MSN program. (5 sem. hrs.)

title: Master's Project
description: A capstone course designed to demonstrate student learning and attainment of program outcomes. Students complete a project developed with their adviser that successfully integrates learning from the core courses, as well as within the specialty track. Prerequisites: NUR 586 & NUR 587 .

title: Human Development & Occupation
description: Students will examine occupational performance across the lifespan by exploring physical, social-emotional, behavioral, and cognitive development along with environmental and contextual factors influencing performance. Typical and atypical changes in normative life tasks and occupational roles in relationship to environment and culture are discussed.   Prerequisites:  Admission to program.

title: Functional Anatomy
description: Students will assess and differentiate anatomical landmarks in order to understand related function and dysfunction. The emphasis is on functional understanding of the clinical application and impact of common injuries and conditions related to bones, muscles, and peripheral nerves on occupation. The lab portion provides students with hands-on study of musculoskeletal structures.   Prerequisites:  Admission to program.

title: Human Resource Mgt in Healthcare Orgs
description: Examines the complexities and multiple issues involved in human resources management in healthcare organizations. Explores such issues as recruitment, retention,performance management, organizational development and employee relations. Emphasizes federal, state and professional regulatory requirements specific to healthcare. Prerequisite: Admission into the MSN program.

title: Research I: Founds to Scholarly Review
description: Students will be introduced to research principles and evidence-based practice (EBP). The student learns to frame EBP questions, explore peer-reviewed research leading to a literature review, understanding of ethics and approaches to scientific inquiry. The course will culminate with developed research designs in an Institutional Review Board (IRB) proposal.   Prerequisites:  Admission to program.

title: OT Interventions I: Psycho Soc
description: Students will examine historical and current OT models to provide interventions for psychosocial and community challenges. It emphasizes task and activity analysis, reflective video analysis, and faculty-guided experiences to develop effective interventions. This course includes an experiential lab component. Prerequisites: OTD-600 , OTD-602 , OTD-603 and OTD-604 .

title: Fieldwork Seminar 1A: Psycho Soc
description: Students will be offered fieldwork experiences in psychosocial and community settings. Students will participate in the screening, evaluation, and intervention of populations with behavioral impairments affecting occupational performance. The primary focus will be on psychosocial factors that influence engagement and participation in occupation, enriching the concepts explored in concurrent courses. Prerequisites: OTD-600 , OTD-602 , OTD-603 and OTD-604 .

title: Introduction to Research Methodologies
description: The purpose of this course is to introduce  graduate students to qualitative and quantitative  methods for conducting meaningful inquiry and  research. Content covers commonly used data  analysis techniques for both methods as well as  key issues in the design of qualitative and  quantitative research.

title: Leadership in Nursing
description: Focuses on organization theory, management theory and their applications to critical nursing administrative leadership issues. Standards and frameworks of competencies in nursing administration are reviewed as guidelines for career development. Explores evidence-based management and promotion of a culture of safety as a basis for health-related organizational  development. Prerequisite: Admission into the MSN program. (3 sem. hrs.)

title: Neurological Foundations
description: Students will examine the theoretical explanations of occupational choices using neuroscience as a context. Contemporary concepts of brain-function that support occupation are explored with emphasis on sensory, perception, and motor and cognitive processes. Lab activities emphasize elements of the neurologic examination with an introduction to standard measures and tools for assessment. Prerequisites: OTD-610 , OTD-611 , OTD-615 , OTD-617

title: Wellness and Health Promotion
description: Students will explore the role of occupational therapy in public health, wellness, and health promotion. Students will examine public health policies, develop occupation-based environments using health assessments, and understand the impact of business practices on wellness programs. Key topics include service competency, professional development, ethics, and community engagement. This course includes an experiential lab component. Prerequisites: OTD-600 , OTD-602 , OTD-603 and OTD-604 .

title: Research II: Method, Design, Analysis
description: Students will conduct a research study which will include an examination of research and its relationship to areas of practice and practice assumptions. The student acquires concepts pertaining to the implementation of research methodology, data analysis, and synthesis of quantitative and qualitative data experience. Students learn to effectively disseminate research outcomes.  Prerequisites: OTD-610 , OTD-611 , OTD-615 , OTD-617

title: Kinesiology and Occupation
description: Students will be provided with a fundamental knowledge of the structure/function of human movement and their relationship to each other. Course content applies knowledge of human anatomy to occupational performance and includes assessments related to palpation, muscle testing, and goniometry with a focus on analyzing the impact of dysfunction on occupational performance. This course includes an experiential lab component. Prerequisites: OTD-610 , OTD-611 , OTD-615 , OTD-617

title: Foundations in OT Practice
description: Students will examine the historical foundations, philosophical base, core values, and code of ethics of the profession. Occupation-based models of practice and the Occupational Therapy Practice Framework (OTPF) are examined with a focus on the domain. Professional development and the transition to professional roles is facilitated. This course includes an experiential lab component. Prerequisites:  Admission to program.

title: Musculoskeletal Rehabilitation
description: Students examine the etiology, symptoms, and interventions of various disabling musculoskeletal conditions commonly treated by occupational therapy. Topics include assessment of muscle function, range of motion, joint mobilization, sensation, pain, edema, and wounds. Intervention practices pertaining to musculoskeletal conditions will be examined. This course includes an experiential lab component. Prerequisites: OTD-622 , OTD-624 , OTD-625 , OTD-629

title: Theories and Models II: Application
description: Students will focus on the models and frames of reference that shape occupational therapy practice in relationship to engagement in occupation. The student participates in the critique and discussion of the theoretical perspectives commonly used in occupational therapy practice and examines the role of theory in the clinical decision-making process as it relates to clients across the lifespan and varied conditions. Prerequisites: OTD-622 , OTD-624 , OTD-625 , OTD-629

title: Fieldwork Seminar 1C: Children
description: Students will be provided fieldwork in settings for children and youth, where students will screen, evaluate, and intervene with populations facing various impairments. The emphasis will be on developmental factors that influence engagement and participation in occupations, reinforcing concepts explored in concurrent courses. Prerequisites: OTD-630 , OTD-631 , OTD-635 , OTD-637

title: Theories and Models of Practice I
description: Students will examine core theoretical concepts and professional reasoning through completion of an occupational profile, analyzing activities and occupations, and creating intervention plans using a variety of models of practice and frames of reference.   Prerequisites: OTD-600 , OTD-602 , OTD-603 and OTD-604 .

title: Fieldwork Seminar 1B: Phys Dis
description: Students will participate in fieldwork in physical disabilities settings, where students will screen, evaluate, and intervene with populations experiencing neurologic, orthopedic, or motor impairments. The focus will be on physical factors affecting engagement and participation in occupations, reinforcing concepts from concurrent courses. Prerequisites: OTD-622 , OTD-624 , OTD-625 , OTD-629

title: Neurorehabilitation
description: Students will implement occupation-based theories and evidence-based approaches to neurological injuries and conditions. Neurological diagnoses including cerebral vascular accidents, traumatic brain injuries, spinal cord injuries, and degenerative diseases are emphasized. Neurological evaluation and treatment of impairments related to cognition, communication, dysphagia, visual-perceptual, and motor control will be presented. This course includes an experiential lab component. Prerequisites: OTD-630 , OTD-631 , OTD-635 , OTD-637

title: Technological Intervention in OT
description: Students will receive comprehensive instruction and hands-on activities within an experiential lab component for the acquisition of foundational competencies related to technological interventions. Instruction will include theoretical frameworks and the assessment, design, adaptation, modification, and monitoring of assistive technologies. The interventions will support activities of daily living, mobility, communication, social interactions, and executive functions. Prerequisites: OTD-640 , OTD-641 , OTD-642 , OTD-648

title: Instructional Strategies
description: Students will examine best practices and evidence in occupational therapy associated with teaching and learning. Teaching strategies are explored across a wide range of practice settings based on population, consumer needs, contexts, roles, task demands, resources, and expected outcomes. Methods for professional presentations and interprofessional teaching are highlighted. Prerequisites: OTD-630 , OTD-631 , OTD-635 , OTD-637

title: Professional Development
description: Students will be prepared for professional clinical practice as a lifelong learner and educator in the occupational therapy profession. The course examines ethical decision making, personal and professional responsibilities, professional development, professional engagement, continuing competence, application of scholarship, and accountability and advocacy for self, the profession, and the community. Prerequisites: OTD-610 , OTD-611 , OTD-615 , OTD-617

title: OT Interventions II: Phys Dys
description: Students will focus on evaluation and interventions for adults with physical impairments using the International Classification of Impairments, Disabilities, and Handicaps (ICIDH) framework. It highlights screening, planning, and applied treatment through occupation-based practice, along with acquisition, restorative, and compensatory strategies. Students engage in the OT process to enhance occupational performance across therapy domains, with a focus on rehabilitation. This course includes an experiential lab component. Prerequisites: OTD-622 , OTD-624 , OTD-625 , OTD-629 .

title: Case Management in Client Care
description: Students will cover basic principles of health care systems and transitional and related services. The student learns to integrate knowledge of delivery models, reimbursement, policies, and systems to make clinical decisions for monitoring and planning for services through application of theory, ethics, and evidence-based reasoning.  Prerequisites: OTD-640 , OTD-641 , OTD-642 , OTD-648

title: Level 2 Fieldwork A
description: This 12-week experience allows students to deliver occupational therapy services while developing entry-level skills. It emphasizes clinical reasoning and reflective practice through client-centered, meaningful interventions, enabling students to apply their theoretical knowledge in diverse occupational therapy settings. Prerequisites: Permission of Department

title: OT Intervention III: Children
description: Students will focus on OT theory and competency-based assessments and interventions for physical, developmental, sensory, cognitive, and psychosocial impairments in children, youth, and their families. It emphasizes applying pediatric frames of reference to specific challenges within a multicultural family context. This course includes an experiential lab component. Prerequisites: OTD-630 , OTD-631 , OTD-635 , OTD-637

title: Professional Leadership & Advocacy
description: Students will cover the principles of leadership and advocacy essential for individual and professional growth. Knowledge and skills to advocate for clients, programs, and the profession of OT are integrated. Topics and methods of advocacy that promote the role of occupational therapy in addressing societal and leadership needs are explored. Prerequisites: OTD-640 , OTD-641 , OTD-642 , OTD-648

title: Doctoral Mentorship I
description: Students will be assisted in developing a scholarly doctoral capstone experience and project. It is facilitated by utilizing a thorough PICO (population, intervention, comparison, and outcome) question, literature review, needs assessment, learning objectives, identification of a plan for project evaluation and student supervision culminating in a capstone proposal. Prerequisites: OTD-624 and Permission of Department.

title: Doctoral Capstone Experience & Project
description: Students will participate in an experiential placement consistent with the student's interest, under the capstone team's guidance. It creates and enhances the student's professionalism and abilities allowing them to acquire advanced knowledge.
