The course will culminate with the dissemination of the student's Capstone Project to allow for scholarly contribution to the community partner site and/or the profession. Prerequisites: OTD-682 and Permission of Department

title: Professional Competencies
description: Students will be prepared for the NBCOT licensure exam and independent practice by reviewing occupational therapy knowledge and skills. Students will analyze key competencies for certification and licensure, while also exploring their future roles as fieldwork educators.  Corequisites: OTD-681 and Permission of Department

title: Orthotic Fabrication and PAMs
description: Students examine the theoretical foundations and evidence for selecting physical agent modalities and fabricating orthotics in occupational therapy practice. Students develop advanced clinical reasoning skills while demonstrating competence in evidence-based and client-centered practice. This course includes an experiential lab component.  Prerequisites: OTD-640 , OTD-641 , OTD-642 , OTD-648

title: Cardio Fitness
description: Exercise class designed to improve student levels of physical fitness. Fitness tests are administered before and after the course to monitor improvement. Students read various materials relating to diet and exercise. (two semester hours.)

title: Recreational Activities
description: Students are introduced to a variety of activities that can be played throughout their lifetime. Activities may include backyard games, hiking, swimming, self-defense and more. (two semester hours.)

title: Strength & Weight Training
description: Designed to improve students' current levels of physical fitness relative to muscular strength, muscular endurance, flexibility and body composition through a carefully designed and monitored personal weight training program. (two semester hours.)

title: Lifetime Sports
description: Students will participate in sports that can be played throughout their lifetime.  Sports may include volleyball, pickleball, golf, soccer, and more. (two semester hours.)

title: Level 2 Fieldwork B
description: This 12-week experience allows students to deliver occupational therapy services while developing entry-level skills. It emphasizes clinical reasoning and reflective practice through client-centered, meaningful interventions, enabling students to apply their theoretical knowledge in diverse occupational therapy settings. Prerequisites: Permission of Department

title: Yoga
description: Students will gain an understanding of the benefits of yoga and participate in organized yoga sessions. (two semester hours.)

title: Doctoral Mentorship II
description: Students will be assisted in the finalization of a scholarly doctoral capstone project with a focus on the methods and procedures unique to the capstone project. It culminates in a fully executed Experiential Plan and Written Agreement on implementation and evaluation reflective effort on issues related to occupational therapy practice/research. Prerequisites: OTD-672 and Permission of Department.

title: Functional Fitness
description: Students will participate in a fitness program designed to simulate daily movements and/or sport movements and intended to improve muscle fitness and core strength. (two semester hours.)

title: Personal Fitness
description: Designed to help students develop a fitness program based on personal goals, such as improving diet, losing weight, gaining strength and improving muscular and/or cardiovascular endurance. Assesses current levels of fitness and prescribes a program based on individual goals and personal assessments. (two semester hours.)

title: Special Program
description: This course option may be selected by students with physical limitations who wish to meet their physical education requirement through an individually designed personal program. (2 semester hours)

title: Ethical Issues Today
description: Focused study of pressing ethical issues of our day. Topics may include sex, love and friendship; war and peace; computers and technology; affluence and poverty; and globalization. CC, ETH, WI

title: Pilates
description: An exercise class designed to improve student's current level of physical fitness through participating in a variety of Pilates exercises. (two semester hours.)

title: Team Sports
description: Students will be introduced to competitive team sports like volleyball, basketball, baseball, softball, soccer to gain an understanding of the rules, skills and history of the sports.(two semester hours.)

title: Logic and Scientific Reasoning
description: An introduction to logic with special emphasis on  scientific reasoning and evidence-based  decision-making. NS.

title: Special Activity
description: Activity classes not otherwise offered through the physical education curriculum that are designed to meet special student and faculty interests such as skiing, squash, cross country, orienteering, bicycling, water aerobics and synchronized swimming. (two semester hours.)

title: Topics in Philosophy
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty which are not treated in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Ethics
description: Introduction to ethics with an emphasis on  identifying ethical values and formulating  ethical responses to complex circumstances.  ETH

title: Bioethics
description: Ethical issues in the biological sciences and medical technology: human experimentation, euthanasia, abortion, reproductive technology, genetic engineering, cloning and stem-cell research. ETH, WI

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Philosophical exploration of the nature and  existence of God, the problem of evil, and the  relationship between faith, science, and reason.  Prerequisite: One course in philosophy or  religion studies. FWC, FT

title: Racquet Games
description: Students will be introduced to several racquet sports including badminton, tennis, pickleball and table tennis. (two semester hours.)

title: Topics in Physical Education
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum. (two semester hours.)

title: Ancient & Medieval Philosophy
description: Explores major philosophical issues that have left  a lasting imprint on Western cultural heritage,  including detailed examination of the  philosophies of Plato, Aristotle, St. Augustine  and St. Thomas Aquinas. FWC, WI

title: Ethical Issues Today
description: Focused study of pressing ethical issues of our day. Topics may include sex, love and friendship; war and peace; computers and technology; affluence and poverty; and globalization. CC, ETH, WI

title: Environmental Ethics
description: Reflection on ecological issues in ethical and belief-based perspectives. Students examine selected problems related to our use and abuse of the natural environment, and come to terms with the values, motives and other forces that shape decisions here. ES, ETH, WI

title: World Philosophy
description: An introduction to philosophy, focusing on figures and texts of global origin and significance. Drawing from both western and non-Western sources, the course explores enduring contributions to thinking about the human condition. HWC, NWC

title: Ethics and Leadership
description: This course examines ethical theories and decision-making methods relevant to leadership, the virtues of leadership, and the role of leadership in cultivating ethical organizations and communities. Prerequisite: Foundations in English.  ETH

title: Modern Philosophy
description: Explores major philosophical issues that have left  a lasting impression on the Western cultural heritage, including detailed examination of the  thought of Descartes, Hume, Kant and Hegel. HWC, WI

title: Food Ethics
description: An exploration of ethical issues in our food  systems and personal dietary choices, including  food scarcity and hunger, over-consumption and  public health, and industrial animal and plant  agriculture. Course prerequisite: ENG Foundations  Course. ETH

title: Topics in Philosophy
description: Courses offered occasionally to enhance the offerings available to students.

title: Food Ethics
description: An exploration of ethical issues in our food  systems and personal dietary choices, including  food scarcity and hunger, over-consumption and  public health, and industrial animal and plant  agriculture. Course prerequisite: ENG Foundations  course and at least one PHI course at the 200  level. ETH

title: Private Values & Public Policy
description: Interplay between private persons, their beliefs and values, and the larger community with its customs and laws. Covers such topics as:  individualistic versus community values;  influences of individuals on public policy;  strategies of resistance, nonviolence, civil disobedience and political activism; ethical and legal issues in regulating variant behavior, high-risk activities and victimless crimes; privacy, civil rights, civic responsibilities and related issues;  minority protections; church-state relations;  role of personal beliefs in the public arena and  in schools. CC, ETH

title: Aesthetics
description: Advanced survey of classical and contemporary theories of the meaning and function of art (including visual art, literature, music, dance and other art forms), beauty and aesthetic value and the interpretive process. Prerequisite: One 200-level humanities course or permission of instructor. ART

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content, having an appropriate, complementary role within the student's academic program. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Topics in Philosophy
description: Courses offered occasionally to enhance the offerings available to students.

title: Business Ethics
description: Study of ethical issues that arise in business and the professions. Covers rational methods that can be devised for adjudicating disputes concerning such issues as corporate rights, responsibilities, environmental impact, consumer rights and the moral status of corporations. ETH

title: Senior Thesis
description: Philosophy Senior Thesis

title: Healthcare Ethics
description: A survey of ethical issues in national and international healthcare, intended for practitioner audiences working to develop ethical decision-making frameworks for the healthcare professions. Prerequisites: Foundations in English course. ETH

title: Aesthetics
description: Advanced survey of classical and contemporary theories of the meaning and function of art (including visual art, literature, music, dance and other art forms), beauty and aesthetic value and the interpretive process. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

title: Topics in Philosophy
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty which are not treated in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Physics I
description: The first course of a two-semester, algebra-based sequence designed primarily for students pursuing a premedical or science program. Topics include mechanics, fluids, sound and thermodynamics. With an emphasis placed on problem-solving and medical applications of physics. Laboratory experiments supplement and reinforce the lecture topics. Three hours lecture; three hours laboratory. Prerequisite: MAT 110 or equivalent. NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Philosophical exploration of the nature and  existence of God, the problem of evil, and the  relationship between faith, science, and reason.  Prerequisite: One course in philosophy or  religion studies. FWC, FT

title: Physics II
description: A continuation of PHY 101 covering the general topics of electricity magnetism, optics and atomic/nuclear physics. Three hours lecture; three hours laboratory. Prerequisite: PHY 101 . NSL (4 sem. hrs.)

title: American Government
description: Survey of U.S. federal government, particularly Congress, the Supreme Court and the presidency. Attention is also given to political parties, elections, interest groups and analysis of contemporary issues. CC

title: Congress & the Presidency
description: Covers the role of Congress and the president in  the policymaking process. Also explores  relationships with such external  publics as constituents, the bureaucracy, interest  groups, media and staff, as well as the extent  and limits of each institution's  powers. Discusses case studies involving foreign  or domestic policies. Prerequisites: PS 110 or PS  120. CC

title: International Relations
description: Examines the forces influencing relations of nations, as well as gencies, organizations and procedures devised to carry on such relations. Prerequisite: PS 110. NWC

title: Women in American Government & Business
description: Examines the changing role of women in the  contemporary American political and business environments, with emphasis on obstacles and opportunities for women today. Prerequisite: Foundations in English course. WS, WI

title: Ethics and Leadership
description: This course examines ethical theories and decision-making methods relevant to leadership, the virtues of leadership, and the role of leadership in cultivating ethical organizations and communities. Prerequisite: Permission of the Program Director.

title: Political Theory
description: The works of a variety of political theorists with special attention to contemporary global political thought. Prerequisite: 217:  PS, CJ, or SOC.  ETH, NWC

title: Public Policy
description: Examines the methods and substance of public  policy analysis. Where possible, the policy focus will be interdisciplinary. Possible topics include environmental policy, human reproductive technology and public policy, economic policy and criminal justice policy. Resources in surrounding areas used as appropriate. CC, ETH

title: Physics I Lab
description: Laboratory component of PHY101: Physic I.  Lab  Fee Required.

title: Politics in Comparative Perspective
description: Examines U.S. political systems in relation to  other political systems: modern and transitional,  eastern and western, democratic and  nondemocratic. Also explores  fundamental political problems: internal and  international order, the establishment  of authority, resolution of conflict, violence and  politics, political socialization and capacity  for change. Prerequisite: Foundations in English  course. NWC

title: Physics II Lab
description: Laboratory component of PHY 102 : Physics II. Lab Fee Required.

title: American Constitutional Law in His Persp
description: Analysis of the Supreme Court as a political institution and its role in defining and interpreting the extent and limits of governmental power. Prerequisite: 218: Foundations in English course; 318: Foundations in English course and any course in PS, HIS, GS, CJ, or SOC. HWC, ETH, WI

title: Topics in Political Science
description: A course offered as needed to cover topics of  interest to students and faculty that are not  covered in depth in the  regular curriculum.

title: Europe in Contemporary World
description: Major historical, political, economic, and social developments, institutions, and issues shaping contemporary Europe since 1945. Prerequisite for 314: any 200--level course in Political Science. HWC, WI

title: Europe in Contemporary World
description: Major historical, political, economic, and social developments, institutions, and issues shaping contemporary Europe since 1945. Prerequisite for 214: Writing Foundations Course. HWC, WI

title: Political Theory
description: The works of a variety of political theorists with special attention to contemporary global political thought. Prerequisite: 217: Foundations in English course. 317: Foundations in English course and any 200-level course in PS, CJ, or SOC.  ETH, NWC.

title: Comparative and Regional Studies
description: This course introduces students to the major historical, political, economic, and social developments, institutions, and issues shaping the region. Prerequisite: Foundations in English  course and any 200 level course in History or Political Science. NWC

title: Internship
description: Work experience with a significant academic component. Students will complete a portfolio that exhibits both a visual and written display of learning, including evidence of theoretical knowledge and experiential learning. The portfolio may be used as a record of the student's work for graduate school or employment. A presentation to the college community is also required. Prerequisite: second-semester junior or senior if used to fulfill capstone requirement.

title: Topics in Political Science
description: A course offered as needed to cover topics of  interest to students and faculty that are not  covered in depth in the  regular curriculum.

title: Comparative and Regional Studies
description: This course introduces students to the major historical, political, economic, and social developments, institutions, and issues shaping the region. Prerequisite: Foundations in English  course. NWC

title: Topics in Political Science
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty which are not treated in the regular curriculum.

title: Introduction to Psychology
description: Introduction to the science of cognition and behavior. Topics include the biological foundations of cognition and behavior, sensation, perception, learning, memory, motivation, emotion, lifespan development, personality and adjustment, social interactions, and cognitive and behavioral abnormalities. CC

title: Understanding Statistics
description: Introduction to statistical procedures and their  application to research in the behavioral  sciences. Topics include descriptive  and inferential statistics, measures of central  tendency, variation, standardized distributions,  correlation, regression and prediction and hypothesis testing, including one- and two-way analyses of variance. Prerequisite:  MAT 098 or math placement exam. NS

title: Public Policy
description: Examines the methods and substance of public  policy analysis. Where possible, the policy focus will be interdisciplinary. Possible topics include environmental policy, human reproductive technology and public policy, economic policy and criminal justice policy. Resources in surrounding areas used as appropriate. CC, ETH

title: American Constitutional Law in His Persp
description: Analysis of the Supreme Court as a political institution and its role in defining and interpreting the extent and limits of governmental power. Prerequisite: 218: Foundations in English course; 318: Foundations in English course and any course in PS, HIS, GS, CJ, or SOC. HWC, ETH, WI

title: Abnormal Psychology
description: Overview of the psychosocial and biological models of mental disorders in adults, and how such disorders are defined, understood and studied. Includes coverage of anxiety and mood disorders, schizophrenia and adjustment and personality disorders, among others. Special attention is paid to the interaction of sociocultural variables such as poverty, race, age, ethnicity, class,  subcultural and gender role expectations with the incidence, labeling and treatment of neuroses and psychopathological conditions. Prerequisite: PSY 110 . CD

title: Life-Span Development
description: Explores human development from conception to death. Topics include: philosophical and scientific views of the life cycle; and biological, cognitive, social, emotional and personality themes of development during childhood, adolescence and adulthood. Prerequisite: PSY 110 . CC

title: Social Psychology
description: Examination of the ways in which social stimuli  affect the thoughts, motivations and behaviors of  individuals. Topics include self-perception,  impression management, social power and  influence, attitude formation and change and  interpersonal relations. Prerequisites: PSY 110 ,  CC

title: Learning Principles and Applications
description: Examines the principles governing learning and conditioning including acquisition, extinction, stimulus generalization and discrimination, schedule effects, avoidance and punishment, and the systematic application of learning principles in the modification of behavior. Prerequisite: PSY 110 . NS

title: Senior Thesis
description: In conjunction with a faculty adviser, the student will design and complete a major research project and compile a portfolio. Prerequisite: senior major.

title: Psychology of Human Sexuality
description: Psychological perspectives on the processes of sexual development and differentiation and the link between sex/sexuality and gender role identity. A bio-psychosocial approach will be used to explore theory and research on sexuality and sexual expressions across the lifespan, socially, and cross-culturally. Prerequisite: PSY 110 or SOC 120 . CD, WS

title: Industrial & Organizational Psychology
description: Introduces students to concepts about how people relate to and understand work. This is a survey course that covers topics such as job analysis and performance, training, recruitment, motivation to work, stress and employee well-being, leadership, and team management.   Prerequisite: PSY 110 or BUS-124 . CC

title: Psychology of Gender
description: Examines the development and maintenance of gender role identities across the lifespan.  Analyzes the "nature versus nurture" literature regarding identity development as female and male and how this identity influences behavior.  Considers the influences of biology, social and cultural expectations on the development and expression of masculine and feminine traits. PSY-110 or SOC-120 . CD, WS

title: Topics in Psychology
description: Examines subject areas within the discipline of  psychology. Offered on an occasional basis to  enhance the curriculum. Prerequisites: PSY 110 and permission of instructor.

title: Health Psychology
description: Incorporates a bio-psychosocial approach to understanding human wellness and health-promoting and health-compromising behaviors. Considering individual cognition, social factors, and the main systems of the human body, theories used by health psychologists are used to explore topics such as stress, chronic illness/disease, pain and lifestyle behaviors, and are applied to the promotion and maintenance of health and the prevention and treatment of illness.  Prerequisites: PSY-110 . NS, CD

title: Theories of Personality
description: Examines the development of modern approaches to the understanding of personality. Detailed comparisons of the major theoretical systems proposed to explain personality structure and dynamics. Prerequisite: PSY 110 . HWC

title: Sensation and Perception
description: Examines the basic anatomical structures and physiological processes of sensation that underlie perception to demonstrate how information is perceived by the human organism. Topics include vision, hearing, touch, taste, smell, movement, attention, and speech. Prerequisites: PSY 110 and PSY 218 or BIO 102 or BIO 110 . NS

title: Evolutionary Psychology
description: Examines the basic mechanisms of evolution and their influence on cognition and behavior. Topics include perception, attention, learning, memory, emotion, mating and parenting strategies, kinship and social interactions, aggression, competition, altruism, cooperation, and cognitive and behavioral abnormalities. Prerequisites: PSY 207 or PSY 217 or PSY 218 . NS

title: Experimental Methods in Psychology
description: Combines theory and practical application of the principles of experimental design, hypothesis-testing and statistical inference, including correlational and quasi-experimental techniques. Incorporates an introduction to the use of computer software for statistical analyses. Three hours lecture; two hours laboratory. Prerequisites: PSY 115 and at least two classes at or above the 200 level, or permission of instructor.

title: Psychology Research Lab
description: Current research and methodologies are stressed and used to guide students in writing a research paper (literature review and methodology) using constructs from Social Psychology. (Can only be taken concurrently with PSY 217 or if PSY 217 has been completed. If a non-psychology major enrolls in PSY 217 , enrolling in this lab course is optional.) WI (1 sem. hr.). Prerequisite: PSY-115

title: Experimental Methods Psych Lab
description: Principles of experimental design, hypothesis-testing and statistical inference are stressed and used to guide students in writing a proposal for research to be conducted in PSY 498 and PSY 499 . (Can only be taken concurrently with PSY 341 or if PSY 341 has been completed. If student is does not intend to enroll in PSY 498 / PSY 499 , enrolling in this lab course is optional.)

title: Biopsychology
description: Examines the biological structure/function of cognition and behavior. Topics include: basic anatomy and physiology of the brain/body, behavioral genetics, sensory and motor systems, learning, memory, reproduction, emotions, social interaction, and higher cognitive functions/dysfunctions. Prerequisite: PSY 110 . NS

title: Advanced Seminar
description: The Advanced Seminar serves as a culminating experience for psychology majors, encouraging a synthesis of psychological knowledge, further development of research skills, and fostering professional growth. Students will explore current themes and directions of psychology, write an APA-style review paper about an area of career interest, explore academic and professional futures, and create a professional portfolio. Prerequisite: PSY-341 . WI

title: Clinical & Counseling Psychology
description: Treatment of the individual experiencing psychological distress. Topics include theories of psychotherapy and counseling, ethics, individual and group work, and basic counseling skills. Prerequisite: PSY 209 .

title: Topics in Psychology
description: Examines subject areas within the discipline of  psychology. Offered on an occasional basis to  enhance the curriculum. Prerequisites: PSY 110 and permission of instructor.

title: History & Systems of Psychology
description: Examines the historical origins and evolution of the field of psychology as exemplified in the works of the great philosophers/psychologists from Plato to the present. Prerequisites: PSY 110 , at least two PSY 2XX, junior or senior class standing, and must be a psychology major or have instructor permission

title: Theories of Personality
description: The development of modern approaches to the understanding of personality. Detailed comparisons of the major theoretical systems proposed to explain personality structure and dynamics. Prerequisite: a 200-level PSY course. HWC

title: Ethical Issues Today
description: Focused study of pressing ethical issues of our day. Topics may include sex, love and friendship; war and peace; computers and technology; affluence and poverty; and globalization. CC, ETH, WI

title: Senior Thesis
description: Second of two-semester sequence. This sequence of courses will focus on the application of principles of research design culminating in an empirical research study. In this course, students will analyze data and present their empirically based findings through oral and written presentations. Completion of this sequence will fulfill one WI requirement. (2 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: PSY 498 .

title: Bible: From Paradise to Apocalypse
description: What does the Bible really mean? Students will learn how to interpret the Bible and its foundational place in western culture by drawing on historical criticism and literary studies. FWC, LIT, WI

title: Religions of the World
description: Introduction to major beliefs that have shaped the world in which we live. Seeks to understand differences of viewpoint that fuel misunderstanding and tensions today. Highlights symbols of major religions and their origins, especially those affecting Middle Eastern hot spots (Islam, Judaism and Christianity) in comparison with Asian traditions (Hindu, Buddhist, Taoist, Shinto). Addresses possibilities for interfaith understanding. NWC, WI

title: Theories of Psychotherapy
description: Focuses on operational theories of personality.  Examines the currently most accepted theories regarding behavior change, both classical and contemporary viewpoints. Prerequisite: PSY-209 or PSY-204 /304.

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Philosophical exploration of the nature and  existence of God, the problem of evil, and the  relationship between faith, science, and reason.  Prerequisite: One course in philosophy or  religion studies. FWC, FT

title: Cognitive Psychology
description: Examines the psychological processes behind the acquisition, storage, and use of information.  Topics include cognitive neuroscience, lower order cognitive processes such as sensation, perception, and attention, and higher order cognitive processes such as memory, judgment and decision making, and problem solving. Prerequisite: a 200-level PSY course. NS

title: Private Values & Public Policy
description: Interplay between private persons, their beliefs and values, and the larger community with its customs and laws. Topics individualistic  versus community values; the influences of individuals on public policy; strategies of resistance, nonviolence, civil disobedience and political activism; ethical and legal issues in regulating variant behavior, high-risk activities and victimless crimes; privacy, civil rights, civic responsibilities and related issues;  minority protections, church-state relations;  role of personal beliefs in the public arena and schools. CC, ETH

title: Christianity
description: An introduction to the academic study of religion in general and the Christian religion in particular. The course is largely a historical study that traces the emergence of Christianity from its beginnings as a minority sect within 1st-century Judaism to its contemporary form as a global faith. Crucial moments to be examined include the Early Church, Medieval Church and the Protestant Reformation, and modern challenges to Christianity. The course is also a contemporary cultural study. Students will read primary and secondary materials from each of the three largest branches of Christianity: Eastern Orthodox, Catholicism and Protestantism. They will also conduct field research in which they observe communities from each branch. Prerequisite: One Foundations English course. FWC, HWC

title: Senior Thesis I
description: First of two-semester sequence for psychology majors. This sequence of courses will focus on the application of principles of research design culminating in an empirical research study. In this course, students will polish their research proposal started in PSY 341 through oral and written presentations and begin data collection. (2 sem. hrs.) Prerequisite: PSY 341 .

title: Women & Religious Traditions
description: Examines feminine aspects of Western traditions  and beliefs, including women's roles in cult and  culture of Old and New Testaments; the emerging Western Church and rival movements; feminine images for God from the earliest times; contemporary archetypal theories; women's roles in recent religious thought; and religious aspects of the contemporary women's movement. FWC, WS

title: Business Ethics
description: Study of ethical issues that arise in business and the professions. Rational methods that can be devised for adjudicating disputes concerning such issues as corporate rights, responsibilities, environmental impact, consumer rights and the moral status of corporations. ETH

title: Environmental Ethics
description: Reflection on ecological issues in ethical and belief-based perspectives. Students examine selected problems related to our use and abuse of the natural environment and come to terms with the values, motives and other forces that shape decisions here. ES, ETH, WI

title: Bioethics
description: Ethical issues in the biological sciences and medical technology: human experimentation, euthanasia, abortion, reproductive technology, genetic engineering, cloning and stem-cell  research. ETH, WI

title: Comparative Religious Ethics
description: Examines select moral issues from the standpoint of diverse religious identities, both western and non-western. Issues to be considered include such areas as family, sexuality, war and peace, and work. Attention will be paid to the role traditional authorities such as the Bible and the words of the Buddha playing in informing the moral reasoning of religious adherents. Foundations English Course.  NWC, ETH.

title: Topics in Religion Studies
description: Courses on current topics in religious studies are offered on an occasional rather than regular basis. Recent courses have included: Religion and Democracy, Christian Mysticism, New Religious Movements and Women and the Bible.

title: What Is Religion?
description: The course examines religion from multiple  perspectives, including anthropology, sociology,  psychology, philosophy and theology. CC, WI.

title: Martin Luther King Travel Seminar
description: The course examines the life and work of Martin Luther King, Jr. in the context of the history of slavery, segregation, and the struggle to overcome, especially the institutions of the Black Church and the civil rights movement. The  course requirements include a travel/service  component.

title: Science and Religion
description: Impact of scientific method on religious thought. Confrontation of ancient themes and scientific world views. Current discussions on newer science and religious beliefs (big-bang cosmology and creation, respectively); religious sources and ecological values; ways of knowing and believing; theories of revelation, encounter, inspiration and symbol. Relationships of beliefs to factual knowledge. HWC, FT

title: American Spiritualities
description: Introduction to the background and character of diverse religions and spiritual movements and their  impact on the formation of American society, values and politics. Prerequisite:  Foundations in English. HWC, CD, WI

title: Comparative Religious Ethics
description: Examines select moral issues from the standpoint of diverse religious identities, both western and non-western. Issues to be considered include such areas as family, sexuality, war and peace, and work. Attention will be paid to the role traditional authorities such as the Bible and the words of the Buddha playing in informing the moral reasoning of religious adherents. One course in PHI or RLS at the 200 level.  NWC or ETH.

title: Death and Dying
description: The course examines religious beliefs and practices about illness, death, and the afterlife. Close attention is given to how this knowledge might inform caregivers working in a diverse professional context. Religions to be considered include Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, as well as Chinese traditions.  NWC

title: Advanced SeminarýAdvanced Seminar
description: Advanced study of selected figures or topics in Philosophy and Religious Studies. Prerequisites: one course in Foundations English and one course in RLS or PHI at the 200 level or permission. ETH, HWC, WI

title: Politics of Jesus and Muhammad
description: An examination of the political interpretations of  the teachings of Jesus and Muhammad, with an  emphasis on recent interpretations in religious  ethics. ETH

title: Politics of Jesus and Muhammad
description: An examination of the political interpretations of  the teachings of Jesus and Muhammad, with an  emphasis on recent interpretations in religious  ethics. ETH

title: Topics in Religion Studies
description: Courses on current topics in religious studies are offered on an occasional rather than regular basis. Recent courses have included: Religion and Democracy, Christian Mysticism, New Religious Movements and Women and the Bible.

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Philosophical exploration of the nature and  existence of God, the problem of evil, and the  relationship between faith, science, and reason.  Prerequisite: One course in philosophy or  religion studies. FWC, FT

title: Advanced Seminar
description: Advanced study of selected figures or topics in Philosophy and Religious Studies. Prerequisites: one course in Foundations English and one course in RLS or PHI at the 300 level or permission. ETH, HWC, WI

title: What Is Religion?
description: The course examines religion from multiple  perspectives, including anthropology, sociology,  psychology, philosophy and theology. CC, WI.

title: Sourcing and Operations
description: This course teaches students the fundamentals of sourcing and operations. This course is divided into two parts: a) the key components of sourcing and b) the application of two operations methodologies.  Prerequisite: MAT 115 and SCM 301 .

title: Capstone - Supply Chain Management
description: This course challenges students to apply the supply chain management skills they have learned to a series of challenges. These challenges involve: logistics, operations, planning, sourcing in real world applications.  Prerequisite: MAT 115 , SCM 301 , and SCM 303 .

title: Topics in Sociology
description: A seminar course offered as needed to cover topics of interest to students and faculty which are not treated in the regular curriculum. Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

title: Environmental Sociology
description: Explores sociological and social scientific approaches to the study of reciprocal interactions between the physical environment and human societies. Topics include: political and economic dynamics of pollution and environmental regulations; social impact assessment and community response to toxic hazards; global trends in population growth, resource development and environmental degradation; alternative environmental futures and sustainable development. Prerequisite: SOC 120 . ES

title: Sociology of the Family
description: Examines the changing nature of the family in U.S.  society. Varied perspectives and empirical  studies are incorporated to facilitate an  understanding of the transitions taking place in  the areas of gender roles, coupling and  parent-child relationships. This course takes a  multicultural approach and addresses historical,  political, and economic factors that shape family  formation. Prerequisite: SOC 120 . CC

title: Social Problems & Inequalities
description: Applies theories of inequality and stratification to the analysis of the structural basis of social problems. Combines macro-level economic and social analysis with practical examples of problems faced by communities locally, nationally and globally. Explores the relationship between social inequalities and problems such as poverty, crime, environmental crises and the impact of the industrial revolution and post-industrial society on work and unemployment. Prerequisite: SOC 120 . CC

title: Internship
description: Work experience with significant academic content, having an appropriate, complementary role within the student's academic program. Approval of the academic dean is required.

title: Martin Luther King Travel Seminar
description: The course examines the life and work of Martin Luther King, Jr. in the context of the history of slavery, segregation, and the struggle to overcome, especially the institutions of the Black Church and the civil rights movement. The course requirements include a travel/service component.

title: Deviance & Criminal Justice
description: Explores sociological theories and critical  perspectives on deviance and criminal behavior  are explored. Considers informal and formal methods of  social control including stigma, incarceration,  and institutionalization.   Critically analyzes  the impact of social institutions and  inequalities on individual,  governmental and corporate crime and deviance is critically analyzed. Prerequisite: SOC 120 . CC

title: Race, Class and Gender
description: Analyzes the ways in which social categories such as race, ethnicity, sexuality, socioeconomic class and gender intersect and organize social relations. Using comparative and historical perspectives on group formation, immigration and conflict, social phenomena such as assimilation, ethnocentrism, racism and multiculturalism are examined. Prerequisite: SOC 120 . CD

title: Food, Culture, and Society
description: Explores the symbolic nature of food in society, and the social significance of  industrialized food production, particularly in the United States.  Students will critically examine our globalized food system and alternatives that have emerged to it. Service-learning experiences on Wilson's Fulton Farm and in local community organizations are required. Foundations English Course. CC, CD, WI

title: Topics of Sociology
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum with subjects of special interest (e.g., Sociology of Religion). Prerequisite: SOC 120 .

title: Social Movements and Social Change
description: Surveys the theoretical literature (collective behavior, identity politics, resource mobilization and new social movements) on social movement analysis within the context of studying specific social movements. Questions such as why people start, join and leave movements are addressed along with discussions of movement strategies for attracting adherents and for achieving social change. Movements across the political spectrum and throughout the globe are considered. Prerequisite: SOC 120 . CD

title: Introduction to Sociology
description: A general introductory course to the discipline of sociology that provides a broad overview of the field, its areas of study, methods of inquiry and theories of society. The central  objective of the course is to encourage students  to think sociologically. CC, CD

title: Forecasting and Logistics
description: This course teaches students the fundamentals of forecasting and logistics. This course provides a broad overview of the planning and execution of customer demand and is divided into two parts: forecasting and logistics.  Prerequisite: MAT 115 .

title: Environmental Sociology
description: Explores sociological and social scientific approaches to the study of reciprocal interactions between the physical environment and human societies. Topics include: political and economic dynamics of pollution and environmental regulations; social impact assessment and community response to toxic hazards; global trends in population growth, resource development and environmental degradation; alternative environmental futures and sustainable development. Prerequisite: SOC 120 . ES

title: Sociological Theory
description: Survey of theoretical perspectives that have guided sociological thought and inquiry. Considers both classical social theorists (e.g., Marx, Weber, Durkheim) and contemporary theoretical perspectives (e.g., feminist, postmodernist, deconstructionist). Prerequisite: SOC 120 and at least one 200-level sociology course. Prerequisite for 500-level: graduate standing/permission. HWC

title: Food, Culture, and Society
description: Explores the symbolic nature of food in society, and the social significance of  industrialized food production, particularly in the United States.  Students critically examine our globalized food system and alternatives that have emerged as a response to  it.. Service-learning experiences on Wilson's Fulton Farm and in local community organizations are required. Foundations English Course. CC, CD, WI

title: Internship
description: Work experience with a significant academic component. Students will complete a portfolio that exhibits both a visual and written display of learning, including evidence of theoretical knowledge and experiential learning. The portfolio may be used as a record of the student's work for graduate school or employment. A presentation to the college community is also required. Prerequisite: second-semester junior or senior if used to fulfill capstone requirement.

title: Advanced Seminar
description: The Advanced Seminar serves as a culminating experience for sociology majors, encouraging a synthesis of sociological knowledge, further development of research skills, and fostering professional socialization. Students will analyze and respond to foundational sociological texts, engage in designing a research project, and explore academic and professional futures. Prerequisite: SOC 380 . WI

title: Sociological Theory
description: Survey of theoretical perspectives that have guided sociological thought and inquiry. Considers both classical social theorists (e.g., Marx, Weber, Durkheim) and contemporary theoretical perspectives (e.g., feminist, postmodernist, deconstructionist). Prerequisite: SOC 120 and at least one 200-level sociology course. Prerequisite for 500-level: graduate standing/permission. HWC

title: Social Movements and Social Change
description: Surveys the theoretical literature (collective behavior, identity politics, resource mobilization and new social movements) on social movement analysis within the context of studying specific social movements. Questions such as why people start, join and leave movements are addressed along with discussions of movement strategies for attracting adherents and for achieving social change. Movements across the political spectrum and throughout the globe are considered.

title: Transforming Communities
description: Study of the practices, attitudes, and other factors capable of transforming communities marred by violence into flourishing communities. Topics include violence, nonviolence, community organizing.  Prerequisites:  Foundations in English.  ETH

title: Gender, Crime and Justice
description: This course analyzes how gender is expressed in laws, policing practices, and who perpetrates or is victimized by crime. An intersectional approach to violence against women and gender non-conforming groups also will be examined. Prerequisite: CJ 101 or SOC 120 . CC, WS

title: Low Incidence Disabilities
description: Focuses on teaching students with low incidence disabilities:  severe cognitive disabilities, low vision/blindness, physical/health disabilities and Traumatic Brain Injuries. Current research related to the academic/social issues related to teaching students with these disabilities will be examined. Prerequisite: EDU 215 .

title: Qualitative Methods and Social Research
description: Introduces students to basic research procedures  for collecting qualitative data in the social  sciences. Students learn to analyze qualitative  data, and to conduct archival research,  participant observation, and depth-interviews.   Considers theoretical underpinnings of  qualitative research with special attention to  research ethics and project design. Permission of the Director

title: High Incidence Disabilities
description: Focuses on teaching students with high incidence disabilities:  Learning Disabilities, Emotional/Behavioral Disorders, and Mild Intellectual Disabilities. Current research related to the academic/social issues related to teaching students with these will be examined. Prerequisite: EDU 215

title: Creating an Effective Classroom Culture
description: This course will survey instructional approaches, materials, and methods for classroom management, social/emotional learning, and trauma informed instruction. Emphasis will be placed on management styles and the various methods associated in all three areas of course focus. Prerequisite: EDU 206 .

title: Topics in Sociology
description: Courses offered on an occasional basis to enhance the curriculum with subjects of special interest (e.g., Sociology of Religion). Prerequisite: SOC 120 .

title: Secondary Instructional Methods
description: Evidence-based instructional methods and assessments for students (grades 7-12)with disabilities in academic, vocational, and social settings will be investigated and applied to student generated lesson plans, differentiated to meet the needs of students with disabilities.  Progress monitoring and data collection related to Individualized Education Plans (IEPs) will also be investigated. Student will complete observation hours. Prerequisites: SPE 216 or SPE 217 .

title: Qualitative Methods and Social Research
description: Introduces students to basic research procedures  for collecting qualitative data in the social  sciences. Students learn to analyze qualitative  data, and to conduct archival research,  participant observation, and depth-interviews.   Considers theoretical underpinnings of  qualitative research with special attention to  research ethics and project design. Prerequisite: SOC 120 and any 200-level course in a social  science discipline.

title: Reading/Writing Methods
description: This course teaches students how to apply best practices in reading/writing instruction for students with special needs. Students will develop appropriate lesson planning strategies related to differentiated instruction, assessment and pedagogy related to students with special needs. Students will complete observation hours. Prerequisite: EDU 238 and official acceptance into education program.

title: Math/Technology Methods
description: This course teaches students how to apply best practices in mathematics instruction and use of technology for students with special needs. The students will develop appropriate lesson planning strategies related to differentiated instruction, assessment, and pedagogy related to students with special needs. Students will complete observation  hours. Prerequisite: Official acceptance to  Education Program.

title: Foundations of Autism
description: This course will examine the characteristics and academic/social needs of students with autism.  Functional Behavioral Assessments, Social Skills strategies and progress monitoring of academic/social behaviors will be covered.  Current research and best practices related to working with students with Autism will be discussed. Students will complete observation hours. Prerequisite: SPE 216 or SPE 217 .

title: Special Education Pre-Practicum
description: A full-time clinical experience in a local school during the student's senior year. Placement will be in a special education setting. Requires observing, aiding, tutoring, teaching and maintaining a portfolio. Preparation for the senior year student teaching practicum. Supervised by college faculty. Prerequisites: 204, 215 and admission to the education program.

title: Advanced Professional Practicum
description: A period of guided teaching designed for experienced certified teachers who seek an additional instructional certification. Duration and specifications depend upon previous teaching experience and areas of certification. Supervised by a college supervisor and a master teacher in an accredited public or private school. Prerequisites: PA Instructional I or II certification, adviser approval. (6 sem. hours)

title: Early Intervention
description: This course provides an in-depth study of individuals with disabilities from birth to age 5. Typical and atypical child development theories will be studied. Methods of instruction/assessment and current research related to Early Intervention will also be examined. Course prerequisite/co-requisite: SPE 216 or SPE 217

title: Intern Teaching Practicum I
description: Combined with EDU/SPE5-509, this is a nine-month clinical experience for post-baccalaureate certification candidates in the Teacher Certification Pathways program who are employed in an accredited public or private school. Prerequisites: Intern certificate, adviser approval. (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Parent Conferencing/Collaboration SpEd
description: This course will explore research relevant to conferencing with parents of students with special needs.   Students will develop Individual Education Plans (IEPs) / Individualized Family Service Plans (IFSPs) for students with disabilities. Current trends and research related to advanced skills in collaboration with general education teachers, social service agencies, and other professionals will also be studied. Course prerequisite is one of the following: SPE 216 :High Incidence Disabilities; SPE 217 : Low Incidence Disabilities SPE 526 :Advanced Studies in High Incidence Disabilities or SPE 527 :Advanced Studies in Low Incidence Disabilities Or Hold a PA Level I or II Certification Or Hold a Bachelor Degree Or Permission of the Program Director

title: Creating an Effective Classroom Culture
description: This course will survey instructional approaches, materials, and methods for classroom management, social/emotional learning, and trauma informed instruction. Emphasis will be placed on management styles and the various methods associated in all three areas of course focus.  Prerequisite: EDU 206 . Waived for degree-seeking graduate education students.

title: Intern Teaching Practicum II
description: Combined with SPE-508 , this is nine-month clinical experience for post-baccalaureate certification candidates in the Teacher Certification Pathways Program who are employed in an accredited public or private school. Prerequisites SPE-508 . (6 sem. hrs.)

title: Advanced Studies in Disabilities
description: Students examine the characteristics, identification procedures, and current research related to students in the 13 disability categories: autism deaf-blindness, deafness, emotional disturbance, hearing impairment, intellectual disability, multiple disabilities, orthopedic impairment, other health impairment, specific learning disability, speech or language impairment, traumatic brain injury, or visual impairment (including blindness)

title: Adv Studies - Low Incidence Disabilities
description: Characteristics, identification, and current research related to students with Low Incidence Disabilities, including severe intellectual disabilities, low vision/blindness, physical/health disabilities, and Traumatic Brain Injuries will be examined.  Two (2) hours of field experience within a school setting are required.  Requires permission of the appropriate Graduate Program Director.

title: Transition Planning & Services
description: This course will discuss the legal/ethical aspects of planning for students with disabilities transitioning from secondary to post-secondary life.  Current trends and research in transition planning and designing transition plans for students with various disabilities will be studied. Agencies related to life-long services for individual needs will also be explored. Prerequisites: SPE 216 or SPE 217 .

title: Parent Conferencing/Collaboration SpEd
description: This course will explore research relevant to conferencing with parents of students with special needs.   Students will develop Individual Education Plans (IEPs) / Individualized Family Service Plans (IFSPs) for students with disabilities. Current trends and research related to advanced skills in collaboration with general education teachers, social service agencies, and other professionals will also be studied. Course prerequisite is one of the following: SPE 526 , SPE 527 or SPE 521 Or Hold a PA Level I or II Certification Or Hold a Bachelor Degree Or Permission of the Program Director

title: Special Education Practicum (prek-12)
description: A 14-week clinical experience in local schools for students who pursue instructional  certification for special education. Supplemented with the Special Needs Seminar. Prerequisites: All professional coursework and permission  of adviser. (12 sem. hrs.)

title: Pre-K - 8 Instructional Methods
description: Evidence-based instructional methods and assessments for students (PreK-8) with  disabilities in academic, social and life skills settings will be investigated and applied  to student generated lesson plans, differentiated to meet the needs of students with disabilities.  Progress monitoring and data collection related to Individualized Education Plans (IEPs) will also be investigated. Students will complete observation hours. Course prerequisite/co-requisite: SPE 521 , SPE 526 or SPE 527 .

title: Secondary Instructional Methods
description: Evidence-based instructional methods and assessments for students (grades 7-12)with disabilities in academic, vocational, and social settings will be investigated and applied to student generated lesson plans, differentiated to meet the needs of students with disabilities. Progress monitoring and data collection related to Individualized Education Plans (IEPs) will also be investigated. Ten (10) hours of field experience in various instructional settings are required. Prerequisites: SPE 216 or SPE 526 or SPE 217 or SPE 527 or EDU 215 .

title: Positive Behavioral Supports
description: An in-depth study of classroom and behavioral management practices for students with disabilities. Emphasis will be placed on proactive approaches supported in the principles of Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA). Application of these principles will be applied through conducting Functional Behavior Assessments (FBAs) and writing effective Behavior Intervention Plans (BiPs). Three (3) hours of field experience are required. Prerequisites/Corequisites: SPE-521 , SPE-526 or SPE-527 .

title: Characteristics/Etiology of Autism
description: This course will explore the characteristics of individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) throughout their lifespan. Students will explore key theories and scientific hypothesis regarding the etiology of ASD.  Prerequisite:  Hold valid PA Instructional I or II teaching certificate, completed bachelor degree, or permission.

title: Adv Studies High Incidence Disabilities
description: This course provides an in-depth study of the characteristics, identification procedures, and current research related to students with High Incidence Disabilities, including Learning Disabilities, Emotional/Behavioral Disabilities, Speech/Language Disabilities, and Mild Intellectual Disabilities. Students will complete observation hours. Requires permission of the appropriate Graduate Program Director.

title: Prek-12 Special Ed Practicum
description: This course applies the knowledge, skills, and strategies to extensive field experiences in a variety of settings including community agencies, local education agencies and Intermediate Units under the supervision of  Wilson College faculty and qualified educators/service providers serving individuals from PreK- Grade 12. A minimum of 120 hours of field experiences is required.

title: Prek-8 Special Ed Practicum
description: This course applies the knowledge, skills, and strategies to extensive field experiences in a variety of settings including community agencies, local education agencies and Intermediate Units under the supervision of Wilson College faculty and qualified educators/service providers serving individuals from PreK- Grade 8. A minimum of 120 hours of field experiences is required. Prerequisites: Completion of all course work or corequisite SPE 598 .

title: Early Intervention
description: This course provides an in-depth study of individuals with disabilities from birth to age 5. Typical and atypical child development theories will be studied. Methods of instruction/assessment and current research related to Early Intervention will also be examined. Course prerequisite/co-requisite: SPE 526 , SPE 527 or SPE-521 .

title: Secondary Special Ed Practicum
description: The course applies the course work to extensive field experiences in a  variety of  settings including community agencies, local education agencies, and Intermediate Units under the supervision of Wilson College faculty and qualified educators/service providers serving individuals from grades 7-12.  A minimum of 120 hours of field experience is required. Prerequisites: Completion of all course work or corequisite SPE 598 .

title: Educational Research/Design
description: This course will examine exemplary literature and research projects that represent examples of the systematic collection, evaluation, presentation and interpretation of research data in education. Various research designs and methodologies will be explored, emphasizing action research done in educational contexts. Student research projects will reflect a specific educational research style, such as experimental, correlational, survey, grounded theory, ethnography, narrative or mixed methods. Students will be guided in the selection of an appropriate master's project topic and a review and critique of relevant literature. They will develop a comprehensive design proposal for a major research project.

title: Sport in Society
description: Students will explore the relationship between sport and society. Through interdisciplinary perspectives, students analyze current issues including society-imposed barriers, Federal legislation and media depiction of sport.  By engaging with readings, discussions, and writing reflection papers, students will develop a deeper understanding of sport in society. Prerequisite: SOC120 and ENG101, ENG108,ENG104 or ENG180 or permission of instructor. WS,CD, WI

title: Foundations - Sport Management
description: An introductory view of the skills and information necessary for a career in the sport industry. Topics covered reflect the core competencies for sport management, including management and leadership in the sport industry, sport finance, sport facility and event management, sport governance, ethics in the sport industry and more.

title: Instruct/Assess for Students W/Autism
description: This course investigates current instructional strategies and assessments for students with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD).  Students will learn to apply strategies and administer assessments to students throughout their lifespan and across all levels of the spectrum. Field experience is required for this course.  Prerequisite:  Hold valid PA Instructional I or II teaching certificate, completed bachelor's degree, or permission.

title: Introduction to Games
description: Of all the new media forms that have emerged since digital technologies have become ubiquitous in our social and cultural environment, videogames could be described as the form that is most fundamentally "native" to the computer. As a form of entertainment, they have introduced new relationships between audiences/players and the media with which they interact, and present unique challenges to producers, critics and the public at large.

title: Transition Planning and Services
description: It should be: This course will discuss the legal/ethical aspects of planning for students with disabilities transitioning from secondary to post-secondary life.  Current trends and research in transition planning and designing transition plans for students with various disabilities will be studied.  Agencies related to life-long services for individual needs will also be explored. Prerequisite: SPE-521 , SPE526 or SPE527

title: Convention, Event & Trade Show Planning
description: This course closely examines standards and  guidelines underlying operation of a fitness or  sport facility. Topics may include risk management; policy development; planning,  organizing and evaluating facilities; and  marketing, budgeting and public relations in facilities.

title: Distribution of Games
description: The role of a publisher in the games industry is to ensure that a game can get in front of itsaudience successfully. To do that, a publisher must consider a variety of distribution strategiesand channels. This course explains the role of a publisher in game distribution and details thevarious channels by which a game can be distributed.Prerequistes: SPM-120 and SPM-121

title: Leadership in Sport
description: Whether pursuing a career in coaching or as a  manager in sport, this course will introduce  students to the basic principles and foundations of leadership.  Case studies will be utilized to illustrate fundamental aspects of leadership, such  as creating a vision and overcoming obstacles.   Students will learn leadership theory, apply leadership concepts, and participate in practical strategies for becoming better leaders/coaches.

title: Master's Project
description: Represents the final stages in the completion of a Master's Project begun in SPE 598 , where action research is conducted and a master's paper is completed and presented.  Students will work closely with a research advisor to conduct the action research project and associated report. Prerequisite:  EDU/ SPE-598 .

title: Sport Facility Management
description: This course closely examines standards and  guidelines underlying operation of a fitness or  sport facility. Topics may include risk management; policy development; planning,  organizing and evaluating facilities; and  marketing, budgeting and public relations in facilities.

title: Introduction to Esports Management
description: Between 2018 and 2019, the number of jobs in esports nearly doubled-growing a staggering 185%. In addition, there are numerous business jobs elsewhere in the games industry, including roles in distribution, research, sales, and marketing. The explosive growth in esports-and the attendant growth within the games industry which it has field-has created a demand for business professionals with a solid understanding of the nuances of the industry. This course provides students with an understanding of the complexities specific to managing esports teams and leagues.

title: Psychology of Sport
description: The course will provide an overview of the psychological principles as they relate to sport, coaching and exercise; and will give students an understanding of human behavior and performance in sports situations. Topics will include theoretical foundations of behavior, neurophysiological responses to stress and exercise, group and team dynamics, adherence and motivation, practical application of mental training strategies, etc.

title: Elementary Spanish I
description: Designed to meet the needs of beginners who wish to master basic structures and vocabulary and become generally acquainted with the culture and civilization of the Hispanic world. Attention paid to understanding, speaking, reading and writing skills. Five hours per week.

title: Elementary Spanish II
description: Designed to meet the needs of beginners who wish to master basic structures and vocabulary and become generally acquainted with the culture and civilization of the Hispanic world. Attention paid to understanding, speaking, reading and writing skills. Five hours per week. Prerequisite: SPN101

title: Intermediate Spanish
description: Intensive study of Spanish at the intermediate level including conversation, listening, vocabulary, reading and cultural understanding with an emphasis on grammar and writing. Three hours lecture, one hour language lab ( SPN-205LL ). (3 semester hours) Prerequisite: SPN 102 or placement. Corequisite: SPN-205LL .

title: Hispanic Literatures
description: Serves as an introduction to the literatures of the Hispanic world (Spain, South America, Central America, Mexico, the Caribbean and the U.S.). Prerequisite: SPN 209 . LIT

title: Hispanic Cultures
description: This course serves as an introduction to the cultures of the Hispanic world (Spain, South America, Central America, Mexico, the Caribbean and the United States). Prerequisites: SPN-209 . HWC, NWC

title: Internship
description: Sport Management majors can select an internship experience that meets their individual career and educational goals. This work experience, with accompanying academic content, plays a complimentary role within the student's academic program and future career aspirations.

title: Admin Aspects of Exercise/Sport Science
description: This course closely examines standards and  guidelines underlying operation of a fitness or  sport facility. Topics may include risk management; policy development; planning,  organizing and evaluating facilities; and  marketing, budgeting and public relations in facilities.

title: Business Spanish
description: Familiarizes students with the professional vocabulary and cultural nuances necessary to successfully communicate in Spanish in the business world. Prerequisite: SPN 209 for SPN 227 . CD

title: Adv Spanish Grammar & Composition
description: Uses short texts in a variety of modes (expository,narrative, descriptive, persuasive, etc.)as models for weekly compositions, including personal essays, journals and letters, creative compositions, business correspondence, etc. There will also be intensive practice in translation from English to Spanish. This course aims for correctness of expression, vocabulary acquisition and some basic stylistic sophistication. Prerequisite: SPN-209 . WI

title: Medical Spanish II
description: Familiarizes students with the professional vocabulary and cultural nuances necessary to successfully communicate in Spanish in the medical field. Prerequisite: SPN 209 . CD

title: Advanced Spanish Conversation
description: Intensive practice in conversational Spanish with a special emphasis on the phonetic aspects of the language, including pronunciation, intonation, rhythm, and phraseology. Prerequisites: SPN 209 CD

title: Medical Spanish I: Patient Communication
description: This is a third-semester intermediate course for future healthcare professionals where students will learn to communicate with Spanish-speaking patients. Covers the vocabulary, grammar, and cultural topics necessary to provide culturally competent health care.  Emphasizes oral communication of healthcare providers with Spanish-speaking patients.  Prerequisite: SPN-102 or placement.

title: Global Health Service Learning Abroad
description: A globalA global health service-learning trip to a country in Latin America (Mexico, Central America, South America or the Caribbean), where students will assist local populations through relevant service work. We will study global health inequalities and efforts to resolve them in the context of our own work in the same. Includes background orientation to our destination country, characteristics of the patient population we will serve, and study of health-care delivery across linguistic and cultural barriers. Prerequisite: Foundations in English course, Permission of the Instructor, and Approved Study Abroad Application. NWC, CD.

title: Intermediate Spanish Conversation
description: Development of listening and speaking competencies through extensive use of multimedia (text, audio, and visual materials). Special attention is given to spoken Spanish with a concentration on pronunciation and fluency in order to achieve intermediate-level linguistic proficiency. Three hours lecture, one hour language lab ( SPN-209LL ). (3 semester hours) Prerequisite: SPN 205 , Corequisite: SPN-209LL .

title: Language Lab for Spn-205
description: Language Lab for SPN-205 : Intermediate Spanish.  Corequisite: SPN-205 .

title: Language Lab for SPN-209
description: Language Lab for SPN-209 : Intermediate Spanish Conversation.  Corequisite: SPN-209 .

title: Hispanic Film Studies
description: Designed to familiarize students with Hispanic  cultures through the lens of film. Students will  study cultural themes of major importance to the  Hispanic world, as seen in both Latin American  and Spanish cinema, as well as become familiar  with both major and minor directors and film  movements in the Spanish-speaking world. NWC, ART

title: Service Learning in Spanish
description: Focuses on linguistic fluency, while at the same time centers on service to the Hispanic community in Chambersburg (i.e., ESL tutoring, migrant student mentorship, interpretation services). Prerequisite: SPN 240 or SPN 242 . CD

title: Coloniality in Hispanic World
description: Focuses on the various forms of colonialism perpetrated and perpetuated in the Hispanic world (Spain and the Americas) through analysis of theoretical formulation and cultural constructions, such as literature, art, philosophy, politics and economics.  Prerequisite: SPN 240 or SPN 242 . HWC, NWC

title: Hispanic Women Writers
description: Focuses theoretically and practically on how women writers were able to open up spaces for their own artistic creation within the traditional, patriarchal boundaries erected by masculine authority/authorship in the Hispanic world. Prerequisite: SPN 240 or SPN 242 . WS, LIT, WI

title: Medical Spanish Internship
description: A brief internship experience that allows students  to make use of Spanish and cross-cultural  communication skills in context, within a  professional medical placement.  Prerequisites:  Permission

title: Global Health Srvce Learnh Abroad--Span
description: A global health service-learning trip to a country in Latin America (Mexico, Central America, South America or the Caribbean), where students will assist local populations through medical interpretation and other relevant service work. We will develop basic skills of medical interpretation, and study cross-cultural, health-care communication in the context of the intersection between Western and non-Western cultures, whether they be of indigenous American or African descent. Prerequisite:  Permission of Instructor and approved study abroad application.

title: Coloniality in Hispanic World
description: Focuses on the various forms of colonialism perpetrated and perpetuated in the Hispanic world (Spain and the Americas) through analysis of theoretical formulation and cultural constructions, such as literature, art, philosophy, politics and economics.  Prerequisite: English Foundations course and 200-level course in any discipline. HWC, NWC

title: Hispanic Film Studies in Translation
description: Designed to familiarize students with Hispanic  cultures through the lens of film. Students will  study cultural themes of major importance to the  Hispanic world, as seen in both Latin American  and Spanish cinema, as well as become familiar  with both major and minor directors and film  movements in the Spanish-speaking world. Prerequisite:  Foundations in English course and one 200-level course in any discipline. NWC, ART.

title: Hispanic Women Writers
description: Focuses theoretically and practically on how women writers were able to open up spaces for their own artistic creation within the traditional, patriarchal boundaries erected by masculine authority/authorship in the Hispanic world. Prerequisite: English Foundations course and 200-level course in any discipline. WS,  LIT, WI

title: Business Spanish
description: Familiarizes students with the professional vocabulary and cultural nuances necessary to successfully communicate in Spanish in the business world. Prerequisite: SPN 240 or SPN 242 for SPN 327 . CD

title: Spanish Translation
description: Focuses on English-Spanish and Spanish-English  translation of a variety of types of texts, thus  refining students' linguistic proficiency and  cultivating understanding of the cultural nuances  of translation.  Pre-requisite: SPN 240 or SPN  242. CD

title: Medical Spanish Mission Trip
description: A medical mission trip to a region in Latin America (Mexico, Central America, South America or the Caribbean), where students will assist local populations through medical interpretation and other relevant service work. In these interactions,emphasis will also be placed on cross-cultural communication in the context of the intersection between Western and non-Western cultures, whether they be of indigenous American or African descent. Permission of Instructor.

title: Cervantes' Don Quijote de la Mancha
description: Course in translation. Close reading and discussion of Don Quijote.  Background lectures will depict the contexts  (historical, social, cultural) in which Cervantes  wrote his masterpiece. Additional readings will  acquaint students with some of the most important  criticism relating to Don Quijote, particularly  its role as perhaps the first metafictional  modern novel. Prerequisite: Foundations in English Course AND one 200-level course in any discipline.

title: Hispanic Film Studies in Translation
description: Designed to familiarize students with Hispanic  cultures through the lens of film. Students will  study cultural themes of major importance to the  Hispanic world, as seen in both Latin American  and Spanish cinema, as well as become familiar  with both major and minor directors and film  movements in the Spanish-speaking world.  Permission

description: Focuses on linguistic fluency, while at the same time centers on service to the Hispanic community in Chambersburg (i.e., ESL tutoring, migrant student mentorship, interpretation services). Prerequisite: permission of instructor.

description: Focuses theoretically and practically on how women writers were able to open up spaces for their own artistic creation within the traditional, patriarchal boundaries erected by masculine authority/authorship in the Hispanic world. Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.

title: Introduction to Veterinary Nursing
description: Overview of the field of veterinary medicine, including ethical jurisprudence and medical terminology. Topics include: reproduction, nutrition, and management of dogs, cats, horses and livestock species. Kennel assignments and animal care outside of class are mandatory. Three hours lecture. (3 sem. hrs)

title: Parasitology
description: Study of various life cycles of animal parasites as they apply to an understanding of parasitic  control clinically. Prerequisite: BIO 101 or BIO  110.

title: Infectious Diseases and Diagnostics
description: A study of infectious animal diseases with emphasis on infectious disease prevention, control, diagnostics, and zoonosis and the life-cycles of commonly-occurring parasites of domestic animals.  Three hours lecture; two hours laboratory. Prerequisite: VMT 115 and BIO 101 OR 110. (4 sem. hrs.)

title: Coloniality in Hispanic World
description: Focuses on the various forms of colonialism perpetrated and perpetuated in the Hispanic world (Spain and the Americas) through analysis of theoretical formulation and cultural constructions, such as literature, art, philosophy, politics and economics.  Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
